lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. RNA secondary structure is
also one of the cis determinants that shape splicing timing
(post-transcriptional-splicing) alongside GC content, intron length and
splice-site strength choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
G-quadruplexes (rG4s)
A prominent structural regulator is the g-quadruplex — a noncanonical
four-stranded fold in guanine-rich RNA. rG4s near 5′/3′ splice sites act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all alternative-splicing
event types, by recruiting or blocking factors and by masking/exposing splice
sites; ~60% of transcripts contain at least one putative quadruplex sequence and
splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes. G4-reader proteins include the
hnRNP family, FMRP and SRSF1; DNA G4s can also act indirectly via Pol II
elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Disease and therapeutic angle
Structural mis-regulation is tied to cancer and neurological disease (e.g. TP53,
BCL-x, hTERT; FMR1/Fragile X), and G4-stabilising ligands are an emerging way to
modulate splicing — a structural complement to sequence-targeted ASOs
(splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — primary: rG4s as structural AS regulators, readers, disease, ligands
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — RNA–RNA base pairing (DSCAM); structured transcriptome
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — structure as part of the splicing code
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — RNA structure as a cis determinant of splicing timing

wiki/concepts/rna-structure-prediction.title: rna-structure-prediction
wiki/concepts/rna-structure-prediction.content:
RNA Structure Prediction
Computational prediction of RNA 3D (tertiary) structure — and increasingly
conformational ensembles — from sequence. Harder than protein folding because
RNA is highly flexible and experimental 3D structures are scarce (orders of
magnitude fewer than proteins in the PDB).
In this wiki

trrosettarna2 tackles the data-scarcity problem by pre-training on abundant
secondary-structure annotations (bpRNA) and injecting that base-pairing prior
into 3D attention, reaching AlphaFold-3-competitive accuracy at ~1/12 the
parameters and topping CASP16’s automated servers
(wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure).
Varying the secondary-structure input lets the model sample distinct conformers
without experimental data — a step beyond single-structure prediction.

Connections

A sibling of the sequence-prior idea in genomics models like spliceai within
ai-in-genomics; complements the splicing topic’s rna-structure-and-splicing
(which concerns RNA structure’s regulatory role rather than its prediction).

Sources

wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure

wiki/concepts/single-cell-foundation-mod.title: single-cell-foundation-models
wiki/concepts/single-cell-foundation-mod.content:
Single-cell Foundation Models
Pretrained models (scGPT, SCimilarity, scVI, scTab, scFoundation) that embed
single-cell RNA-seq profiles into a biologically meaningful latent space,
transferable across datasets and tasks (cell-type annotation, integration,
querying). A domain-specific branch of foundation-models applied to
ai-in-genomics.
Why they matter here

They are the substrate that signature-tool interprets: gradient
attributions over their embeddings score per-gene importance, surfacing shared
disease programs (gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting, see
model-interpretability).
Their value is uneven — attribution and downstream benefit are strong for
well-represented cell types but weak for poorly-represented ones, a
data-quality-and-bias issue.
Heavyweight FMs are also a baseline to beat: lighter task-specific models like
cmonge match them with far fewer parameters in
single-cell-perturbation-modeling.

Sources

gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting

wiki/concepts/single-cell-perturbation-m.title: single-cell-perturbation-modeling
wiki/concepts/single-cell-perturbation-m.content:
Single-cell Perturbation Modeling
Predicting how individual cells shift state in response to genetic or chemical
perturbations (gene knockouts/activations, drugs, doses, combinations) from
single-cell readouts (scRNA-seq, protein imaging). The central challenge is
generalization to unseen conditions — new drugs, new gene pairs, or new cell
types — since exhaustively measuring the perturbation space is infeasible.
Approaches in this wiki

Optimal transport — cmonge learns a conditional transport map between
control and perturbed cell distributions, generalizing to unseen drugs from
structure (driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable, see
optimal-transport).
Graph priors — txpert injects gene–gene knowledge-graphs into a GNN
to predict transcriptomic responses, including cross-cell-line zero-shot
(wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge).
Interpretation — signature-tool doesn’t predict perturbations but scores
gene importance from single-cell-foundation-models
(gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting).

Open issues

A recurring “reality check”: several methods fail to beat simple additive/mean
baselines, and cross-context (new cell type) generalization remains weak.
Lightweight task-specific models (CMonge) can rival or beat heavyweight single-cell
foundation-models on parameter efficiency.

Sources

driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable · wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge

wiki/concepts/single-cell-proteomics.title: single-cell-proteomics
wiki/concepts/single-cell-proteomics.content:
Single-cell Proteomics
Measuring protein abundance proteome-wide in individual cells, typically by
label-free mass spectrometry (one MS run per cell). Because mRNA is an imperfect
proxy for protein, single-cell proteomics directly localizes the functional,
disease-relevant layer that transcriptomics can miss.
In this wiki

wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of pushes the method to very small
primary human cells (~7–10 μm prenatal neurons, ~5–50 pg protein), achieving ~800
proteins/cell — the anchor atlas of functional-single-cell-genomics.
Its central finding is pervasive, cell-type-specific transcriptome–proteome
discordance (even in excitatory neurons, mRNA–protein ρ≈0.36), concentrated in
neurodevelopmental-disorder genes.

Connections

A protein-level complement to single-cell/single-nucleus RNA-seq atlases; methods
like ORBIT adjust MS intensities for protein detectability.

Sources

wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of

wiki/concepts/splice-sites.title: splice-sites
wiki/concepts/splice-sites.content:
Splice sites
Splice sites are the conserved sequence motifs at exon–intron boundaries that the spliceosome recognizes to define what is removed (intron) versus retained (exon) during splicing editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. Each intron is delimited by four core signals: the 5’ splice site (at the exon–intron junction), the branch point (BP), the polypyrimidine tract (PPT), and the 3’ splice site rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Recognition
These signals are recognized through base pairing of snRNPs and splicing-factors: U1 snRNA base-pairs with the 5’ splice site, and its complementarity defines 5’ss “strength”; the 3’ end of the intron is recognized by SF1 (branch point), U2AF2/U2AF1 (PPT and 3’ss), and the U2 snRNP (including SF3B1/PHF5A) at the branch point rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Some positions are near-invariant: a G at the +1 position of the 5’ss is required for catalysis, and a pyrimidine at +2 is present in 99.6% of 5’ splice sites rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Degeneracy enables alternative splicing
In higher eukaryotes splice sites are remarkably diverse and often resemble non-splice-site sequences, so reliable recognition depends on sequence context plus auxiliary splicing-regulatory-elements rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. This weak, degenerate metazoan consensus is what makes splice-site choice tunable, enabling alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Internal exons are constrained to ~50–250 nt by exon definition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Mutations that disrupt splice sites can cause exon skipping, intron retention, or activation of cryptic splice sites, contributing to splicing-and-disease editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — BP/PPT/3’ss recognition by SF1, U2AF, U2; U1 base-pairing and 5’ss strength; +1 G, +2 pyrimidine; degeneracy
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — weak metazoan splice sites underpinning alternative splicing
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — conserved boundary motifs; variant effects (skipping, retention, cryptic sites)

wiki/concepts/spliceosome.title: spliceosome
wiki/concepts/spliceosome.content:
Spliceosome
The spliceosome is the dynamic ribonucleoprotein (RNP) machine that carries out splicing, removing introns via a two-step transesterification reaction editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. In humans it is roughly ~4.8 MDa and is built from five small nuclear RNP particles (snRNPs) — U1, U2, U4/U6 and U5 — together with numerous proteins: more than 200 in metazoans (more than 150 additional proteins beyond the snRNAs) versus ~100 in yeast lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Assembly cycle
The spliceosome is not preassembled; it assembles stepwise on every intron and then recycles, passing through ordered states E → A → B → Bact/B* → C lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Its composition is highly dynamic: the B → Bact transition loses the U1 and U4 snRNAs and ~35 proteins while adding 12, and Bact → C loses 2 proteins and adds 9 lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Cryo-EM has now resolved many of these conformational states rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
RNA-based catalysis
The spliceosome is a ribozyme: its catalytic center is RNA — the base-paired U2–U6 snRNA complex — and metal-ion-rescue experiments in yeast U6 snRNA show that RNA, not the prp8 protein, coordinates the catalytic divalent metals lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This makes it structurally and mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns, supporting an evolutionary origin from them (splicing-and-evolution) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Key proteins
prp8 sits at the active-site core and contains RNase H- and reverse-transcriptase-like domains; SF3b (including SF3B1) participates in branch-point recognition by U2 and is a drug target lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. The spliceosome works with splice-sites and splicing-factors.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — ~4.8 MDa size, snRNP composition, E→C assembly cycle, ribozyme/group II analogy, prp8
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — five snRNPs + >150 proteins; cryo-EM snapshots; SF3B1
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — dynamic RNP, two-step transesterification, resolved conformational states

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-chromatin.title: splicing-and-chromatin
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-chromatin.content:
Splicing and Chromatin
Chromatin state is coupled to splicing through histone marks, adapter proteins, nucleosome positioning and RNA polymerase II elongation speed, which together influence cotranscriptional-splicing outcomes and the retention of pre-mRNA on chromatin lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Histone marks, adapters, and nucleosomes
Histone modifications recruit splicing machinery and tune elongation. “Adapter” proteins physically bridge chromatin to the spliceosome and splicing-factors: CHD1 links chromatin to the U2 snRNP, and MRG15 (an H3K36me3 reader) recruits the repressor PTB to regulate alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Marks such as H3K9ac and H3K9me2 modulate Pol II elongation and thus splicing outcomes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. A documented feedback loop: G9a (EHMT2) exon-10 inclusion creates an H3K9me2-driven positive loop reinforcing neuronal differentiation marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Pol II speed and chromatin retention
Pol II elongation (~3–4 kb/min) sets the kinetic “window” for splice-site recognition; nucleosomes act as elongation barriers, and slow-polymerase variants shift splicing patterns lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Beyond synthesis, polyadenylated pre-mRNA can remain chromatin-associated long after transcription ends, where splicing completion gates release — making chromatin retention central to post-transcriptional-splicing choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Nuclear compartmentalization
Subnuclear localization correlates with splicing timing: ~31% of nuclear-speckle-associated retained introns overlap annotated detained introns, versus 12% for lamina-associated ones — implicating nuclear speckles and other compartments in coupling transcript position to splicing completion choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of. How speckles mechanistically link localization to timing remains an open question choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — adapter proteins (CHD1–U2, MRG15–PTB); Pol II speed; histone-mark coupling.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — H3K9ac/H3K9me2 and elongation; G9a/H3K9me2 neuronal feedback loop.
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — chromatin retention of polyadenylated pre-mRNA; nuclear speckles vs lamina.

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-disease.title: splicing-and-disease
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-disease.content:
Splicing and Disease
Errors in splicing are a major source of human pathology, arising both from mutations in the pre-mRNA itself and from mutations in the splicing-factors that interpret it.
Cis-acting mutations
Mutations in consensus splice-sites account for roughly 10% of human inherited disease wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. Mutations in splicing-regulatory-elements (enhancers/silencers) also cause disease — examples include β-globin thalassemia lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Because the splicing-code is context-dependent, many disease variants act by disrupting exon definition: ~90% of BRCA2 5′ss mutations in breast cancer affect splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Single-gene disorders

Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA): loss of SMN1; a C>T difference makes the paralog SMN2 skip exon 7, yielding insufficient functional protein marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD): dystrophin exon deletions disrupt the reading frame marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Familial dysautonomia: an IKBKAP intron-20 +6 mutation causes exon skipping rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.

Cancer
Recurrent somatic mutations in core splicing-factors drive myeloid malignancies and others: SF3B1, U2AF1, SRSF2 and ZRSR2 in myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and CLL lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. SF3B1 mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts, 15–36% of eye/uveal melanomas, and are among the most common single mutations in CLL; mutant SF3B1 activates cryptic 3′ss and represses the tumour suppressor BRD9 via a poison exon rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. SRSF1 acts as a proto-oncogene marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Neurodegeneration
Prion-like-domain mutations in rna-binding-proteins hnRNPA1, TDP-43 and FUS cause ALS lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. These disorders motivate splicing-therapeutics.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — splicing-factor mutations in cancer; BRCA2; familial dysautonomia.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — SMA, DMD, oncogenic SRSF1.
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — ~10% of inherited disease from splice-site mutations.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — thalassemia, ALS, myeloid splicing-factor mutations.

wiki/concepts/splicing-and-evolution.title: splicing-and-evolution
wiki/concepts/splicing-and-evolution.content:
Splicing and Evolution
alternative-splicing (AS) is an evolutionarily labile process that can fuel adaptation, speciation and organismal complexity wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Scaling with complexity
AS prevalence correlates with organismal complexity: pre-RNA-seq Ensembl estimates rise from C. elegans (25%) and Drosophila (45%) to mouse (63%) and human (88%) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. AS is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular eukaryotes and prevails in K-selected lineages, offering a readily available pool of mRNA variability co-opted without making new genes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Drift versus selection; cis versus trans
Most AS events are probably noise and non-conserved, but a functional subset diversifies the proteome wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. AS profiles diverge faster than gene expression — vertebrate organ transcriptomes track species identity more than organ type marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Novel splice patterns arise via cis-effects (splice-sites/splicing-regulatory-elements changes, exonization, intronization) and trans-effects (mutations in splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins); cis effects appear more common in interspecies divergence wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Concrete adaptations

Stickleback MSX2A: an exon-1 mutation creates an ISE that activates an alternative 5′ss → truncated protein → shorter spines in freshwater fish wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.
Vampire bat TRPV1: trigeminal-specific exon-14a inclusion yields a short isoform sensing ~30 °C for infrared prey detection wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Cavefish (Astyanax) per2 exon-21 skipping → truncated Per2 enhancing lipogenesis, convergent across species wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source.

Origin of the machinery
The spliceosome is a ribozyme whose RNA catalytic core is mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns, from which it is thought to have evolved lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. AS also drives splicing-and-disease and links to splicing-therapeutics.
Sources

wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — drift vs selection, cis/trans divergence, stickleback/bat/cavefish adaptations.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — adaptive value of AS, species-tracking transcriptomes.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — AS scaling with complexity; group II intron origin of the spliceosome.
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — joint evolution of splicing and protein codes.

wiki/concepts/splicing-code.title: splicing-code
wiki/concepts/splicing-code.content:
Splicing Code
The splicing code is the idea that splicing outcomes are written in genomic sequence and can be predicted from it. The “ideal” model would predict the full set of transcript isoforms and their frequencies from sequence alone in any cellular context, while approximating the underlying biology read by the spliceosome and splicing-factors capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. The code combines four core motifs at splice-sites with position-dependent splicing-regulatory-elements interpreted by rna-binding-proteins capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-thelee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Model lineage. Capitanchik traces ~30 years of evolution capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the:

Core-motif models scoring splice-sites (PWMs, MaxEntScan).
Integrative feature models using ~1,000+ predefined features (Splicing Code, SPANR).
End-to-end deep learning that learns features de novo (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice). SpliceAI is a 32-layer residual CNN; Pangolin shares the architecture but trains across species and tissues, adding tissue-specific splice-site strength.
Whole-locus / omics models (Borzoi, BigRNA) taking up to ~0.5 Mb input and predicting tissue-specific RNA-seq plus other functional genomics.
Self-supervised sequence LLMs (SpliceBERT, Nucleotide Transformer).

Context window matters: extending SpliceAI’s input from 80→400 nt raised top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95% capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Clinical variant interpretation. SpliceAI and Pangolin predict non-coding cryptic-splicing variants — roughly 10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations — informing ClinGen/ACMG classification, consistent with the long-known ~10% of inherited disease caused by consensus-site mutations capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-thewright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source. This grounds splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics applications.
Open problems. Black-box accuracy outpaces mechanistic interpretability; tissue-specific prediction is weak (median Spearman ~0.22–0.43), limited by scarce training data; and predicting full-length isoforms — not just per-site usage — remains unsolved, awaiting long-read and single-cell data capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Sources

capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — full model lineage, accuracy data, clinical use, open problems
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — position-dependent regulatory-element basis of the code
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — ~10% of inherited disease from splice-site mutations

wiki/concepts/splicing-factors.title: splicing-factors
wiki/concepts/splicing-factors.content:
Splicing Factors
Splicing factors are the trans-acting proteins that bind splicing-regulatory-elements and steer the spliceosome’s choice of splice-sites, thereby setting up alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are a subset of rna-binding-proteins dedicated to splicing; this page focuses on their splicing roles, while broader RBP biology is on the rna-binding-proteins page.
The two classic families are antagonistic: SR proteins generally act as activators that promote exon inclusion, whereas hnRNPs generally act as repressors lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This activator/repressor split is not absolute — the effect of a given factor is position-dependent relative to the regulated exon, the basis of the splicing-code (e.g. the Nova RNA map) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Many splicing factors are tissue-specific: Nova, PTB (PTBP1), Rbfox, and Muscleblind (MBNL)/CELF tune splicing in particular cell types lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. SRRM4 coordinates neural microexon recognition and is reduced in autism rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative; MBNL1/MBNL2 control the FOXP1 differentiation/pluripotency switch, and hnRNPA1/A2 and PTBP1 govern the PKM Warburg-effect switch marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Activity is also regulated post-translationally and via cross/auto-regulation: CLK1 phosphorylates SR proteins as a body-temperature sensor marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and mutations in core factors (SF3B1, U2AF1/2) drive splicing-and-disease, notably myeloid cancers rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SR-vs-hnRNP activator/repressor split, position-dependence, tissue-specific Nova/PTB/Rbfox/MBNL/CELF
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — splicing code, SRRM4 microexons, core-factor disease mutations
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — CLK1/SR phosphorylation, MBNL/FOXP1 and hnRNP/PTBP1 gene-specific switches

wiki/concepts/splicing-methods.title: splicing-methods
wiki/concepts/splicing-methods.content:
Splicing Methods
The experimental and computational toolkit for studying alternative-splicing
— how isoforms are mapped, how their function is tested, and how their genetic
control is decoded. For decades technical barriers limited the field; recent
advances (long-read sequencing, CRISPR functional assays, population genetics,
deep learning) are now transforming it
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Mapping isoforms (sequencing)

Short-read RNA-seq quantifies splicing either isoform-based or event-based
(exon/junction), summarised as  ΔPSI; different
tools (e.g. rmats and other event callers, MAJIQ, LeafCutter) can disagree,
so orthogonal validation is needed
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying. The reference event-based
caller rmats models per-replicate uncertainty and tests ΔPSI above a
user-set magnitude (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).
Long-read sequencing (PacBio HiFi ~99.8% / 10–25 kb; Oxford Nanopore cDNA
10–20 kb) resolves full-length exon connectivity; ONT direct RNA-seq reads
native RNA and modifications (m6A) sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Single-cell full-length (Smart-seq2/3, VASA-seq) and long-read single-cell
(ScISOr-Seq2, MAS-ISO-seq, snISOr-Seq) and spatial methods (MERFISH,
Slide-seq, Stereo-seq, Spl-ISO-Seq) extend isoform views to cell-type and
tissue resolution sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Testing function

CRISPR is the new functional layer: Cas9 editing/fitness screens, base
editors targeting splice-sites, and RNA-targeting Cas13/dCasRx for
isoform-specific perturbation sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Minigene / reporter and MPRA assays (millions of synthetic sequences)
dissect splicing-regulatory-elements and variant effects
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Decoding genetic control

Population genetics — splicing QTLs (sQTL), allele-specific expression,
and atlases (GTEx, ENCODE) map how variation shapes splicing and
disease risk; MPRAs found ~10% of exonic disease
mutations disrupt splicing vs ~3% of common SNPs
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Deep-learning splicing-code models (spliceai, Pangolin, DeltaSplice,
Borzoi, AlphaGenome) predict outcomes from sequence but do not yet capture
cell-type-specific isoform repertoires
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying,
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Why it matters here
These tools are exactly what the alternative-splicing-functionality-debate
identified as the way to settle whether isoforms are functional or noise —
long-read sequencing, single-cell, and CRISPR perturbation make isoforms
directly measurable and testable sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Sources

sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — primary: the full
experimental + computational toolkit, with metrics and caveats
shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection — the rMATS methods paper:
the canonical replicate-aware event-based ΔPSI caller,
plus RNA-seq study-design guidance
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — the deep-learning
splicing-code modelling lineage

wiki/concepts/splicing-qtl.title: splicing-qtl
wiki/concepts/splicing-qtl.content:
Splicing QTL (sQTL)
A splicing quantitative trait locus is a genetic variant whose genotype
associates with the splicing level (PSI) of a nearby
event. sQTLs are how population-scale genetics connects DNA variation to
alternative-splicing and, through it, to disease — many GWAS signals act by
altering splicing rather than expression.
In this wiki

The landscape. Population RNA-seq (GTEx, Geuvadis) reveals widespread
splicing variation across human populations, frequently colocalizing with GWAS
traits (park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative).
Regional structure. In human brain, sQTLs split into regionally ubiquitous
(splice-site SNPs) vs region-specific (exonic/intronic) classes; regional
variation plus RBP expression helps fine-map causal variants — e.g. an RBFOX2
site at MAPT (zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls).
Allele-specific resolution. Paired/allele-specific tests (PAIRADISE,
demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing) gain power
for rare-variant splicing effects by comparing alleles within individuals.
Scalable mapping. Annotation-free intron-excision quantification
(LeafCutter, li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna)
finds 1.4–2.1× more sQTLs and links them to disease genes.

Connections

The genetics-facing counterpart of the splicing-code (sequence→splicing
prediction) and splicing-and-disease; built on event quantification
(percent-spliced-in, rmats, splicing-methods).

wiki/concepts/splicing-regulatory-elemen.title: splicing-regulatory-elements
wiki/concepts/splicing-regulatory-elemen.content:
Splicing Regulatory Elements
Splicing regulatory elements are short cis-acting RNA sequences in pre-mRNA that tune how the spliceosome recognizes splice-sites and so shape alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are classified by location and effect into four types: exonic splicing enhancers (ESEs), exonic splicing silencers (ESSs), intronic splicing enhancers (ISEs), and intronic splicing silencers (ISSs) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
These elements work by recruiting trans-acting splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins: enhancers typically bind SR proteins that promote exon definition and splice-site usage, while silencers typically bind hnRNPs (and related proteins such as RBM and CELF) that repress recognition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Crucially, the effect of an element is position-dependent: the same motif can act positively or negatively depending on where it sits relative to a splice site, a principle central to the splicing-code rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Splice-site choice thus depends jointly on cis-elements, trans factors, and coupling to transcription (cotranscriptional-splicing) marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Because they govern exon inclusion, these elements are common disease targets: two-thirds of all possible mutations in an alternative exon affect inclusion rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and mutations in silencers/enhancers cause disease lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. They are also therapeutic handles (splicing-therapeutics): nusinersen blocks an intron-7 ISS to boost SMN2 exon 7 inclusion, and eteplirsen targets an ESE to skip a dystrophin exon marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — defines exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers and their position-dependent, protein-recruiting role
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS classification, recruited RBP families, splicing code, mutation/therapeutic relevance
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — joint cis/trans/transcription control; ISS- and ESE-targeting drugs

wiki/concepts/splicing-therapeutics.title: splicing-therapeutics
wiki/concepts/splicing-therapeutics.content:
Splicing Therapeutics
Understanding the splicing-code has made splicing a druggable process, turning splicing-and-disease into a target for several now-clinical interventions.
Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs)
ASOs base-pair with the pre-mRNA to redirect splice-sites selection. Nusinersen (FDA-approved 2016) blocks an intronic splicing silencer bound by hnRNPA1/A2 to boost SMN2 exon-7 inclusion in SMA, delivered by intrathecal injection rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Eteplirsen targets an exonic splicing enhancer to skip a dystrophin exon in DMD, restoring an in-frame, internally truncated (Becker-like) protein rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Small molecules
Risdiplam (FDA-approved 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone, “repairs” the U1–SMN2 exon-7 interaction by aiding U1C zinc-finger contact rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. In oncology, SF3B1 inhibitors (pladienolide B, H3B-8800 in trials, spliceostatin A, sudemycins) block the open→closed branch-point-recognition switch of the spliceosome rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Indisulam degrades the splicing-factors component RBM39, exploiting synthetic lethality rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Immunotherapy and engineered machinery
Splicing modulators generate tumour neoantigens, combinable with anti-PD1 checkpoint blockade rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Engineered U1 snRNAs can be redesigned to recognize mutant splice sites (proposed for familial dysautonomia) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing is an emerging route to correct splice defects directly lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Role of the splicing code
Rational design depends on predicting how a sequence change alters splicing — splicing-regulatory-elements recognized by rna-binding-proteins — and on computational splicing-code models (SpliceAI, MMSplice, Pangolin) trained by saturation mutagenesis rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — risdiplam, SF3B1/RBM39 drugs, neoantigens, U1 snRNAs, splicing-code models.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — nusinersen and eteplirsen mechanisms.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — spliceostatin/SF3b, ASOs, CRISPR.

wiki/concepts/splicing.title: splicing
wiki/concepts/splicing.content:
Splicing
Splicing is the process that removes introns from a pre-mRNA and ligates the flanking exons together to produce mature mRNA, an essential step for accurate translation of genetic information editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. Most eukaryotic genes are organized as a “mosaic” of introns (non-coding, removed) and exons (retained), a “split gene” architecture discovered in 1977 by Richard Roberts and Phillip Sharp and recognized with the 1993 Nobel Prize editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. The boundaries between introns and exons are defined by conserved splice-sites.
Chemistry
Splicing is carried out by the spliceosome through a two-step transesterification reaction editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. In the first step, the branch-point adenosine attacks the 5’ splice site; in the second step, the freed 5’ exon attacks the 3’ splice site, joining the two exons and releasing the intron. Because the same boundaries are recognized multiple times during assembly, this provides a built-in principle of accuracy rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Significance
The split-gene organization makes alternative-splicing possible: by regulating which exons are included or excluded, a single gene can yield multiple mRNA isoforms, greatly expanding coding potential and affecting mRNA stability, localization and translation editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer. More than 95% of human multi-intron genes are alternatively spliced editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Splicing can occur cotranscriptional-splicing or post-transcriptional-splicing, and disruptions to it underlie splicing-and-disease.
Sources

editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer — split-gene mosaic, two-step chemistry, mature mRNA, historical discovery
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — intron removal by the spliceosome; alternative splicing prevalence
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — two-step transesterification; multiple-recognition accuracy principle

wiki/concepts/topologically-associating-.title: topologically-associating-domains
wiki/concepts/topologically-associating-.content:
Topologically Associating Domains (TADs)
Topologically associating domains (TADs) are self-interacting chromatin domains at the ~Mb scale (reported as ~150 kb to ~1 Mb in mammals), forming the middle tier of genome-architecture between multi-Mb chromatin-compartments and sub-Mb chromatin-loops che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. They insulate contacts between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, constraining enhancer–promoter interactions within domain boundaries and so shaping gene-expression programs che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. In mammals, TADs arise from the loop-extrusion machinery: cohesin extrudes DNA until blocked by convergent pairs of CTCF-binding sites, producing both loops and domain boundaries che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
TAD-like organization recurs across kingdoms through convergent evolution via distinct machinery — selection acts on architectural function, not on specific molecular components (see 3d-genome-evolution) che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Plants lack CTCF; TAD evidence is weak in Arabidopsis but clearer in rice, sorghum, maize, tomato, foxtail millet, and cotton, with boundaries enriched for highly expressed genes che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Mueller’s freshwater sponge Ephydatia muelleri lacks TADs altogether, and bacteria show TAD-analogous chromosome interaction domains with boundaries at highly expressed genes or NAP sites che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome. Young Alu elements are enriched at human-specific TAD boundaries che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome.
Sources

che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome — defines ~Mb self-interacting domains, their insulating role, and cross-kingdom analogues arising via distinct machinery.

wiki/entities/cmonge.title: cmonge
wiki/entities/cmonge.content:
CMonge
CMonge (Conditional Monge Gap) is a conditional neural optimal-transport
library for single-cell-perturbation-modeling, introduced in
driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable.
What it is

Learns a single global transport map conditioned on arbitrary covariates
(drug, dose, combinations) via the Monge Gap regularizer, instead of training one
model per condition.
Encodes conditions by RDKit structure fingerprints or data-driven mode-of-action
embeddings; combinations handled with permutation-invariant DeepSets pooling.
Open-source (PyPI cmonge); very lightweight (~23K–580K params vs chemCPA’s
~1.37M).

Standing

Matches condition-specific models in-sample and generalizes to unseen drugs
from structure (SciPlex OOS R² up to 0.900), beating chemCPA especially on
distributional metrics.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence method; sibling to txpert and
signature-tool in the single-cell cluster; contrasts with heavyweight
single-cell foundation-models on parameter efficiency.

Sources

driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable — the method paper

wiki/entities/crossdna.title: crossdna
wiki/entities/crossdna.content:
CrossDNA
CrossDNA is a parameter-efficient DNA language model
that explicitly and dynamically models cross-strand (forward/reverse-complement)
interactions, introduced in
yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions.
What it is

Dual-branch model with a rotating forward/RC “cross-view” schedule, a Comba +
sliding-window-attention backbone, a TokenBridge module exchanging
cross-strand context, gated fusion, and EMA self-distillation.
Single-nucleotide tokenization; pretrained on GRCh38 with a masked-LM objective;
variants from 408K to 519M parameters.

Standing

Orientation-robust (forward vs RC scores tightly aligned) without augmentation or
hard equivariance; a 408K variant tops the Genomic Benchmark (88.2%) against
models 2–4 orders of magnitude larger (Evo2 7B, AlphaGenome, NTv2).
Weak at individual-level/eQTL effect sizes (single-reference-genome pretraining).

In this wiki

A genomics foundation-models instance (ai-in-genomics); connects to the
splicing-code / spliceai sequence-to-function lineage (reports splice-site
prediction, with large gains in antisense regions).

Sources

yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions — the method paper

wiki/entities/cufflinks.title: cufflinks
wiki/entities/cufflinks.content:
Cufflinks
Cufflinks is an open-source C++ program that assembles full-length transcripts from paired-end RNA-Seq alignments without relying on prior gene annotations and probabilistically estimates isoform abundances in FPKM. Introduced in trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification.
What it is

Annotation-free comparative transcriptome assembler: reduces assembly to a minimum path cover of an “overlap graph” of compatible fragments, via a constructive proof of Dilworth’s Theorem (solved as a maximum matching in a weighted bipartite reachability graph). Does not require an ORF, so it can recover noncoding isoforms.
Abundance estimation: a generative statistical model in which each fragment’s observation probability is a linear function of transcript abundances; uses the fragment-length distribution to assign paired-end fragments to isoforms. Reports FPKM (extends the unpaired Jiang–Wong model to paired-end). MAP estimates and 95% confidence intervals obtained by Bayesian importance sampling.
Takes spliced alignments (e.g., from TopHat) as input. Ships with companion utilities Cuffcompare (structural comparison of assemblies against annotations) and Cuffdiff (differential expression testing). Distributed under the Boost License; runs on Linux and Mac OS X (version 0.8.0 at publication).
Edges in the reachability graph are weighted by the percent-spliced-in metric to phase distant exons; arbitrates between equally parsimonious assemblies via minimum-cost maximum matching.

In this wiki

A foundational splicing-methods tool for isoform-level RNA-Seq quantification and novel-transcript discovery; conceptual predecessor to later differential-splicing tools like rmats.
Uses the percent-spliced-in metric to weight its assembly graph.

Sources

trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification

wiki/entities/darts.title: darts
wiki/entities/darts.content:
DARTS
DARTS (Deep-learning Augmented RNA-seq analysis of Transcript Splicing) is a computational framework that integrates a deep-learning prediction of differential alternative splicing with empirical RNA-seq evidence via Bayesian inference, enabling reliable splicing analysis even at modest sequencing coverage. Introduced in zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis.
What it is

Two components: the DARTS DNN, a deep neural network predicting differential splicing between two conditions from exon-specific cis sequence features plus trans RBP mRNA-expression levels; and the DARTS BHT, a Bayesian hypothesis test with a spike-and-slab prior.
BHT(flat): uninformative prior, RNA-seq data only (equivalent to a likelihood-ratio test); used to generate high-confidence training labels.
BHT(info): folds the DNN prediction in as an informative prior, with the largest accuracy gains on low-coverage / low-expression events.
Trained on ENCODE RBP-knockdown RNA-seq and Roadmap Epigenomics data; extended to exon skipping, alternative 5’/3’ splice sites, and retained introns.
Open source (Python/R, Keras DNN) at github.com/Xinglab/DARTS, with trained model parameters and predictive features provided.

In this wiki

Built on top of rmats for splicing event detection and read counting, and benchmarked against rMATS, MISO, and SUPPA2.
Quantifies and tests changes in percent-spliced-in (psi / exon-inclusion level).
A learned, data-driven splicing-regulation model contributing to the splicing-code effort; complements sequence-only predictors like spliceai by additionally conditioning on RBP expression.

Sources

zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis

wiki/entities/dexseq.title: dexseq
wiki/entities/dexseq.content:
DEXSeq
DEXSeq is a statistical method and R/Bioconductor package for detecting differential exon usage between experimental conditions in RNA-Seq data, using negative-binomial generalized linear models that account for biological variability. Introduced in anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons.
What it is

Tests, per “counting bin” (exon or part of an exon, obtained by flattening overlapping transcript annotations), whether the fraction of a gene’s reads falling into that bin changes between conditions.
Fits per-gene NB GLMs and detects differential exon usage via a counting-bin x condition interaction term, tested by analysis of deviance (chi-squared likelihood-ratio test of full vs reduced model).
Estimates per-bin dispersion with the Cox-Reid adjusted profile likelihood (as in edgeR / McCarthy et al. 2012) and shares information across genes through a fitted mean-dispersion trend; uses the maximum of per-bin and fitted dispersion for conservative FDR control (Benjamini-Hochberg).
Distributed as an open-source Bioconductor R package plus HTSeq-based Python scripts for annotation flattening and read counting; produces HTML reports, GLM-fit and normalized-count plots, and a variance-stabilizing axis transform.
Methodologically derived from DESeq and edgeR; designed to use biological replicates, unlike contemporaneous Fisher’s-exact / single-sample approaches.

In this wiki

An exon-centric tool under splicing-methods; contrasts with isoform/event-centric tools such as miso and cufflinks, and complements read-counting event detectors like rmats.
Quantifies relative exon usage, conceptually related to percent-spliced-in.

Sources

anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons

wiki/entities/dscam.title: dscam
wiki/entities/dscam.content:
Dscam
What: The Drosophila Down syndrome cell adhesion molecule gene, the canonical extreme exemplar of alternative-splicing, whose mutually exclusive exon clusters can combinatorially generate tens of thousands of isoforms.
Dscam arranges alternative exons into four clusters used in a mutually exclusive manner. lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative reports this can combinatorially generate >36,000 distinct spliced mRNA isoforms — roughly three times the fly’s gene count — with the exon-6 cluster alone containing 48 mutually exclusive exons. marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative states the gene can give rise to up to 38,000 mRNA variants, half (~19,000) encoding distinct extracellular domains. Using long-read RNA sequencing, wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for normal neural circuit development.
Selection within a cluster is dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing: a conserved “docking” site base-pairs with a complementary “selector” site upstream of each exon-6 variant, ensuring exactly one is included (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative). The hnRNP protein hrp36 enforces mutual exclusivity by repressing the other exons. This makes Dscam the best example of cis-acting RNA secondary structure controlling splice-sites choice, a mechanism beyond protein splicing-factors alone.
Why it matters
Each neuron stochastically expresses a unique subset of DSCAM isoforms; identical extracellular domains bind homophilically and trigger repulsion, enabling neuronal self-avoidance (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). It is the showcase for how alternative-splicing expands proteomic and functional diversity far beyond gene number (splicing-and-evolution).
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — >36,000 isoforms, 48 exon-6 exons, docking/selector base pairing, hrp36 repressor.
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — up to 38,000 variants (~19,000 extracellular domains); self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion.
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — long-read RNA-seq found 7,874 complete Dscam1 isoforms.

wiki/entities/dt-lung.title: dt-lung
wiki/entities/dt-lung.content:
DT-Lung
DT-Lung is an ex vivo human lung digital-twin platform
(Sage Lab / University Health Network) introduced in
zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex.
What it is

Hybrid physics-informed + machine-learning models (XGBoost + GRU) trained on the
largest clinical ex vivo lung perfusion (EVLP) dataset (951 human lungs) to
forecast >75 multimodal parameters of an individual lung.
Runs “static” (baseline-only) and “dynamic” (continuously recalibrated) twins;
serves as a per-organ untreated counterfactual control.
Deliverables: code (github.com/Sage-Lab-ai/DT_Lung), models + demo data
(huggingface.co/SageLabUHN), web app (dt-lung.streamlit.app).

Standing

Detected alteplase efficacy (lower PAP, P=0.031) that a conventional control
cohort missed, with ~3× sample-size efficiency.
Single center, disease-free donors, first-3-h forecasts; data not publicly
shareable.

In this wiki

The translational/biomedical digital-twins entry of
functional-single-cell-genomics; a multimodal human-tissue platform.

Sources

zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex — the method paper

wiki/entities/encode-rbp-map.title: encode-rbp-map
wiki/entities/encode-rbp-map.content:
ENCODE RBP map
ENCODE RBP map is the ENCODE Project Phase III integrative binding-and-functional resource for human RNA-binding proteins — 1,223 replicated datasets spanning 356 RBPs in K562 and HepG2 cells. Introduced in van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional.
What it is

Five orthogonal assay types: eCLIP (in vivo binding, 150 RBPs / 223 datasets), RNA Bind-N-Seq (in vitro specificity, 78 RBPs), knockdown RNA-seq (function, 263 RBPs / 472 profiles), immunofluorescence (localization, 274 RBPs), and ChIP–seq (chromatin association, 37+ RBPs).
844,854 eCLIP peaks; 38,555 knockdown-responsive alternative splicing events; per-RBP “RNA splicing maps.”
Data released at the ENCODE Data Coordination Center (encodeproject.org), GEO (GSE107768), and the RBP Image Database (rnabiology.ircm.qc.ca/RBPImage/).

In this wiki

A resource catalogue for rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors, supporting splicing-methods and splicing-map analyses.
Contains data for factor entities including ptbp1, srsf1, hnrnpa1, u2af1.

Sources

van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional

wiki/entities/erange.title: erange
wiki/entities/erange.content:
ERANGE
ERANGE (Enhanced Read Analysis of Gene Expression) is a computational pipeline for analyzing RNA-Seq data that assigns reads to their gene of origin, allocates ambiguous “multireads”, detects splice-crossing reads, and quantifies transcript prevalence in RPKM. Introduced in mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian.
What it is

Assigns uniquely mapped reads to their site of origin and probabilistically allocates multireads (reads matching 2–10 genomic loci) in proportion to unique + splice reads at candidate loci.
Detects splice-crossing reads against a library of known gene-model splices and assigns them to their gene of origin.
Clusters reads that fall outside known gene models into “RNAFAR” candidate exons, which can be appended to neighboring gene models (within a tunable radius parameter) or flagged as candidate novel transcripts.
Calculates normalized transcript prevalence (RPKM) from unique reads, spliced reads, and multireads.
Distributed as Supplementary Software and from the Wold lab (woldlab.caltech.edu/RNA-Seq).

In this wiki

One of the splicing-methods; an early RNA-seq quantification pipeline conceptually upstream of dedicated alternative-splicing tools such as rmats.
Produces RPKM values; related to isoform-level metrics like percent-spliced-in used by later splicing analyses.

Sources

mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian

wiki/entities/exonscan.title: exonscan
wiki/entities/exonscan.content:
ExonScan
ExonScan is a general splicing-simulation algorithm that predicts the splicing pattern of a pre-mRNA by scanning for candidate exons and scoring their splice sites together with nearby exonic and intronic splicing regulatory elements. Introduced in wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts (originally from Wang et al. 2004).
What it is

A three-step simulator: (1) identify exon candidates as pairs of nearby potential 3’ss and 5’ss ~50-250 bp apart; (2) score the splice sites and the splicing enhancers/silencers (ESEs/ESSs etc.) within the candidate and sum them; (3) apply a threshold to make the final inclusion prediction.
Uses log-odds scoring that rewards elements by their statistical enrichment in the relevant location relative to genomic background, naturally giving enhancers positive and silencers negative scores.
Designed for “faithfulness to the in vivo mechanism”: it uses only information accessible to the spliceosome (sequence/SRE content), deliberately excluding reading-frame consistency and cross-species conservation that gene-finders rely on.
Helped clarify the in vivo function of ESSs and serves as an early integration of a “parts list” of cis-elements toward a predictive splicing code.

In this wiki

An early instance of splicing-methods for sequence-to-splicing prediction; conceptual ancestor of modern deep-learning predictors like spliceai.
Operates on the splicing-regulatory-elements / cis-regulatory-elements catalogued toward the splicing-code.

Sources

wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts

wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.title: g-quadruplex
wiki/entities/g-quadruplex.content:
G-quadruplex
What: A noncanonical four-stranded nucleic-acid secondary structure formed in
guanine-rich regions, where four guanines Hoogsteen-bond into a G-quartet and
stacked quartets are stabilised by K⁺; it forms in both DNA (dG4) and RNA (rG4)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Role in splicing
rG4s are an emerging structural layer of alternative-splicing regulation
(rna-structure-and-splicing). Positioned near 5′/3′ splice-sites, they act
position-dependently as enhancers or silencers across all event types — by
recruiting or blocking splicing-factors and by remodelling the pre-mRNA to
mask or expose splice sites. dG4s additionally influence splicing indirectly
via RNA Pol II elongation and R-loops (cotranscriptional-splicing)
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Reader proteins named in the source include the hnRNP family (F, H1/H, L),
FMRP (RGG motif), the AFF family, SRSF1, and RBM25
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.
Significance

Prevalence: ~60% of transcripts carry ≥1 putative quadruplex sequence (~1.2M
PQSs); splice junctions are ~3-fold enriched for G4s on the coding strand.
Disease: linked to cancer (TP53, BCL-x, hTERT, EWS-FLI)
and neurological disease (FMR1/Fragile X, BACE1/Alzheimer).
Therapeutics: G4-stabilising ligands (e.g.
pyridostatin, PhenDC3) can modulate splicing, though effects are sometimes
contradictory and ligand selectivity remains unsolved.
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes

Sources

peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes — definition, mechanism, readers, prevalence, disease, ligands

wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.title: hnrnpa1
wiki/entities/hnrnpa1.content:
hnRNPA1
What: The prototypical hnRNP, a well-studied splicing factor that represses splicing by binding silencer elements.
Human hnRNPA1 is the archetypal repressor among the hnRNP family of rna-binding-proteins, the silencing arm of the splicing-code. It binds exonic and intronic splicing silencers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) to fine-tune alternative-splicing lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. This contrasts with SR activators like srsf1; consistent with the position-dependent rule, hnRNPA1 can in some cases act as an activator, and can even promote U1 snRNP binding during splicing silencing.
Role in splicing
hnRNPA1 has several mechanisms: (a) binding a silencer to block recognition of nearby splice-sites; (b) binding a high-affinity site, then cooperatively spreading to adjacent low-affinity sites; and (c) looping of pre-mRNA via interactions between hnRNPA1 proteins bound at flanking intronic sites, promoting exon exclusion lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. It regulates smn2 and HIV pre-mRNA splicing, and (with hnRNPA2/PTBP1) drives the PKM Warburg switch marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Disease / significance
Cis-acting mutations in the prion-like, glycine-rich C-terminal domain of hnRNPA1 cause amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Such prion-like low-complexity domains are shared with hnRNPA2B1, TDP-43, FUS, EWSR1 and TAF15; TDP-43 and FUS prion-like-domain mutations are likewise linked to ALS (splicing-and-disease) lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — repressor mechanisms (blocking, spreading, looping), SMN2/HIV, ALS prion-like domain
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — PKM/Warburg switch; SMN2 intron-7 ISS

wiki/entities/jcast.title: jcast
wiki/entities/jcast.content:
JCAST
JCAST is an open-source software tool (github.com/ed-lau/jcast) that translates RNA-seq alternative splice-junction pairs into compact, tissue-specific protein sequence databases for mass-spectrometry-based discovery of protein splicing isoforms. Introduced in lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of.
What it is

Takes rMATS-derived AS events (A3SS, A5SS, MXE, SE, RI) plus an Ensembl/GTF annotation and SwissProt canonical sequences as input.
Filters junctions by skipped-junction read count (Gaussian mixture cutoff) and replicate consistency, then performs one-frame in-silico translation (annotated reading frame, or a frame avoiding premature termination codons) to limit database-size inflation and false positives.
Stitches translated junction slices back to full-length canonical sequences through a 10-aa overhang and writes a FASTA database for standard proteomics search engines.
Produces databases 6–17% the size of RefSeq while still recovering noncanonical and undocumented isoforms; applicable to non-human proteomes too.

In this wiki

Consumes output from rmats and operates within the splicing-methods toolset, extending percent-spliced-in / AS quantification from the RNA level to the protein level.
Resource counterpart to RNA-centric splicing quantification tools, focused on the alternative-splicing proteome.

Sources

lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of

wiki/entities/kallisto.title: kallisto
wiki/entities/kallisto.content:
kallisto
kallisto is an RNA-seq transcript quantification program that uses pseudoalignment of reads to a reference transcriptome to estimate transcript abundances about two orders of magnitude faster than alignment-based pipelines while retaining comparable accuracy. Introduced in bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification.
What it is

Pseudoalignment, not base alignment: assigns each read to the subset of transcripts it is compatible with (its equivalence class) without computing exact base-level coordinates.
Index = transcriptome de Bruijn graph (T-DBG): a colored de Bruijn graph of transcriptome k-mers plus a hash table mapping each k-mer to its contig; k-mers are grouped into k-compatibility classes that are intersected per read.
EM over equivalence classes: abundances are estimated by the expectation-maximization algorithm applied to equivalence-class counts (sufficient statistics), making each iteration very fast; output in transcripts per million (TPM).
Fast and light: ~30M paired-end reads in <10 min on a laptop, max ~3.2 Gb RAM; few parameters (k-mer length, mean fragment length).
Bootstrap uncertainty: rapidly resamples equivalence-class counts to quantify inferential variance in abundance estimates (output compressed in HDF5).
Includes sequence-specific (6-mer priming) bias correction; supports allele-specific expression; available at pachterlab.github.io/kallisto/.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool: provides upstream isoform-level abundance estimates that feed alternative-splicing and differential-isoform analyses.
Complements rmats (differential alternative-splicing event detection/quantification) by supplying transcript-level quantification.
Benchmarked against Cufflinks, Sailfish, RSEM, eXpress and EMSAR in its introducing paper, outperforming Cufflinks and Sailfish in accuracy and all methods in speed.

Sources

bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification

wiki/entities/leafcutter.title: leafcutter
wiki/entities/leafcutter.content:
LeafCutter
LeafCutter is an annotation-free suite of methods that quantifies RNA splicing by measuring intron excision from split (junction) reads in short-read RNA-seq, then detects differential splicing and maps splicing QTLs. Introduced in li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna (Li, Knowles & Pritchard, Nature Genetics 2018).
What it is

Intron-centric quantification: pools split reads, groups overlapping introns that share a donor/acceptor splice site into a graph, and takes its connected components as intron clusters of alternatively excised introns; summarizes each intron as a count or within-cluster proportion. No transcript annotation required.
Differential splicing: a Dirichlet-multinomial generalized linear model tests whole-cluster changes in intron usage between two groups via a likelihood-ratio test; the multinomial likelihood normalizes implicitly (no library-size parameters, unlike dexseq).
sQTL mapping: outputs intron-excision proportions consumed by standard QTL mappers (fastQTL, MatrixEQTL); WASP filtering of allele-specific reads is required first.
LeafViz: an interactive R Shiny / ggplot2 browser app for visualizing differential-splicing results, labeling clusters “annotated” vs “cryptic”.
Fast and scalable: an order of magnitude or more faster/leaner than MAJIQ; open-source at github.com/davidaknowles/leafcutter.

In this wiki

A core splicing-methods tool in the percent-spliced-in / differential-splicing-caller family, alongside rmats (benchmarked against; comparable P-value calibration) and the isoform-assembly tool cufflinks (Cufflinks2, outperformed on sQTL discovery).
Distinct from event-based ΔPSI callers (rmats) and exon-count models (dexseq) by working on intron clusters rather than predefined events.

Sources

li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna — the methods paper (intron clustering, Dirichlet-multinomial model, GTEx/GEUVADIS benchmarks, sQTL/GWAS applications, LeafViz)

wiki/entities/majiq-v2.title: majiq-v2
wiki/entities/majiq-v2.content:
MAJIQ v2
MAJIQ v2 is a software suite for detecting, quantifying, and visualizing local splicing variations (LSVs) from large, heterogeneous RNA-seq datasets, extending the original MAJIQ (2016) to scale to thousands of non-replicate samples. Introduced in vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous.
What it is

Quantifies splicing in units of LSVs (splits into/out of a reference exon) in terms of percent-spliced-in (Ψ) and dPSI (ΔΨ) using a Bayesian posterior model, capturing complex (>2 junction), de novo, and intron-retention events.
MAJIQ HET: non-parametric, per-sample differential-splicing test (TNOM, InfoScore, or Mann–Whitney U) for heterogeneous populations, instead of assuming a shared group PSI.
Incremental builder: reads BAM coverage once into reusable SJ coverage files, accelerating subsequent builds (relevant for GTEx/ENCODE-style incremental releases).
Simplifier: prunes lowly-used junctions/introns to reduce splicegraph/LSV complexity.
VOILA Modulizer: organizes overlapping LSVs into single-entry/single-exit alternative splicing modules (ASMs), pattern-matched to 14 basic AS event types (incl. non-classical subtypes).
VOILA v2: cross-platform (macOS/Windows/Linux), server-capable visualization app rendering thousands of samples as violin beeswarm plots with on-the-fly filtering.
The only benchmarked method (vs rMATS, LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet) offering de novo intron-retention detection and quantification; ships with a synthetic benchmark dataset and the validationstools comparison package.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool centered on percent-spliced-in quantification.
Direct alternative/competitor to rmats and other local-event splicing tools.
Used in its source paper to characterize cerebellar splicing regulation by ptbp1 and other splicing factors.

Sources

vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous

wiki/entities/miso.title: miso
wiki/entities/miso.content:
MISO
MISO (mixture-of-isoforms) is a Bayesian statistical model that estimates alternative-exon and isoform expression from single- or paired-end RNA-seq, provides confidence intervals on those estimates, and tests for differential regulation. Introduced in katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna.
What it is

A probabilistic generative model of RNA-seq: isoforms are chosen by length-rescaled abundance and reads sampled uniformly along the mRNA; the posterior over isoform abundances Ψ is recovered by Bayes’ rule.
Produces Ŷ_MISO, an unbiased Ψ estimator that uses splice-junction reads, flanking constitutive-exon reads, and paired-end insert-length distributions.
Exon-centric (two-isoform) inference has an analytic solution; isoform-centric inference uses a hybrid MCMC sampler (Metropolis-Hastings + Gibbs with a logistic-normal proposal on the simplex).
Reports Bayesian confidence intervals for Ψ and detects differential expression via a Bayes factor over ΔΨ (Savage-Dickey density ratio).
Encodes isoforms generically as lists of exon coordinates, so it handles skipped exons, alternative 5’/3’ splice sites, alternative first/last exons, mutually exclusive exons, retained introns, and tandem alternative polyadenylation.
Software released at genes.mit.edu/burgelab/miso.

In this wiki

A method for quantifying percent-spliced-in and isoform abundance; part of splicing-methods.
Conceptually related to rmats for differential alternative-splicing analysis from RNA-seq, with a Bayesian rather than likelihood-ratio framing.

Sources

katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna

wiki/entities/pairadise.title: pairadise
wiki/entities/pairadise.content:
PAIRADISE
PAIRADISE (Paired Replicate Analysis of Allelic Differential Splicing Events) is a hierarchical statistical model and software program for detecting allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) from replicate or population-scale RNA-seq data. Introduced in demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing.
What it is

Treats the two alleles of an individual as a matched pair and multiple individuals heterozygous for the same SNP as replicates, framing ASAS detection as paired-replicate differential alternative splicing.
Uses a binomial distribution to model inclusion/skipping read counts (capturing coverage-dependent percent-spliced-in uncertainty) and a logit-normal distribution for allele-specific inclusion variation across individuals; an additive parameter d_i is the interpretable allelic difference, tested by a likelihood ratio test (chi-square, 1 df) with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.
Inputs: RNA-seq FASTQ and phased-genotype VCF files, plus reference genome, GTF annotation, and an RNA-editing mask. Aligns reads to haplotype-modified personal genomes (via the rPGA procedure, STAR) to reduce reference bias, then detects AS events with rmats.
Freely available as Xinglab GitHub software and a stand-alone Bioconductor R package; supports an equal-allele-variance user option (si1=si2) for more permissive detection.
Distinctive capabilities: aggregates ASAS signal across samples for higher power than per-individual tests, and detects rare-variant effects on splicing that conventional sQTL analysis filters out.

In this wiki

Relation to splicing-methods: a population-genetics method for allele-specific splicing, complementary to sQTL approaches.
Refines the rmats paired model, replacing its correlation-parameter covariance with an interpretable additive difference parameter; also uses rMATS for AS event detection.
Quantifies events with percent-spliced-in.

Sources

demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing

wiki/entities/psi-sigma.title: psi-sigma
wiki/entities/psi-sigma.content:
PSI-Sigma
PSI-Sigma is a splicing-detection method that quantifies Percent Spliced-In (PSI) and differential splicing (DPSI) for individual alternative exons across all isoforms, handling complex multiple-exon-skipping (MES) and intron-retention (IR) events that two-isoform and isoform-resolution tools miss. Introduced in lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method.
What it is

Uses a modified PSI index (PSIΣ, the “Σ” denoting that all AS isoforms contribute to the PSI calculation), based on splice-junction reads of all isoforms between two constitutive exons.
A single alternative exon can carry multiple PSI values, one per isoform it appears in; estimates IR via intronic-read counts at the 1st/25th/50th/75th/99th intron percentiles.
Works on both short-read and nanopore long-read RNA-seq (the only one of the compared tools to support long reads).
Output: event coordinate, gene symbol, exon coordinate, event type, DPSI, p-value, adjusted P-value, and per-sample PSI. Open-source at github.com/wososa/PSI-Sigma; single-threaded.

In this wiki

Quantifies percent-spliced-in; catalogued under splicing-methods.
Benchmarked against and broadly comparable to rmats for single-exon events, but more comprehensive for MES/IR.

Sources

lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method

wiki/entities/ptbp1.title: ptbp1
wiki/entities/ptbp1.content:
PTBP1
What: The polypyrimidine-tract binding protein, an hnRNP-class splicing factor and tissue-specific repressor of splicing.
PTBP1 (PTB/hnRNPI) is a tissue-specific RNA-binding protein that, like other hnRNPs, mainly represses alternative-splicing by binding pyrimidine-rich splicing-regulatory-elements. As with hnrnpa1, its activator-versus-repressor effect is position-dependent, the hallmark of the splicing-code that distinguishes it from SR activators such as srsf1 lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
PTBP1 (with hnRNPA1 and hnRNPA2) controls the PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons, raising the PKM2:PKM1 ratio to enable the Warburg effect in cancer; the genes encoding these regulators are activated by MYC marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. PTBP1 also links splicing to splicing-and-chromatin: the adapter MRG15 binds both PTB and the H3K36me3 histone mark, targeting PTB to transcripts such as FGFR2 exon IIIb lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative.
Disease / significance
PTBP1 is a determinant of splicing timing: it is among the proteins associated with post-transcriptional-splicing, where RNA loops mediated by PTBP1 shape regulation after transcription, complementing cotranscriptional-splicing rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. Across species, a PTBP1 isoform reshapes splicing, tying it to organismal/evolutionary differences marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Its PKM control connects it to cancer metabolism (splicing-and-disease).
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — repressor; MRG15/H3K36me3 chromatin adapter; FGFR2
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — PKM/Warburg switch; cross-species isoform
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — post-transcriptional splicing timing; PTBP1 RNA loops

wiki/entities/rmats-turbo.title: rmats-turbo
wiki/entities/rmats-turbo.content:
rMATS-turbo
rMATS-turbo is the fast, scalable re-implementation of rmats (rMATS v4.0.1 and above) that preserves the original statistical framework and command-line interface while adding a decoupled prep/post workflow, enabling alternative-splicing analysis on RNA-seq datasets with tens of thousands of samples. Documented as a protocol in wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible.
What it is

A two-step engine: a prep step turns each FASTQ/BAM file independently into a weighted splicing-graph file (.rmats; exons = nodes, junctions = weighted edges), and a post step integrates those files across samples to discover and quantify events. Prep can be parallelized/distributed; new samples only require re-running post.
Discovers and quantifies the five canonical event types — skipped exon, alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites, mutually exclusive exons, retained intron — reporting percent-spliced-in (PSI/Ψ) with junction-only (JC) and junction+exon (JCEC) read counts.
Retains the original rmats hierarchical statistical test for two-group differential splicing (with |Δψ| > c thresholding), and adds single-group mode, --statoff/--task stat decoupling, --novelSS unannotated splice-site discovery, --fixed-event-set, and --paired-stats (PAIRADISE).
Organism-agnostic, short-read only; companion rmats2sashimiplot renders events as sashimi plots. Limited to basic (binary) events.

In this wiki

The production successor to rmats and its methods paper shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection; the de facto reference event-caller in splicing-methods built on percent-spliced-in.
Widely used for cancer/EMT and sQTL splicing studies → splicing-and-disease.

Sources

wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible — the Nature Protocols protocol (workflow, features, two worked examples, benchmarks-by-citation)

wiki/entities/rmats.title: rmats
wiki/entities/rmats.content:
rMATS
rMATS (replicate Multivariate Analysis of Transcript Splicing) is the widely
used event-based tool for detecting differential alternative-splicing from
replicate RNA-seq data, from the Yi Xing lab (UCLA). It is the de facto
reference implementation of the ΔPSI event-caller class
named in splicing-methods.
What it is

A hierarchical statistical model + program that calls the five canonical AS
event types — skipped exon, alternative 5′/3′ splice-sites, mutually
exclusive exons, retained intron — and tests each for a between-group change in
exon inclusion level ψ (percent-spliced-in).
Distinctive features (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection):
models both within- and between-replicate variation; supports paired
designs (exon-specific correlation ρ); and tests |Δψ| > c for a user-defined
threshold c rather than just inequality.

Lineage & ecosystem

MATS (Shen et al. 2012) → rMATS (2014, this entity) → later
rMATS-turbo (faster engine, widely used in practice).
Sits alongside other event/exon callers (MAJIQ, LeafCutter, DEXSeq) and
isoform-level methods (Cufflinks); benchmarked above the JSD-based Cufflinks and
DiffSplice in its paper.

Why it’s in this wiki

The empirical, short-read measurement counterpart to the broader toolkit in
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and the prediction line
(spliceai / splicing-code).
Heavily used for cancer/EMT splicing studies → splicing-and-disease.

Sources

shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection — the methods paper (model,
validation, benchmarks, design guidance)
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying — places rMATS-style event
callers within the modern AS toolkit

wiki/entities/sf3b1.title: sf3b1
wiki/entities/sf3b1.content:
SF3B1
What: A core U2 snRNP / SF3b component that recognizes the intron branch point during 3’-end definition by the spliceosome.
SF3B1 is one of the splicing-factors that, together with SF1, the U2AF heterodimer (u2af1/U2AF2) and U2 snRNP, define the 3’ end of introns at splice-sites rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
SF3B1 and PHF5A have a key role in branch-point (BP) recognition. A major rearrangement of SF3B1 from an open to a closed conformation is triggered by recognition of the pre-mRNA, with its HEAT-repeat domain contacting the BP adenosine, which is sandwiched between SF3B1 and PHF5A — a fidelity checkpoint confirming proper BP recognition rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Disease & therapeutics
SF3B1 (also called SAP155) is recurrently mutated in myeloid malignancies and other cancers lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Mutations occur in 81% of MDS with ring sideroblasts and 15–36% of eye melanomas; K700E correlates with worse CLL prognosis but better MDS prognosis. Mutant SF3B1 activates cryptic 3’ss 10–30 nt upstream and converges on repressing the tumour suppressor BRD9 via a poison exon (splicing-and-disease). Small molecules (H3B-8800 in trials, pladienolide B, spliceostatin A) block the open→closed change (splicing-therapeutics) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — BP recognition mechanism, mutation spectrum, inhibitors
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SF3b/SAP155 in myeloid cancer; spliceostatin
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/entities/signature-tool.title: signature-tool
wiki/entities/signature-tool.content:
SIGnature
SIGnature is an open-source Python package (built on Captum) that scores
per-gene importance by computing gradient-based attributions over
single-cell-foundation-models, introduced in
gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting. (Slug signature-tool
distinguishes it from the generic notion of a gene signature.)
What it is

Applies model-interpretability attribution methods (integrated gradients,
input×gradient, DeepLIFT) to pretrained scRNA-seq foundation models (SCimilarity,
scGPT, scVI, scTab, scFoundation) to measure each gene’s contribution to a cell’s
latent position.
Attributions are precomputed and queried as fast vector operations across
atlas-scale data (22M cells in minutes).

Standing

Beats expression-based signature scoring (highest F1 in 23/32 cell-type tests;
robust to depth/dropout); a query of the MS1 monocyte signature surfaced shared
activation in Kawasaki disease, SFTS and HLH, with in-vitro validation.
Attribution units are interpretive and inherit the underlying FM’s biases.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence / model-interpretability tool; complements the
perturbation-prediction siblings cmonge and txpert by interpreting
rather than predicting.

Sources

gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting — the method paper

wiki/entities/smn2.title: smn2
wiki/entities/smn2.content:
SMN2
What: The human survival motor neuron 2 gene, a near-duplicate of SMN1 that fails to produce enough functional protein because of an alternative-splicing defect — the central target in spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) therapy.
Spinal muscular atrophy is caused by loss of SMN1; SMN2 persists but cannot compensate (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). The two genes are ~30 kbp duplicates differing in only 11 nucleotides, the key change being a C>T substitution in SMN2 exon 7 that increases its skipping (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). The predominant SMN2 mRNA therefore lacks exon 7 (E7) and yields a truncated, inactive SMN protein, while the minor E7-included variant is full-length and functional.
Both pre-mRNAs carry a splicing silencer in intron 7 bound by the repressive splicing-factors hnrnpa1 and hnRNPA2, which promote E7 skipping (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). This is a textbook case of splicing-and-disease driven by silencer-controlled exon definition (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Why it matters
SMN2 is the proof-of-concept target for splicing-therapeutics.
