Nusinersen (approved 2016), an antisense oligonucleotide, blocks the intron-7 silencer to displace hnrnpa1/hnRNPA2 and boost E7 inclusion (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative). Risdiplam (FDA 2020), an oral pyrido-pyrimidinone small molecule, has similar effects by stabilizing U1 snRNA base-pairing at the SMN2 E7 5′ splice site (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). These were among the first splicing-corrective drugs.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — 11-nt SMN1/SMN2 difference, C>T in E7, intron-7 hnRNPA1/A2 silencer, nusinersen/risdiplam.
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — SMA from SMN1 loss; nusinersen ISS-blocking ASO; risdiplam mechanism on U1–E7 pairing.
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SMN2/SMA as a silencer-driven splicing disease and antisense target.

wiki/entities/spliceai.title: spliceai
wiki/entities/spliceai.content:
SpliceAI
What: A landmark end-to-end deep-learning model of the splicing-code that predicts splice-sites directly from genomic sequence.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual convolutional neural network trained on human GENCODE sequence, learning features de novo rather than from predefined motifs capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. It marks a shift in the model lineage from core-motif scorers (PWMs, MaxEntScan) and feature-engineered integrative models toward learned representations, and it sits alongside related deep models — Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice — and feeds into large omics-prediction models (Borzoi, BigRNA) and self-supervised sequence language models.
How it works
The model is tasked to call donor and acceptor sites from raw sequence. Context window size is decisive: extending input from 80 nt to 400 nt raised top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%, and going to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5% capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the. Accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%). In silico mutagenesis showed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin).
Significance & limits
SpliceAI predicts non-coding cryptic-splicing variants — estimated ~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations — and underpins clinical splicing-and-disease workflows: it reached AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants and informs ClinGen/ACMG variant classification. Limits remain: exonic and distal variants are predicted poorly, tissue-specific prediction is weak, and black-box interpretability is an open problem.
Sources

capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the — describes SpliceAI’s architecture, context-window effect, clinical use, and place in the splicing-code model lineage.

wiki/entities/splicetrap.title: splicetrap
wiki/entities/splicetrap.content:
SpliceTrap
SpliceTrap is a Bayesian method for quantifying local exon-inclusion ratios from paired-end RNA-seq data, estimating each exon’s inclusion as an independent inference problem instead of reconstructing full-length transcripts. Introduced in wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify.
What it is

A computational tool that reports the inclusion ratio of every exon under a single cellular condition, without requiring a background/reference read set.
Uses TXdb, a precompiled human exon-trio/duo database (RefSeq hg18 + EST-based dbCASE) of 224,995 trios/duos over 182,560 exons, as a mapping reference.
Detects the major AS classes: cassette exons (CA), alternative 5’ (AD) and 3’ (AA) splice sites, and intron retention (IR).
Combines a fragment-size prior (FSM) and an inclusion-ratio prior (IRM, beta-smoothed) in a Bayesian posterior maximized per exon; reads are aligned with Bowtie and filtered with a size-dependent dynamic coverage cutoff.
Outperformed Cufflinks, Scripture, and naive RPKM in simulation (PCC up to 0.87) and showed higher replicate robustness while reporting far more exons. Available via CSH Galaxy and rulai.cshl.edu/splicetrap.

In this wiki

A splicing-methods tool for RNA-seq alternative-splicing quantification.
Computes exon inclusion ratios, conceptually equivalent to percent-spliced-in.
Related to rmats as an RNA-seq exon-inclusion quantifier (SpliceTrap predates it and targets single-condition scoring).

Sources

wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify

wiki/entities/srsf1.title: srsf1
wiki/entities/srsf1.content:
SRSF1
What: The prototypical SR protein, a sequence-specific splicing factor that activates splicing and behaves as a proto-oncogene when overexpressed.
SRSF1 is a member of the SR-protein family of rna-binding-proteins that constitute the activating arm of the splicing-code. It typically binds exonic splicing enhancers (a class of splicing-regulatory-elements) and promotes inclusion of nearby exons during alternative-splicing. This is the classic counterpoint to hnRNP repressors like hnrnpa1: SR activators and hnRNP silencers act in a position-dependent manner, so the same factor or motif can promote or suppress an exon depending on where it binds lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) can promote use of a proximal 5’ splice site (see splice-sites) by increasing U1 snRNP–pre-mRNA binding lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. An SF2/ASF-dependent exonic splicing enhancer in smn2 exon 7 illustrates how its loss alters disease-relevant splicing.
Disease / significance
SRSF1 overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice and qualifies the SRSF1 gene as a proto-oncogene in many human tumours, with targets in the mTORC1 pathway lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative. Its oncogenic function depends on methylation by PRMT5; SRSF1 also shuttles between nucleus and cytoplasm, making PRMT5 inhibitors candidate therapeutics (splicing-and-disease, splicing-therapeutics) rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Sources

lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — SR activator mechanism, proximal 5’ss/U1, proto-oncogene
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — proto-oncogene; PRMT5 methylation
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — PRMT5 dependence, nucleocytoplasmic shuttling

wiki/entities/suppa.title: suppa
wiki/entities/suppa.content:
SUPPA
SUPPA is a fast computational tool that calculates relative inclusion (PSI / Ψ) values of alternative splicing events directly from transcript-level abundances (TPMs), rather than from reads mapped to the genome or to exon–exon junctions. Introduced in alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast.
What it is

Two-step pipeline: generateEvents derives splicing events from a GTF annotation (or a de novo reconstruction), and psiPerEvent computes Ψ per sample from transcript quantifications.
Defines Ψ as the abundance of inclusion-form transcripts (F1) divided by the abundance of transcripts in either form (F1∪F2), assigning each event a unique identifier encoding gene, event type, coordinates, and strand.
Handles event types SE (exon skipping), A5/A3 (alternative 5′/3′ splice sites), MX (mutually exclusive exons), RI (intron retention), and AF/AL (alternative first/last exons).
Quantifier-agnostic: benchmarked with Sailfish and RSEM; >1000× faster than read-based methods (MATS, MISO) for the Ψ step.
Implemented in Python 2.7 with no Python-library dependencies, MIT-licensed (bitbucket.org/regulatorygenomicsupf/suppa).
Accuracy depends on annotation completeness (full-length transcripts as in RefSeq outperform incomplete Ensembl annotation); this version does not propagate quantification uncertainty into Ψ.

In this wiki

A transcript-quantification-based alternative within splicing-methods, contrasting with read/junction-based event detectors like rmats.
Outputs the percent-spliced-in metric for alternative-splicing events.

Sources

alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast

wiki/entities/tass-catalogue.title: tass-catalogue
wiki/entities/tass-catalogue.content:
TASS catalogue
The TASS catalogue is an extended, RNA-seq-based catalogue of tandem
alternative splice sites (closely spaced ≤30 nt alternative donor/acceptor
splice-sites) in healthy human tissues, built from 8,548 GTEx samples and
distributed as a UCSC Genome Browser track hub. Introduced in
mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem.
What it is

A list of ~151k analyzed splice sites in TASS clusters, integrating annotated
(GENCODE v19 + RefSeq), GTEx-expressed (de novo), and spliceai-scored
cryptic sites; 115,912 expressed TASS and 45,739 expressed minor splice
sites (miSS).
Per-cluster ranking into a major splice site (maSS) and minor splice
sites (miSS), quantified by the relative-usage metric φ (a single-ended
TASS analogue of percent-spliced-in).
Annotations layered on top: significance (zero-inflated Poisson model, 9,303
significant miSS), tissue-specificity (2,496), NAGNAG/GYNNGY membership,
RBP-regulation predictions (rmats-turbo knockdown + eCLIP), protein
structural impact (disordered regions, SLiMs, PTM sites), and evolutionary
selection / noise-fraction estimates.
Substantially extends the EST-based TASSDB2 database (+~51k TASS); delivered as
a Genome Browser track hub (TASS indels, structural annotation, tissue-specific
expression) plus a supplementary table.

In this wiki

A reusable splicing-methods resource; uses rmats (rMATS-turbo novelSS)
for the RBP-knockdown analysis and spliceai to define cryptic sites.
Surfaces concrete rna-binding-proteins regulation, notably a QKI miSS
repressed by ptbp1.
Its φ metric is a relative of percent-spliced-in.

Sources

mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem

wiki/entities/trrosettarna2.title: trrosettarna2
wiki/entities/trrosettarna2.content:
trRosettaRNA2
trRosettaRNA2 is an end-to-end deep learning method (~30M parameters) for
RNA 3D structure and conformer prediction from the
Yang lab, introduced in wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure.
What it is

Four-stage pipeline (SS prior → RNAformer → SS-aware invariant-point-attention
structure module → PyRosetta refinement) whose signature trick is
SS-weighted softmax: a pre-trained secondary-structure prior biases the 3D
attention maps, compensating for scarce RNA 3D training data.
Embeds trRNA2-SS, a standalone state-of-the-art RNA secondary-structure
predictor (bpRNA pre-train → PDB fine-tune).
Sampling diverse SS inputs lets it generate alternative RNA conformers
without experimental data.

Standing

Top automated server group (Yang-Server) at CASP16; competitive with AlphaFold 3
on TS28 (RMSD 8.66 Å vs 9.14 Å) with far fewer clashes and ~1/12 the parameters.
Limitations: two-/single-chain RNA only (no RNA–protein/ligand complexes), crop
size ≤384 nt.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence AI-for-structural-biology method; a sibling of
genomics sequence models like spliceai in baking biological priors into the
network.

Sources

wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure — the method paper

wiki/entities/txpert.title: txpert
wiki/entities/txpert.content:
TxPert
TxPert is a latent-transfer deep learning model (Valence Labs / Recursion) for
single-cell-perturbation-modeling that fuses multiple gene–gene
knowledge-graphs, introduced in
wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge.
What it is

A basal-state encoder (control profiles) + a GNN perturbation encoder over
interaction graphs, combined by additive latent shift and decoded to the
perturbed transcriptome.
Handles three OOD tasks: unseen single perturbations, novel doubles, and
perturbations in unseen cell lines.
Key finding: combining complementary graphs (STRING, GO + screen-derived
PxMap/TxMap) is what drives performance.

Standing

Approaches split-half experimental reproducibility for unseen singles in 3/4 cell
lines; +8–25% Pearson Δ over prior methods; beats GEARS (which falls below a
nonlearned baseline).
Documented failure mode: does not predict the target gene’s own knockdown; relies
partly on proprietary graphs.

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence method; sibling to cmonge and
signature-tool in the single-cell cluster.

Sources

wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge — the method paper

wiki/entities/u2af1.title: u2af1
wiki/entities/u2af1.content:
U2AF1
What: The small subunit of the U2AF heterodimer that recognizes the 3’ splice-site AG dinucleotide during intron 3’-end definition.
U2AF1 (also called U2AF35) is one of the splicing-factors that define the 3’ end of introns at splice-sites, acting alongside SF1, sf3b1 and U2 snRNP rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Role in splicing
The U2AF heterodimer (U2AF1–U2AF2/U2AF65) binds three adjacent 3’-end sequence motifs: U2AF1 contacts the 3’ss AG dinucleotide while U2AF2 binds the polypyrimidine tract rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing. This makes U2AF1 a determinant of 3’ss selection. As a RNA-binding step in 3’-end recognition, its binding can be modulated — e.g. m6A at the 3’ss AG inhibits U2AF1 binding, leading to intron retention rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Disease & therapeutics
U2AF1 (U2AF35) is recurrently somatically mutated in myeloid cancers including myelodysplastic syndrome lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative. Mutations alter which flanking-nucleotide 3’ss are selected: S34F/Y mutants favour inclusion of exons with CAG 3’ss and disfavour UAG, whereas Q157P/R mutants favour AG/A 3’ss rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing (splicing-and-disease). U2AF1, SRSF2 and sf3b1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Sources

rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing — 3’ss recognition, S34/Q157 mutant effects, m6A
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — U2AF35 recurrent mutations in MDS/CLL
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — convergence of myeloid splicing-factor mutations

wiki/entities/uniair.title: uniair
wiki/entities/uniair.content:
UniAIR
UniAIR is a modular, multimodal sequence–structure framework for
generalizable mutation-effect-prediction (binding ΔΔG) across adaptive
immune-recognition (antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC), introduced in
han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across.
What it is

ESSM fusion backbone — an S-Former co-attention transformer coupling a
protein language model (ESM2) with geometric
structure.
MoFPE — a mixture of four frozen structure-pretrained experts combined via a
structure-aware gating network (multimodal-learning).
UniAIR-LT — a latent adapter aligning predicted-structure embeddings to
experimental ones, for structure-free use.

Standing

State of the art on SKEMPIv2 (PCC 0.684); generalizes to held-out antibody and
TCR test sets; used zero-shot for antibody/peptide optimization validated by FEP.
Limitation: protein–protein interfaces only (no small molecules/nucleic acids).

In this wiki

An artificial-intelligence / ai-in-genomics method; safety-adjacent
(viral escape, immunogen design) — cf.
hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.

Sources

han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across — the method paper

wiki/overview.title: overview
wiki/overview.content:
Overview
The evolving top-level thesis of this wiki. It now spans four topics:

splicing — pre-mRNA / alternative splicing (44 sources, 1994–2026)
artificial-intelligence — AI/ML in biology & genomics: foundation models,
sequence/structure, single-cell & perturbation methods + safety (7 sources, 2026)
3d-genome — 3D organization of the genome and its evolution (1 source, 2026)
functional-single-cell-genomics — experimental/resource genomics: single-cell
omics, MPRA, digital twins (3 sources, 2026)

Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See index for the full
per-topic catalog.

Cross-topic threads: (1) deep-learning models of biological sequence connect
splicing and AI — the splicing-code / spliceai work is one instance of
the foundation-models discussed under artificial-intelligence;
(2) chromatin links splicing and the 3D genome — splicing-and-chromatin
meets genome-architecture.

Current thesis — topic: splicing
RNA splicing is a central layer of gene regulation, and across these sources
the field’s center of gravity has shifted from how splicing works to how it
is controlled and read:

The machinery is essentially solved. The spliceosome is a large,
dynamic, RNA-catalysed machine (a ribozyme related to group II introns), and
cryo-EM has resolved most of its states
(lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative,
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). Remaining questions are
about regulation, not chemistry.
Outcomes are set by a position-dependent code. Weak metazoan
splice-sites + splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors
determine isoform choice. Modeling this splicing-code has progressed from
position weight matrices to deep-learning models that now interpret ~10% of
pathogenic rare-disease variants
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but
a large fraction (~40%) is post-transcriptional-splicing, gating mRNA
export and translation (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Function vs noise is unresolved. alternative-splicing occurs in
~95–100% of human multiexon genes, but how much is functional is debated
(see Open questions).
Mechanism is becoming medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer and
genetic disease (splicing-and-disease); the first splicing-targeted drugs
are approved (splicing-therapeutics).
A mature measurement-and-genetics stack underpins all of it. A decade-plus of
rna-seq tooling now quantifies splicing two ways — isoform-based
(cufflinks, kallisto, suppa) and event-based (rmats/rmats-turbo,
miso, leafcutter, majiq-v2, dexseq) — and population genetics maps
sQTLs linking variation to disease
(park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative). Tools disagree, so the
open methodological question is which readout to trust when.

Open questions

Functionality of AS — ✅ Mostly reconciled (see
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate): the apparent
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (functional) vs
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (noise) clash is largely a
difference in the definition of “functional”, the denominator counted, and the
evidence weighted — not a factual contradiction. Both agree on a conserved
functional core; residual uncertainty is the size of the low-abundance tail,
resolvable only with long-read/single-cell/CRISPR data.
Co- vs post-transcriptional timing trigger — mechanism unknown
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Interpretable, tissue-specific splicing-code models — still open
(capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).

Current thesis — topic: artificial-intelligence
Across seven sources, foundation-models have become general-purpose
biological tools spanning every modality: DNA (crossdna), protein
(uniair), RNA structure (trrosettarna2), and single cells (the FMs
signature-tool interprets). Two patterns recur: (1) injecting domain priors
beats blind scale — secondary-structure priors, explicit cross-strand symmetry,
gene knowledge-graphs, and optimal-transport let small models beat ones
orders of magnitude larger; (2) generalization to the unseen (drugs, cell
lines, mutations, conformers) is the shared frontier, limited by
data-quality-and-bias, and often checked by direct measurement
(massively-parallel-reporter-assay, ΔPSI) where models
are weak. Safety remains the backdrop: generative capability keeps
dual-use-risk immediate and no single ai-safety safeguard suffices,
motivating layered measures plus ai-governance
(hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Current thesis — topic: 3d-genome
One source so far (che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome): nuclear
genome-architecture (loops → TADs → compartments) is an evolvable trait.
It evolves fastest in cancer (3d-genome-and-disease), diverges between close
species mainly via cis-regulatory-elements within conserved frameworks, and
recurs across kingdoms through convergent evolution with distinct machinery
(3d-genome-evolution). Thin until more sources are ingested.
Current thesis — topic: functional-single-cell-genomics
Three sources frame the measurement / resource counterpart to the AI methods:
a single-cell proteomic atlas of the developing human brain
(wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of, single-cell-proteomics), an
MPRA mapping >30,000 cis-regulatory mutations in sorghum
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at,
massively-parallel-reporter-assay), and digital twins of ex vivo human lungs
(zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex, digital-twins). The unifying thesis:
direct, high-throughput measurement still beats prediction where models are
weakest (mRNA→protein inference, activating regulatory variants), and these
resources are exactly the atlases, variant maps, and platforms the
artificial-intelligence topic’s models learn from and are tested against.
Key concepts
Splicing — spliceosome · alternative-splicing · splicing-code ·
post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-disease · splicing-therapeutics.
AI — foundation-models · dna-language-models · single-cell-perturbation-modeling ·
mutation-effect-prediction · model-interpretability · dual-use-risk · ai-safety.
3D genome — genome-architecture · topologically-associating-domains ·
chromatin-loops · 3d-genome-evolution.
Functional & single-cell genomics — single-cell-proteomics ·
massively-parallel-reporter-assay · digital-twins.
See index for the full catalog.

wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.title: alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast
wiki/sources/alamancos-2015-leveraging-t.content:
Leveraging transcript quantification for fast computation of alternative splicing profiles
One-line: Introduces suppa, a computational tool that derives PSI (Ψ) inclusion values for alternative splicing events directly from fast transcript abundances (TPMs), achieving accuracy comparable to read-based methods while running more than 1000 times faster.
Key takeaways

SUPPA computes relative inclusion levels (percent-spliced-in) of local alternative splicing events from transcript-level abundances rather than from reads mapped to the genome or junctions, sidestepping the time/storage cost of mapping.
It works in two operations: generateEvents derives events from a GTF annotation (or a de novo reconstruction), and psiPerEvent computes Ψ per sample from transcript TPMs, defining Ψ as the abundance of the inclusion-form transcripts (F1) over the abundance of all transcripts in either form (F1∪F2).
SUPPA is agnostic to the quantification method; it was tested coupled to Sailfish and RSEM, and benchmarked against the read-based methods MATS and MISO.
On simulated data SUPPA was closer to ground-truth than MATS and MISO; on RT-PCR–validated events it was slightly superior or comparable; and it was >1000× faster than MATS/MISO for the Ψ-calculation step.
Annotation completeness (full-length transcripts, e.g. RefSeq) matters more than the number of transcripts per gene for accurate Ψ estimation; incomplete annotations (Ensembl) degrade accuracy.
Software is implemented in Python 2.7, has no Python-library dependencies, and is MIT-licensed (released at bitbucket.org/regulatorygenomicsupf/suppa).

Notable claims / data

Supported event types: exon skipping (SE), alternative 5′/3′ splice sites (A5/A3), mutually exclusive exons (MX), intron retention (RI), and alternative first/last exons (AF/AL).
Simulated benchmark (144 events, RefSeq, FluxSimulator reads): Sailfish+SUPPA Pearson R = 0.971, RSEM+SUPPA R = 0.987, vs MATS R = 0.833 and MISO R = 0.862 against ground truth. SUPPA kept R > 0.9 for all event types, including MX where MATS/MISO accuracy dropped sharply.
ΔΨ cumulative-distribution differences between SUPPA and MATS/MISO were statistically significant on simulated data (rank-sum P-values from 8.89×10⁻¹² down to 2.2×10⁻¹⁶).
Experimentally validated set: 163 SE events validated by RT-PCR in MDA-MB-231 cells under ESRP1 overexpression vs empty vector (Shen et al. 2012); on the 60 RefSeq-common events SUPPA was slightly better or comparable to MATS/MISO (ΔΨ distributions not significantly different, P > 0.1).
Annotation effect: RefSeq > Ensembl accuracy across all methods; restricting Ensembl to CDS recovered some SUPPA accuracy, but Ensembl-based SUPPA still trailed MATS/MISO — implicating transcript-quantification quality driven by annotation completeness.
Replicate reproducibility (MCF7/MCF10 cytosolic RNA, Sailfish+RefSeq): Pearson R ≈ 0.86–0.89 overall, rising to ≈ 0.95–0.97 when restricted to genes with summed TPM > 1.
Speed: generateEvents took 3 min for 16,714 RefSeq events (25,937 genes / 48,566 transcripts) and 20 min for 66,577 Ensembl 75 events (37,494 genes / 135,521 transcripts); psiPerEvent ran in under a minute and was >1000× faster than MATS/MISO. End-to-end (raw FastQ → Ψ) with Sailfish+SUPPA on RefSeq events: ~3.5 min (214 s) for synthetic data and ~1 h (4022 s) for the MDA-MB-231 samples.
Annotation-free mode: SUPPA on Cufflinks de novo transcripts gave accuracy comparable to the Ensembl annotation but lower than RefSeq; re-quantifying Cufflinks transcripts with Sailfish gave no improvement — a limit of de novo exon–intron structure prediction from short reads.
Caveats: SUPPA (this version) does not propagate transcript-quantification uncertainty into Ψ, handles only binary event forms, and depends entirely on the annotation/reconstruction quality.

Connections

A transcript-quantification-based alternative to read/junction-based event tools such as rmats, MATS, and MISO; complements the broader landscape of splicing-methods.
Centers on percent-spliced-in as its core output metric.
Pairs with fast quantifiers (Sailfish, RSEM; conceptually similar to kallisto/Salmon-style alignment-free quantification) — the speed of SUPPA is gated by the quantifier rather than by mapping.
Relevant to large-scale and resource-limited alternative-splicing studies, including cancer-focused work (the validation data come from ESRP1-perturbed breast-cancer cells), connecting to splicing-and-disease.

Open questions

Would incorporating transcript-quantification uncertainty (posterior intervals) into the Ψ estimate measurably improve SUPPA’s accuracy, as the authors speculate?
Can the binary event model be extended to multi-conformation / complex events without losing experimental testability (e.g. the QKI-type changes noted for lung adenocarcinoma)?
How much does pairing SUPPA with newer alignment-free quantifiers and more complete long-read-derived annotations close the RefSeq-vs-Ensembl accuracy gap?
Does the no-uncertainty, annotation-dependent design limit differential-splicing applications across many conditions, where read-based posterior methods may be more robust?

wiki/sources/anders-2012-detecting-diffe.title: anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons
wiki/sources/anders-2012-detecting-diffe.content:
Detecting differential usage of exons from RNA-Seq data
One-line: Introduces dexseq, a GLM/negative-binomial statistical method (and R/Bioconductor package) for detecting differential exon usage between conditions in RNA-Seq data, with explicit modelling of biological variability for reliable false-discovery control.
Key takeaways

DEXSeq tests for differential exon usage: whether the fraction of a gene’s reads falling into a given exon (or “counting bin”) changes between conditions, rather than assembling full transcripts or estimating isoform abundance.
It belongs to the third of three analysis categories the authors lay out: (1) gene-level counting (edgeR, DESeq, BaySeq); (2) full-length transcript assembly/quantification (cufflinks, MMSeq, Jiang & Wong); (3) directly observable exon-level quantities — DEXSeq’s approach.
Core statistical machinery: per-gene generalized linear models with a negative binomial distribution; the linear predictor decomposes into gene baseline, per-bin exon effect, condition effect on expression, and a counting-bin x condition interaction. A nonzero interaction coefficient flags differential exon usage; the test is an analysis-of-deviance (ANODEV) chi-squared likelihood-ratio test of full vs reduced model.
Key innovation over prior methods (Wang et al. 2008, miso / Katz et al. 2010, AlexaSeq, early cuffdiff): it accounts for biological variability via a per-bin dispersion parameter, sharing information across genes through a fitted mean-dispersion relation.
“Flattening” of gene models: overlapping/variable-boundary exons across transcripts are cut into non-overlapping counting bins; reads overlapping multiple bins are counted in each.
Dispersion is estimated with the Cox-Reid adjusted profile likelihood (McCarthy et al. 2012, as in edgeR), then a per-bin estimate is combined with a fitted mean-dispersion trend alpha(mu)=a1/mu + a0 by taking the maximum (conservative, costs some power but limits false positives).
Implemented as an open-source Bioconductor R package plus two HTSeq-based Python scripts (flatten annotation -> count reads); produces HTML reports and visualizations with a variance-stabilizing axis “warp”.

Notable claims / data

Brooks et al. Drosophila pasilla knock-down data (3 control + 4 knock-down samples): at 10% FDR, DEXSeq found differential exon usage for 259 counting bins across 159 genes. Example gene Ten-m / CG5723, bin E004: p = 2.1e-11, adjusted p = 1.2e-8.
Fitted dispersion-mean relation alpha(mu) = 1.3/mu + 0.012; a0 = 0.012 implies ~11% coefficient of variation in exon usage between biological replicates for strongly expressed exons. Using the expression-absorbing model (3) instead of model (2) lowered dispersion (from 1.6/mu + 0.018), preserving power.
Overdispersion matters: setting dispersions to zero (Poisson assumption) on the Brooks data yielded 36x as many hits (9,432 bins in 3,610 genes), mostly unsupported false positives.
Chimpanzee data (Brawand et al.; prefrontal cortex vs cerebellum): 866 bins in 650 genes at 10% FDR. Biological example hits include PRKCZ (PB1 heterodimerization domain exons, PKMzeta vs PKCzeta), PLCH2 (EF-hand exon), and precortistatin.
ENCODE human cell-line comparison (h1-hESC vs HUVEC, biological duplicates): 7,795 genes affected by differential exon usage.
Benchmark vs cuffdiff: on the Brooks data cuffdiff 1.3.0 called only 37 genes (less power than DEXSeq’s 159); in a mock replicate-vs-replicate comparison (no true effects expected) DEXSeq called only 8 genes while cuffdiff called 455 — indicating poor type-I error control by cuffdiff. Similar pattern on chimpanzee data (DEXSeq 700 genes vs cuffdiff 108 in the real comparison).
Caveat the authors themselves flag: when a large fraction of a gene’s exons are affected, attribution to specific bins becomes unreliable, and differential exon usage cannot always be distinguished from overall up/down-regulation of part of the gene; the per-bin call is trustworthy only when a small fraction of bins are significant.

Connections

A foundational entry under splicing-methods for RNA-Seq-based detection of alternative splicing; explicitly contrasts itself with event/isoform-centric tools such as miso and cufflinks, and conceptually relates to the percent-spliced-in family of metrics (percent-spliced-in) by quantifying relative exon usage.
Methodologically a descendant of DESeq (Anders & Huber 2010) and edgeR, extending negative-binomial GLM count modelling from gene-level differential expression to the exon/counting-bin level.
Sits alongside other read-counting/event detection tools in the wiki’s splicing-tools cluster (e.g. rmats) as an early, replicate-aware, biological-variability-modelling approach.

Open questions

Could leveraging junction (exon-skipping) reads — the ratio of reads skipping vs overlapping a bin — increase power for bins short relative to read length, as the authors anticipate?
How best to interpolate between per-bin and fitted dispersion estimates (beyond the conservative maximum rule), possibly adding covariates beyond the mean?
Can exon-centric testing be combined with isoform-deconvolution methods so that an exon-level analysis becomes a component of transcript-level inference?
Systematic, automated integration of protein-domain / miRNA-target annotation (Pfam, SMART, Prosite) to interpret which differential exons are functionally consequential.

wiki/sources/baralle-2017-alternative-sp.title: baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator
wiki/sources/baralle-2017-alternative-sp.content:
Alternative splicing as a regulator of development and tissue identity
One-line: A foundational review arguing that coordinated alternative splicing networks — driven mainly by developmentally regulated RNA-binding proteins and coupled to transcription/epigenetics — govern cell differentiation, tissue-identity acquisition and organ development across brain, heart, skeletal muscle, liver and other tissues.
Key takeaways

The field has shifted from cataloguing single splicing events to describing global, molecularly coordinated alternative-splicing networks; the central open challenge is demonstrating their functional/physiological consequences, not just their regulation.
splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) are the principal coordinators of developmental splicing transitions: their expression levels, localization, activity and own alternative splicing are finely tuned per tissue and developmental stage. A tissue-specific “splicing-code” is proposed but hard to decipher because the same motif is read differently by different RBPs depending on context and binding position.
A recurring theme: genes regulated by developmental alternative splicing are often not changed in total expression level — splicing is the dominant regulatory layer for them (e.g. 31% of differentially spliced cortical genes show no expression change).
Splicing is frequently co-transcriptional, so cotranscriptional-splicing, Pol II elongation kinetics, chromatin modifications, nucleosome occupancy and DNA methylation modulate outcomes (splicing-and-chromatin); slow Pol II favors weak-splice-site recognition and exon inclusion. Coupling can run both ways (splicing of chromatin modifiers like EHMT2/LSD1 feeds back on the epigenome).
A major mechanistic motif is productive coupling of alternative splicing to NMD (“AS-NMD”): poison exons / intron retention introduce premature termination codons to downregulate proteins, prominently for RNA-processing regulators during cell differentiation (smooth muscle, erythropoiesis).
Misregulation of these same networks underlies disease — reversion of adult tissues to fetal/neonatal splicing patterns is a hallmark of heart failure and myotonic dystrophy (splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

90-95% of human genes undergo some alternative splicing; of ~20,000 protein-coding genes, high-resolution mass spectrometry finds ~37% generate multiple protein isoforms (caveat: not all AS events yield functional proteins; amplification methods can overestimate splicing events).
Brain: adult cortex vs embryos show ~400 differential AS events; 31% of developmentally AS-regulated genes do not change total expression. ptbp1/PTBP2/SRRM4 drive the neural progenitor→neuron transition (PTBP1 represses PTBP2 exon 10 → PTC+NMD; SRRM4 promotes its inclusion). RBFOX-depleted neurons misregulate >500 cassette exons. NOVA2 controls DAB1 exon 7b/7c skipping governing neuronal migration (rescued by short isoform in Nova2−/− mice).
Microexons (3-15 nt) regulated by SRRM4 tune protein-interaction domains in neurogenesis and are misregulated in autism (SRRM4 upregulation). Tau exon 10 sets 3R/4R isoform ratio; imbalance impairs APP axonal transport.
Heart: cardiomyocyte AS transitions concentrate in the first 4 weeks postnatally; sf3b1 is hypoxia-induced in cardiac hypertrophy, mis-splicing ketohexokinase exons toward a liver isoform that drives anabolic metabolism and contractile failure. CELF1/MBNL1 antagonistically regulate cardiac splicing.
Titin (TTN, highest exon count of any known gene): I-band exons 50-219 are developmentally regulated; neonatal long N2BA (3.7 MDa) → adult short N2B (2.97 MDa); ratio sets sarcomere passive tension and myocardium wall stiffness. RBM20 regulates this via a two-step splicing-repressor mechanism; RBM20 mutations cause human dilated cardiomyopathy. A CRISPR-Cas9 mouse blocking the Mex5/is7-containing TTN isoform produced a dystrophic phenotype in soleus and heart.
Myotonic dystrophy (most common adult muscular dystrophy): CTG/CCTG repeat expansions in DMPK 3′UTR (type 1) or CNBP intron 1 (type 2) sequester MBNL1 and activate CELF1, reverting adult tissues to fetal isoforms — e.g. INSR exon 11 (insulin resistance), CLCN1 exon 7a (myotonia), SCN5A (cardiac conduction defects); ~80% of patients develop cardiac dysfunction.
Other tissues: liver maturation requires ESRP2 (one of few postnatally induced RBPs) and SRSF3/SRSF10/SLU7; pancreas networks via NOVA1 and RBM4 (ISL1, PAX4 → insulin); spermatogenesis shows splicing-epigenetics coupling (MRG15 reads H3K36me3, recruits PTBP1/2 to splice TNP2; knockout → intron retention, round-spermatid arrest, sterility); T-cell activation feedback loop (CELF2↔JNK via MKK7 exon 2 skipping; HNRNPL/PSF control CD45). Erythropoiesis uses stage-specific exon 16 switching in protein 4.1R and MBNL1-regulated Ndel1 exon inclusion.
Caveat the authors stress repeatedly: dramatic splicing changes can have only slight physiological impact (e.g. fibronectin EDA transitions do not majorly affect mouse development), so genome-wide data must be paired with molecular validation.

Connections

Companion physiology-of-splicing review to marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative; complements the mechanism/regulation reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of, and the topic editorial editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Heavily features RBP entities in the wiki: ptbp1 (neuron/smooth-muscle/germ-cell repressor), sf3b1 (cardiac hypertrophy fructolysis), hnrnpa1-family logic (HNRNPA/B in erythropoiesis), and U2AF65 (u2af1 paralog context) in spermatogenesis.
Provides the developmental/tissue grounding for splicing-and-disease and motivates splicing-therapeutics (antisense oligonucleotides reverted adult→fetal splicing in mouse muscle).
The co-transcriptional/epigenetic coupling sections connect to cotranscriptional-splicing, post-transcriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; the predict-from-sequence difficulty motivates work cataloged under splicing-methods and splice-sites/splicing-regulatory-elements.

Open questions

How to functionally characterize the thousands of developmentally regulated protein isoforms whose roles remain unknown (the authors call this the field’s biggest bottleneck)?
When does a large splicing change actually matter physiologically vs being tolerated (the FN1-EDA paradox)? What predicts functional impact?
Can a tissue-specific splicing code be learned given that cellular composition, RBP combinations and binding-position effects confound prediction from pre-mRNA sequence alone?
How general is splicing↔epigenetics bidirectional coupling in development beyond the few demonstrated loci (EHMT2, LSD1, MRG15/TNP2, CTCF/CD45)?

wiki/sources/begg-2020-concentration-dep.title: begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled
wiki/sources/begg-2020-concentration-dep.content:
Concentration-dependent splicing is enabled by Rbfox motifs of intermediate affinity
One-line: A biochemistry/mechanism study showing that the Rbfox family of splicing factors binds, beyond its canonical high-affinity GCAUG motif, a set of “secondary” intermediate-affinity motifs that only become functionally active when Rbfox concentration is high — enabling a second, concentration-gated wave of alternative-splicing regulation during neuronal differentiation.
Key takeaways

Rbfox proteins are splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins long known to bind the pentamer GCAUG with high affinity, yet about half of in vivo CLIP peaks lack a GCAYG motif — motivating a search for additional binding determinants.
Using RNA Bind-n-Seq with natural transcriptomic sequences (nsRBNS) across a 2.5-order-of-magnitude RBFOX2 concentration range, the authors identify six non-GCAYG “secondary motifs” of the form GHNUG (G_NUG) of intermediate affinity; four (GCUUG, GAAUG, GUUUG, GUAUG) are validated in vivo.
These secondary motifs are bona fide splicing-regulatory-elements: clusters of them placed downstream of an alternative exon drive exon inclusion comparable to a single GCAUG in a fluorescent splicing reporter, but only when Rbfox is abundant.
The functional consequence is concentration-dependent / stage-specific regulation: secondary motifs act later in neuronal differentiation and in neuronal subtypes with the highest Rbfox expression, adding a “second wave” of splicing changes as Rbfox levels rise.
An equilibrium biophysical model explains the behavior: high-affinity primary motifs saturate at low Rbfox levels; abundant secondary motifs only accumulate occupancy once Rbfox is high.

Notable claims / data

nsRBNS performed with recombinant RBFOX2 at 4, 14, 43, 121 (duplicate), 365 nM and 1.1 µM (plus 0 nM control), constant 250 nM RNA; library of ~2,200 human/mouse 3’ UTRs cut into overlapping 110-base segments (longer than typical 20–40 nt random RBNS, allowing motif-cluster analysis).
Of 55,931 detectable sequences at 365 nM, 8,412 (15%) bound at R ≥ 1.1; about half (4,032) contained a canonical GCAYG. Of the 4,358 bound sequences lacking a primary motif, 1,885 (43%) contained ≥1 GHNUG secondary motif.
Iterative 5-mer enrichment (SKA-style, run at 1.1 µM to favor low-affinity sites) recovered GCAUG/GCACG first, then six GHNUG secondary motifs (e.g., GCUUG, GAAUG, GUUUG, GUAUG, GUGUG, GCCUG).
Position-specificity: G1, U4, G5 are the critical recognition sites (consistent with the RBFOX1–UGCAUG structure, PDB 2ERR), and phyloP conservation of GCAUG instances peaks at G1 and G5.
Primary motifs saturate ~365 nM and decline at 1.1 µM; secondary motifs show steadily increasing enrichment, saturating at/above 1.1 µM. Prior SPR Kd values for secondary motifs were ~100–600 nM vs low-nM for GCAUG.
In RBFOX1 HiTS–CLIP (mouse whole brain, ventral spinal neurons), ≥2 secondary motifs were enriched ~5-fold (intronic) and ~2.5-fold (3’ UTR) above background; together with primary motifs they explain a substantial fraction of peaks that lack a primary motif.
Reporter (RG6, Cerulean–RBFOX1, HEK293T): one GCAUG or six copies of a secondary motif drove PSI toward ~100% with exogenous RBFOX1; effect scaled with single-cell Rbfox level by flow cytometry.
Neuronal differentiation time course (mESC → glutamatergic neurons): total Rbfox rises ~5-fold RG→DS3; secondary motifs correlate with ΔPSI only in the higher-Rbfox DS1–DS3 interval, whereas primary motifs correlate at both intervals. Primary-motif exons (n=388) enriched for membrane/cytoskeleton GO terms; secondary-motif exons (n=561) enriched for dendrite development / signal transduction.
Mouse Cd47 exon 10 (36% inclusion increase DS1→DS3) used as an endogenous target; secondary motifs in its 617-nt downstream intron act additively (restoring a 5-motif cluster gave +19% PSI; adding GUUUG and GAAUG gave +10% and +11%).
Across 13 neuronal cell types spanning ~3 orders of magnitude of Rbfox expression: each downstream GCAUG increases exon PSI by ~15%, each secondary motif by ~3.6% (so ~4 secondary motifs ≈ one primary).
Quantitative equilibrium model: estimated nuclear GCAUG concentration ~16–63 nM, GCACG ~3–12 nM, four secondary motifs together ~67–270 nM; secondary motifs reach ~50% occupancy at ~10–50 µM nuclear Rbfox. Filter-binding experiments confirmed secondary motifs are preferentially bound after primary-motif saturation.
Caveats: nsRBNS is in vitro and lacks the native protein environment (partner proteins, modifications, e.g. SUP-12 / LASR-complex hnRNP M that have been credited with GUGUG binding); identified motifs reflect the strongest plus most transcriptome-abundant secondary sites, not necessarily all high-affinity variants.

Connections

Extends the concept of splicing-regulatory-elements and the broader splicing code by arguing for replacing the binary “binding site vs non-binding site” view with one that incorporates site affinity AND transcriptomic abundance, with low-affinity sites mattering when trans-factor (rna-binding-proteins) activity is high.
Mechanistic complement to existing splicing-factors sources (e.g. lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of) by detailing how one RBP family achieves concentration-gated, cell-type-specific regulation.
Uses percent spliced in (PSI / ΔPSI) as the splicing readout (relates to percent-spliced-in).
Draws an explicit analogy to transcription-factor suboptimal/low-affinity motifs (PHA-4, PREP1, GATA/FGF) and to an miRNA equilibrium-occupancy model; speculates secondary motifs may contribute to RBP phase-separated compartments.

Open questions

Are GUGUG/GCCUG bound directly by Rbfox in vivo, or are their CLIP signals attributable to partner proteins (SUP-12, LASR/hnRNP M)? The study leaves direct binding “not excluded.”
How general is concentration-gated secondary-motif regulation across other RBP families, and does it shape phase-separated condensate behavior?
How much of disease-relevant Rbfox dysregulation (autism, epilepsy) is mediated specifically through secondary-motif targets rather than canonical GCAUG sites?
Can secondary-motif affinity tiers be incorporated into predictive splicing-code models to improve in vivo binding-site inference beyond GCAUG filtering?

wiki/sources/bray-2016-near-optimal-prob.title: bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification
wiki/sources/bray-2016-near-optimal-prob.content:
Near-optimal probabilistic RNA-seq quantification
One-line: Introduces kallisto, a pseudoalignment-based RNA-seq quantification program that is two orders of magnitude faster than alignment-based pipelines while matching their accuracy, removing a major computational bottleneck in transcript-level RNA-seq analysis.
Key takeaways

kallisto replaces base-level read alignment with pseudoalignment: for each read it identifies only the subset of transcripts the read is compatible with, without computing exact base coordinates. This is sufficient for transcript-level quantification because quantification needs to know which transcripts could have generated a read, not where in the transcript it originated.
It builds a transcriptome de Bruijn graph (T-DBG) from k-mers in the reference transcriptome, with a path covering where paths correspond to transcripts. The path cover induces k-compatibility classes on k-mers; intersecting the k-compatibility classes of a read’s constituent k-mers yields the read’s equivalence class.
Abundances are estimated by running the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm over equivalence classes (not individual reads), since equivalence-class counts are sufficient statistics — typically hundreds of thousands of classes instead of tens of millions of reads.
Speed gain is dramatic: ~30 million paired-end reads quantified in <10 min on a standard laptop, versus tens of core-hours for TopHat2 + Cufflinks. This is a splicing-methods tool relevant to isoform-level expression that underlies downstream alternative splicing analyses.
The tool entity is kallisto.

Notable claims / data

Accuracy vs alignment: similar median relative difference to RSEM, EMSAR, eXpress; substantially better than Cufflinks and Sailfish (Fig. 2a). kallisto reported median relative differences in the low range (~0.03-0.06) comparable to the best alignment-based methods, while Cufflinks/Sailfish were markedly worse (~0.21-0.52).
Pseudoalignment specificity/sensitivity: vs Bowtie2 alignments on 20 RSEM simulations of 30M 75-bp paired-end reads (based on GEUVADIS sample NA12716_7), the two agreed exactly for 70.7% of reads; when differing, Bowtie2 reported 8.02 transcripts on average vs 4.96 for kallisto (more specific). Despite being more specific, kallisto had ~100% sensitivity — the true transcript of origin was in the reported set for 99.89% of reads (vs 99.99% Bowtie2).
On real NA12716_7 data: exact agreement 66.22%; for differing reads Bowtie2 aligned 8.94 vs 4.86 transcripts; 86.5% of reads aligned by Bowtie2 vs 90.8% pseudoaligned by kallisto.
Speed: each 30M-read simulation processed on average <7.5 min single-core; total kallisto runtime on the 20 simulated datasets was 11.47 min. A simple word count of a dataset took 75 s (a lower bound), so kallisto’s speed is near-optimal. Memory: max 3.2 Gb RAM per sample; runs on a 1.3-GHz laptop in <10 min.
Allele-specific expression (simulated two-haplotype transcriptome): Spearman correlation 0.833 for kallisto vs 0.848 RSEM, 0.830 eXpress, 0.706 Sailfish — competitive for allele-specific expression. (Caveat: simulation was RSEM-based, which may favor RSEM.)
Bootstrap uncertainty: kallisto resamples multinomially from equivalence-class counts and re-runs EM; each bootstrap ~10 s. On a 216M-read SEQC-MAQCIII human dataset, 40 bootstraps on a single 30M-read sample gave variance correlating R = 0.933 with the across-subsample variance. Naive Poisson/shot-noise variance is shown to be a poor proxy for true variance.
Few parameters: only k-mer length and mean fragment length (the latter estimated at runtime for paired-end reads); performance robust to k chosen. Bias correction uses 6-mers overlapping the 5’ fragment, similar to Roberts et al.
Validation also on SEQC data with qPCR as independent ground truth; sequence-specific bias learning gave a slight improvement in qPCR agreement.

Connections

Methods sibling to rmats and shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection: rMATS detects/quantifies differential alternative splicing events, whereas kallisto provides upstream isoform-level abundance estimates that splicing-quantification pipelines can build on.
Fits the broader splicing-methods tooling landscape; isoform abundances feed percent-spliced-in-style and differential-isoform analyses.
Benchmarked against and contrasted with prior tools: Cufflinks (alignment + EM), Sailfish (k-mer shredding hash), RSEM, eXpress, EMSAR — kallisto’s preservation of k-mer information across reads (unlike Sailfish’s shredding) is the key accuracy advantage.
The bootstrap-based uncertainty quantification is methodologically relevant to any downstream differential-expression or differential-splicing test that needs to factor in inferential variance.

Open questions

How does pseudoalignment perform for splicing-specific tasks (e.g. exon-inclusion / PSI estimation) rather than whole-transcript abundance, where read placement within a transcript matters more?
The authors note kallisto should generalize to other “sequence census” datasets — what is its accuracy on non-RNA-seq assays?
Bias correction is sequence-specific (6-mer priming bias); how much residual positional/fragmentation bias remains and how does it affect isoform-ratio estimates relevant to alternative splicing?
Robustness on highly similar paralogs and complex isoform families remains a limitation shared with all methods — what is the practical impact on splicing-level conclusions?

wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.title: capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the
wiki/sources/capitanchik-2024-from-compu.content:
From computational models of the splicing code to regulatory mechanisms and therapeutic implications
One-line: A review tracing 30 years of computational splicing-code models — from position weight matrices to deep-learning networks like SpliceAI and Pangolin — and how they illuminate splicing regulatory mechanisms and enable rational design of splicing-therapeutics.
Key takeaways

The splicing-code is written in genomic sequence: RNA sequences/structures within exons and introns that determine outcomes (largely via splicing-factors such as snRNAs and rna-binding-proteins) plus DNA sequences that couple splicing to transcription and chromatin. The “ideal” model would predict the full set of transcript isoforms and their frequencies from sequence alone in any cellular context, and approximate underlying biology.
Models evolved through stages: (a) models of core motifs (e.g. MaxEntScan scoring splice-sites); (b) integrative models using ~1,000+ predefined features (Splicing Code, SPANR); (c) end-to-end deep learning that learns features de novo from raw sequence (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice, DeltaSplice); (d) large omics-prediction models spanning whole loci (Borzoi, BigRNA); (e) self-supervised language models (SpliceBERT, Nucleotide Transformer).
Four core motifs form the minimal genetic code read by the spliceosome: 5′ splice site (consensus G|GU(A/G)AGU, recognized by U1 snRNP), branch point ((C/U)U(A/G)A(C/U)), polypyrimidine tract (bound by U2AF65), and 3′ splice site ((C/U)AG|, recognized by U2AF35). Weaker metazoan core motifs provide flexibility for alternative-splicing.
splicing-regulatory-elements (intronic/exonic enhancers and silencers) act position-dependently and multivalently; the same regulator can enhance or repress depending on binding position relative to splice sites. This combinatorial, cotranscriptional-splicing-coupled logic is what models must capture.
Deep models drive splicing-and-disease applications: SpliceAI and Pangolin predict non-coding cryptic-splicing variants (~10% of pathogenic rare-disease mutations); benchmarking informs ClinGen clinical variant classification guidelines.
Splicing is a validated therapeutic target — FDA-approved nusinersen (SSO) and risdiplam (small molecule) promote SMN2 exon 7 inclusion in spinal muscular atrophy; deep models (BigRNA, SpliceAI) are beginning to aid rational design of splice-shifting oligos and splice-gated gene-therapy vectors.

Notable claims / data

Human exons: median length 122 nt (micro-exons as short as 3 nt). Human introns: 30 nt to >1 million nt, median 1,710 nt. Long introns have lower GC than flanking exons; short introns have exon-like high GC and occur in highly expressed genes.
SpliceAI is a 32-layer deep residual CNN trained on human GENCODE; Pangolin uses the same architecture but is trained on multiple species (human, rhesus, mouse, rat) and four tissues (heart, liver, brain, testis), adding tissue-specific splice-site strength outputs.
Context window matters: extending input from 80 nt → 400 nt raised SpliceAI top-k accuracy from ~60% to ~90–95%; extending to 10,000 nt added a further 3–5%. SpliceAI accuracy on lncRNAs (lacking coding bias) was similar to mRNAs (top-k ~84%).
Tissue-specific prediction remains weak: median Spearman correlation with RNA-seq was 0.22 (MTSplice) and 0.43 (Pangolin). DeltaSplice (30,000 nt window, brain RNA-seq from 8 mammals) reached r = 0.69–0.71 on Vex-Seq vs 0.56 for SpliceAI/Pangolin.
Saturation-mutagenesis benchmarking showed all models (SpliceAI, Pangolin, MMSplice) predict exonic variants — especially distal ones — much worse than intronic variants: a key area for improvement.
Clinical benchmarks: SpliceAI AUC 0.959 on BRCA1 near-splice-site variants; up to 0.80 (proximal) / 0.95 (distal intronic) on ABCA4; different algorithms win in different genes (SpliceAI best on ABCA4, others better on MYBPC), motivating consensus/integrative models (CADD-Splice, AbSplice, SpliceVault).
Borzoi (built on Enformer) and BigRNA take up to ~0.5 Mb input and predict tissue-specific RNA-seq coverage plus other functional genomics (CAGE, DNase-seq, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, RBP binding); BigRNA reportedly beats SpliceAI on exon-skipping variants and can predict exon-inclusion-promoting SSOs.
In silico mutagenesis of SpliceAI revealed it indirectly predicts nucleosome positions, contributing to exon-boundary determination (splicing-and-chromatin link). Pangolin mutagenesis: mutations nearly always decrease splice-site usage, implying most exons are near-optimal for splicing accuracy.
Interpretability tools (enhanced integrated gradients, Deep SHAP, attention weights, nucleotide dependency maps) applied to models discovered new biology — e.g. EIG on liver exons identified A1CF as a novel liver-specific splicing regulator (later validated); an interpretable model found stem-loops and G-poor sequences drive exon skipping.

Connections

Central reference for splicing-code modelling; complements core-mechanism concepts: spliceosome, splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-factors, rna-binding-proteins, alternative-splicing.
splicing-and-chromatin: DeepCode found H3K36me3 and H3K79me1 most predictive; SF3B1 interacts with nucleosomes; H3K36me3 enriched in (especially constitutive) exons; SpliceAI implicitly learns nucleosome positions.
cotranscriptional-splicing / post-transcriptional-splicing: RNA Pol II elongation speed affects splicing; first-exon splicing needs cap binding complex; last-exon splicing couples to 3′-end cleavage/polyadenylation; splicing inhibits intronic poly(A) use.
splicing-and-evolution: multi-species training (Pangolin, DeltaSplice, SMsplice) leverages functional conservation; developmentally dynamic alternative-splicing events are most conserved across vertebrates; SMsplice found mammals rely more on regulatory elements, plants on core motifs/structure.
splicing-and-disease / splicing-therapeutics: variant diagnostics (ClinGen, ACMG/AMP), SMA (SMN2/nusinersen/risdiplam), Duchenne, ALS (TDP43 cryptic exons; TDP43–RAVER1 fusion proposal), splice-gated vectors designed with SpliceAI.

Open questions

How to close the gap between high black-box accuracy and mechanistic understanding (explainable-AI toolkit underused for splicing vs transcription); over-reliance on black-box models in the clinic is risky.
Context-dependent / tissue-specific alternative-splicing prediction is limited mainly by scarce training data (few alternative events in healthy-tissue RNA-seq; past focus on cassette exons narrowed data).
Predicting full-length transcript isoforms (not just per-splice-site usage) remains unsolved — needs long-read and uniform-coverage single-cell data (Nanopore, PacBio, Smart-Seq3, VASA-seq, SnISOr-Seq, scNanoGPS).
Need uniform processing, curation and sharing of CLIP/RNA-structure datasets (e.g. Flow, Snakemake/Nextflow workflows); crowdsourced challenges (CAGI, Kaggle/RibonanzaNet) could target alternative-splicing prediction.
Future: multimodal models linking splicing mechanisms (CLIP, RNA structure), outcomes (RNA-seq) and downstream RNA fate (half-life, translation efficiency) in one model.

wiki/sources/che-2026-multiscale-evoluti.title: che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome
wiki/sources/che-2026-multiscale-evoluti.content:
Multiscale evolution of the 3D genome
One-line: A review proposing a micro-to-macro comparative framework that treats 3D genome-architecture as an evolvable trait — rapidly plastic at the cellular/cancer scale, diverging mainly through cis-regulatory-elements within conserved folding frameworks between closely related species, and broadly diverse across the tree of life yet recurrently convergent at higher-order features (see 3d-genome-evolution).
Key takeaways

The mammalian nuclear genome folds into a hierarchy revealed by chromatin-conformation-capture and imaging: chromatin-loops (~10 kb / ~200 kb), topologically-associating-domains (TADs, ~150 kb / ~1 Mb), and chromatin-compartments (multi-Mb, A/B). These layers dynamically modulate the spatial proximity between cis-regulatory-elements and promoters, shaping gene-expression programs and cellular identity.
The central question is how genome-architecture itself evolves. Most prior work compared isolated samples (developmental stage, age, disease) without tracing origins; the paper synthesizes comparative work into a single multiscale evolutionary framework (3d-genome-evolution).
Microscale (cancer): Architecture evolves on the shortest timescale via clonal selection of genomic mutations and plastic epigenetic reprogramming, rewiring chromatin-loops, topologically-associating-domains, and enhancer–promoter contacts (3d-genome-and-disease). Epigenetic changes are a more plastic but equally heritable (across cell divisions) substrate for selection than genomic mutations.
Closely related species: Divergence operates on a longer timescale with stable inheritance, but is restricted to specific loci, driven primarily by sequence-encoded changes in cis-regulatory-elements / TF-binding sites within conserved folding frameworks (the CTCF–cohesin loop-extrusion machinery). Crucially, the chromatin structures themselves are NOT heritable across species — only the molecular information that re-forms them.
Macroscale (tree of life): Genome architectures show striking diversity, yet recurrent higher-order features (loops, domains, Rabl/rosette configurations) arise via convergent evolution through distinct molecular mechanisms — selection acts on architectural function, not on specific molecular components.
A general trend: more intricate 3D organization parallels increasing organismal complexity (proxied by number of cell types), tied to independently evolved multicellularity — though this correlation is mainly demonstrated within animals and unclear in plants.

Notable claims / data

Hierarchy scales (text gives two scale sets): loops ~10 kb (Fig 1) / ~200 kb (intro); TADs ~150 kb (Fig 1) / ~1 Mb (intro); compartments multi-Mb / whole-genome.
Loop extrusion model: cohesin extrudes DNA loops until blocked by convergent pairs of CTCF-binding sites, forming loops and TADs; transcription-related enhancer–promoter loops in mammals are mediated/stabilized by RNA Pol II and TF binding.
A recent comparative analysis suggests 3D genome organization is evolutionarily more flexible (lower constraint) than many other epigenetic markers.
Primate brain: human-specific chromatin loops (enriched for enhancer–enhancer interactions) upregulate neurodevelopmental genes (axon guidance, synapse assembly); young Alu elements are significantly enriched at human-specific TAD boundaries.
Cancer examples: glioblastoma — pervasive SVs create novel enhancer–promoter loops; familial adenomatous polyposis — global loss of promoter–enhancer loops in early colorectal carcinogenesis; DNMT3A mutations in HSPCs — genome-wide CpG hypomethylation promoting stemness; PML-RARα fusion (t15;17, acute promyelocytic leukemia) — aberrant looping that globally condenses the genome; STAG2 (cohesin) mutation in Ewing sarcoma — genome-wide loss of enhancer–promoter interactions; relapsed pediatric B-ALL — comprehensively restructured architecture.
“Structural bottleneck”: in Kras-driven lung adenocarcinoma and pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a transient stage of nuclear compaction and reduced architectural variability may funnel cells to a higher-fitness state, accelerating malignancy.
Convergence across kingdoms: insects use insulator proteins Su(Hw), Ibf1, Ibf2, Cp190 instead of CTCF; non-bilaterians without CTCF (sea walnut Mnemiopsis leidyi) use zinc-finger TFs CTEP1/2; sea walnut lacks compartments; Mueller’s freshwater sponge (Ephydatia muelleri) lacks TADs; placozoans show promoter hubs; Xenopus laevis shows Rabl configuration.
Plants: Arabidopsis thaliana / A. lyrata show little TAD evidence (later higher-res work found >1000 weaker, smaller ~10 kb domains vs human ~100 kb); TADs clearer in rice, foxtail millet, sorghum, tomato, maize, cotton. No CTCF in plants; boundaries enriched for highly expressed genes. Standard protocols detect only global B (pericentromeric, transposon-rich) and A (gene-dense) compartments; functional local compartments need customized local eigenvalue analysis. Plants use linear gene clustering instead of spatial 3D clustering for co-expression — same goal, distinct mechanism. Chromosome-scale Rabl or rosette (chromocenters + nucleolar telomeres) configurations.
Bacteria: no histones/nucleosomes; nucleoid-associated proteins (NAPs) — H-NS (silencing filaments/bridges), SMCs (loop extrusion ori→ter for segregation), DNA-benders Fis/IHF/HU. Chromosome interaction domains (TAD-analogues, boundaries at highly expressed genes/NAP sites), macrodomains (Ori/Ter via MaoP/MatP), interarm interactions as a secondary Hi-C diagonal (SMC loop extrusion); highly responsive to osmolarity/temperature. E. coli active domains vs repressed compartments show distinct architectures.
Condensin II drives gain/loss and disruption of Rabl-like configurations; a hypothesis links Rabl/rosette structures to cellular totipotency (frequent in plant cells, early mammalian embryos) — unverified.
The author’s companion study (Che et al., 2026, Cell) — “evolution of high-order genome architecture revealed from 1,000 species” — underpins the complexity-correlation and linear-clustering claims.

Connections

First source of a new 3D-genome topic — establishes the controlled vocabulary: genome-architecture, chromatin-compartments, topologically-associating-domains, chromatin-loops, chromatin-conformation-capture, 3d-genome-evolution, 3d-genome-and-disease, cis-regulatory-elements. No prior 3D-genome sources to link or contradict.
The three scales relate as a continuum of decreasing plasticity and increasing constraint: cancer (near-absent negative selection, fully reversible epigenetic exploration) → closely related species (sequence-constrained, heritable only via re-forming information) → kingdoms (functional convergence via distinct machinery). Mechanistic parallels exist between cancer and interspecies divergence (both rewire expression by altering local chromatin at TF-binding sites); the key distinction is heritability of the structures.
Distinct from existing wiki topics (splicing, artificial-intelligence). Tangential bridge: the paper cites RNA splicing and protein disorder alongside genome architecture as regulatory layers supporting organismal complexity — a potential cross-link to the splicing topic.

Open questions

Which architectural components evolve under direct selection vs. emerge as biophysical/polymer byproducts?
How do architectural plasticity and sequence-encoded constraints interact — are folding states explored epigenetically before being stabilized genetically (model’s generality unknown)?
To what extent do transposable elements, TF evolution, and SMC dynamics jointly drive divergence? No unified framework yet quantifies their relative impact.
Are recurrent cross-kingdom features (Rabl configurations, boundaries at active genes) convergent optima or shared ancestry?
Can quantitative, species-aware metrics capture architectural intensity (TAD compaction, loop strength) beyond binary presence/absence? Canonical mammalian pipelines fail on non-mammals (e.g. plant local compartments, dinoflagellate condensed heterochromatin).
Can ancestral 3D-genome states be reconstructed (comparative Hi-C + phylogenetics + machine learning)? What are the limits of architectural reorganization a viable cell tolerates?
The regulatory role of conserved features (the universal SMC family; spontaneous aggregation of repressed transposons forming plant telomeres / mammalian repressed compartments) remains elusive.

wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.title: choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
wiki/sources/choquet-2025-the-regulation.content:
The regulation and function of post-transcriptional RNA splicing
One-line: A review arguing that up to ~40% of mammalian introns are spliced post-transcriptionally — largely while transcripts remain chromatin-associated after polyadenylation — and that the timing of splicing is itself a major, regulated layer of gene expression control.
Key takeaways

splicing occurs both co-transcriptionally (during transcription) and post-transcriptionally (after transcription completes); recent long-read and subcellular methods reveal the timing axis with high spatial/temporal resolution. The spliceosome assembly steps are presumed identical for both modes, but it is unknown whether the post-transcriptional delay occurs before or during early assembly, or what triggers eventual splicing.
Roughly 27–40% of mammalian introns are present at high levels in polyadenylated, chromatin-associated RNA, and >70% of expressed genes carry at least one post-transcriptionally retained intron. This makes chromatin-associated post-transcriptional splicing a common feature of gene expression.
Polyadenylated transcripts can stay on chromatin long after synthesis ends; splicing completion appears to be a rate-limiting step for transcript release from chromatin, controlling when mRNA becomes available for nuclear export and translation.
Most retained introns are eventually excised productively rather than degraded; nuclear decay (via the nuclear exosome / PABPN1 hyperadenylation pathway) is the minority fate, affecting only ~4% of protein-coding genes (PUND transcripts).
Detained introns are a specialized class held in the nucleus until specific signals trigger removal — a regulatory reservoir used in neurons, spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, muscle stem cells and heat-shock responses.
Splicing timing is shaped by cis features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary structure) and trans factors/RBPs (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2 snRNA, SNIP1), with phosphorylation (CLK/SRPK), methylation (PRMT5, PRMT1/4) and m6A as key modulators.
Introns flanking alternative exons are preferentially spliced post-transcriptionally, plausibly buying time for correct splice-site choice; Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA folding feed into these outcomes.

Notable claims / data

Up to 40% of mammalian introns are retained after transcription termination; transcriptome-wide estimates put post-transcriptional splicing at 30–45% of introns (Table 1). Most mammalian introns (~70%) are excised on chromatin.
Subcellular TimeLapse-seq (K562): mRNAs have a median chromatin half-life of ~50 min, some genes >170 min — often exceeding transcription time itself; transcripts spend most of their nuclear life on chromatin, not free in nucleoplasm.
Microscopy: an intron in a β-globin reporter was excised ~10-fold faster and more frequently (87%) after release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns, post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Detained introns tend to be shorter, GC-rich, have weaker 5′/3′ splice sites, and are more conserved; ~31% of nuclear-speckle-associated retained introns overlap annotated detained introns vs 12% for lamina-associated ones.
Species differences: Drosophila shows higher splicing completion on chromatin than mouse liver; in Arabidopsis >40% of chromatin RNA is polyadenylated, ~1/3 partially spliced — similar to mammals.
67% of introns flanking alternative exons are excised post-transcriptionally vs 30% of constitutive introns in mESCs.
~20% of human genes have ≥1 intronic poly(A) (PAS) event. Terminal-intron excision is stimulated by 3′-end cleavage/poly(A) signal and may require an optimal poly(A) tail length (PABPN1).
Disease: PRMT5 is aberrantly activated in glioblastoma, excising detained introns in cell-cycle genes to drive proliferation — a hijacked developmental program; PRMT5 inhibition suppresses tumour growth (therapeutic vulnerability). FUS autoregulation via intron retention is disrupted by ALS-causing nuclear-localization-signal mutations.

Connections

Definitive reference for post-transcriptional-splicing and its contrast with cotranscriptional-splicing; central to splicing-and-chromatin (chromatin retention of polyadenylated pre-mRNA, nuclear speckles vs lamina).
Ties alternative-splicing timing to Pol II elongation rate and co-transcriptional RNA structure.
Mechanistic links to splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, CLK/SRPK, PRMTs, SNIP1, U2 snRNA), and to disease/therapeutics via PRMT5 in glioblastoma and FUS in ALS.
splicing-and-evolution: detained introns and intronic PAS show elevated cross-species conservation, implying selected regulatory roles.

Open questions

What signals trigger the eventual post-transcriptional splicing reaction, and is the delay pre- or post-engagement with the spliceosome?
What regulatory features sort partially spliced chromatin-associated transcripts toward productive splicing vs nuclear degradation?
How do nuclear speckles mechanistically couple transcript localization to splicing timing?
To what degree is m6A a universal splicing modulator vs a context-specific one (most marks are not near splice sites and are added post-transcriptionally)?
Do exons regulated by transcription rate overlap with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
Can post-transcriptional splicing be therapeutically targeted in diseases where it is dysregulated, e.g. cancers?

wiki/sources/conesa-2016-survey-of-best-.title: conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices
wiki/sources/conesa-2016-survey-of-best-.content:
A survey of best practices for RNA-seq data analysis
One-line: A broad community review that walks through every major step of an RNA-seq analysis pipeline — experimental design, QC, alignment, quantification, differential expression, alternative splicing, fusion detection, small RNAs, functional profiling, eQTL/sQTL mapping, and data integration — cataloguing the dominant tools and their trade-offs rather than prescribing one pipeline.
Key takeaways

There is no single optimal RNA-seq pipeline; the right choices for library prep, sequencing depth, replicates, mapping, quantification, and DE testing depend on the organism, annotation quality, and biological question.
Experimental-design fundamentals: poly(A) selection vs rRNA depletion (rRNA is >90% of total RNA, mRNA only 1–2%); strand-specific protocols (e.g. dUTP); paired-end and longer reads are preferable for de novo discovery and isoform/alternative-splicing analysis; SE short reads suffice for gene-level expression in well-annotated genomes.
Replicates: minimum three biological replicates for any population inference; without replication, p-values are invalid and analyses are only exploratory. Power depends on effect size, depth, and replicate count.
Three mapping/quantification strategies: (a) map to annotated genome with a gapped/spliced mapper for novel-transcript discovery, (b) map to reference transcriptome with an ungapped mapper for quantification only, (c) de novo assembly when no genome exists.
The alternative-splicing section is the most relevant to this wiki: two method families — isoform-resolution methods (estimate isoform abundance then compare) vs exon/junction-based methods (compare read distributions over exons and junctions directly). rmats and dexseq are cited as exon/junction-based; CuffDiff2 as isoform-based.

Notable claims / data

Expected mapping rates: 70–90% of typical RNA-seq reads map to the human genome (mapper-dependent); transcriptome mapping gives lower total but more multi-mapping reads.
Sequencing depth guidance: ~5M mapped reads can quantify medium-to-high expressed genes; up to 100M for precise low-expression quantification; single cells often profiled at ~1M reads, reliably at 50,000, with cell-type discrimination shown at 20,000 reads.
Statistical power example (Table 1, GTEx whole-blood gene at 70 aligned reads, 30M reads/sample): at fold-change 1.5, power rises from 43% (3 replicates) to 64% (5) to 91% (10); at fold-change 2, 87%/98%/100%.
Technical-replicate reproducibility expected at Spearman R² > 0.9; recommend discarding outliers with >30% disagreement in QC metrics.
DE-method comparisons (Box 4): limma performs well and is fastest; DESeq/edgeR rank genes similarly but are conservative/liberal respectively on FDR; SAMseq controls FDR well but needs ~10 replicates for sensitivity; NOISeq avoids false positives at some sensitivity cost; Cuffdiff/Cuffdiff2 performed “surprisingly poorly,” reflecting the difficulty of transcript-level DE.
Proteomics–RNA-seq correlation is generally low (~0.40), making integration controversial; single-cell transcriptomes typically show only ~3000–8000 expressed genes with 10–50% capture rates.

Connections

Foundational/overview companion to the more specialized splicing-methods sources in this wiki; provides the pipeline context that tool-specific papers assume.
Directly situates rmats (exon-inclusion via junction reads) and dexseq (differential exon usage) within the broader taxonomy of alternative-splicing detection methods — useful cross-reference for those entity pages.
Touches percent-spliced-in-style reasoning implicitly via exon-inclusion-level comparison in junction-based methods.
The eQTL/sQTL subsection (genotype × expression across >50 individuals; splicing QTL via tools like SplicePlot) connects to population-genetics splicing themes, though this review treats it only briefly.

Open questions

Many tools named here (Cufflinks, RSEM, kallisto, STAR, TopHat, SUPPA-era successors, etc.) postdate or were superseded after 2016 — which of the splicing-specific tools (rMATS, DEXseq, DiffSplice, FDM, rSeqDiff, rDiff, DSGSeq) remain current best practice today?
The review flags that isoform-level DE and transcript reconstruction from short reads remain unreliable; how much have long-read (PacBio/Nanopore) approaches, only nascent here, changed the splicing best-practice landscape?
Persistent caveat across the paper: tool results disagree and are parameter-sensitive, especially for low-expression genes — what reproducibility standards have since emerged?

wiki/sources/demirdjian-2020-detecting-a.title: demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/demirdjian-2020-detecting-a.content:
Detecting Allele-Specific Alternative Splicing from Population-Scale RNA-Seq Data
One-line: Introduces PAIRADISE, a paired statistical framework and software that detects allele-specific alternative splicing (ASAS) by aggregating signals across replicates or individuals in a population, boosting power and uniquely capturing rare-variant effects on splicing.
Key takeaways

ASAS analysis tests differential splicing between the two haplotype-derived mRNA pools within an individual, using heterozygous SNPs to assign RNA-seq reads to alleles; because both alleles share one cellular environment, allelic splicing differences should reflect genetic effects.
Prior ASAS approaches were ad hoc: one-individual-at-a-time tests (e.g., Fisher exact test on inclusion vs. skipping counts) that failed to combine signal across people and were confounded by allele-specific gene expression. PAIRADISE fills this methodological gap.
PAIRADISE reframes ASAS detection as differential alternative splicing with paired replicates: the two alleles of an individual are a pair, and individuals sharing a heterozygous SNP are replicates.
It is a hierarchical model: a binomial models RNA-seq inclusion/skipping read counts (accounting for coverage-dependent estimation uncertainty of percent-spliced-in), and a logit-normal models allele-specific inclusion-level variation across individuals. An additive parameter d_i captures the allelic difference; a likelihood ratio test of d_i = 0 (chi-square, 1 df) gives p values, with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR.
Builds directly on the lab’s prior tools: rmats (AS event detection, length-normalization function, and the rMATS paired model it improves upon), rPGA (allele-specific personal-genome alignment), and GLiMMPS (sQTL testing for orthogonal validation).
