A unique advantage over sQTL analysis: ability to detect rare-variant effects on splicing, since ASAS does not require population-level genotype frequency.

Notable claims / data

In simulations vs. paired t test, paired Wilcoxon, rMATS paired test, and Fisher’s combined method, PAIRADISE had the highest AUC/TPR in virtually every setting. With 3 replicates, low variance: AUC 84% (PAIRADISE) vs. 74 / 68 / 63 / 60%; TPR at 5% FPR 61% vs. 26 / 12 / 23 / 4%.
Other models needed roughly 2–3x larger sample sizes to match PAIRADISE’s performance at 3 replicates.
More robust to outliers than a “Fisher’s pooled” strategy; setting si=0 reduces PAIRADISE to an unpaired model similar to EAGLE/rMATS.
GM12878 (6 RNA-seq replicates, single European female): 116 significant ASAS events at FDR <= 10%; 33 in high LD (r2 > 0.8) with GWAS SNPs. 34 of 66 testable events were also significant GLiMMPS sQTLs.
Geuvadis 445 individuals across 5 populations (CEU, FIN, GBR, TSI, YRI): 91 / 82 / 75 / 71 / 143 ASAS events respectively at FDR <= 10%; 13 shared across all five; YRI had the most population-private events (87), many tied to population-specific SNPs (often MAF 0 in Europeans).
ASAS SNPs were significantly enriched for LD with GWAS SNPs vs. random expectation (p < 1e-4 in all six datasets). Example loci: BCL2A1 exon 2 (rs3826007, blood-cell traits / sclerosing cholangitis), LGALS9 exon 6 (rs361497, p=6.7e-16 in CEU; blood protein measurement).
Rare-variant findings: 10 rare-variant-associated ASAS events in GM12878 (e.g., IFI16 rs62621173, CEU MAF 3.4%, p=1.0e-5; FERMT2 rs72686362, MAF ~1%; SCAMP3 rs11557757). Rare-variant events had larger effect sizes (avg allelic PSI difference 25% vs. 18% for common variants in GM12878; Wilcoxon p=0.09).
Conservative design: not constraining si1=si2 reduces detections; the equal-variance user option raised GM12878 from 116 to 133 events (and similarly across Geuvadis populations).

Connections

Methodologically a successor/refinement of the rmats paired model (same lab, Xing); shares the binomial PSI-uncertainty modeling but replaces the correlation-parameter covariance structure with an interpretable additive difference parameter.
Sits alongside sQTL methods (GLiMMPS) as a complementary splicing-methods approach to genetic variation of splicing; the two are orthogonal validation for each other.
Relevant to splicing-and-disease via GWAS colocalization and rare-variant interpretation (e.g., IFI16 linked to Alzheimer age-of-onset).
Tool entity: pairadise.

Open questions

How to model individual-specific allelic differences that depend on covariates (adjacent cis SNPs, trans-regulator levels) — authors propose adding a hierarchical layer but note much larger cohorts are needed.
Short-read limitation: requires a heterozygous SNP outside but near the alternative exon (on the same read); SNPs inside or far from the exon cannot be analyzed — would long-read data relax this?
Extensibility to other allele-specific isoform variation (e.g., allele-specific RNA editing) and to tumor-normal paired cancer designs.

wiki/sources/driessen-2026-conditional-m.title: driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable
wiki/sources/driessen-2026-conditional-m.content:
Conditional Monge Gap enables generalizable single-cell perturbation modelling
One-line: cmonge is a conditional neural optimal-transport framework that learns a single global transport map conditioned on arbitrary covariates (drug, dose, combinations), matching condition-specific models in-sample and generalizing to unseen drugs from compound structure alone — a parameter-efficient approach to single-cell-perturbation-modeling.
Key takeaways

Builds on the Monge Gap (a regularizer that enforces c-optimality of a directly parameterized transport map via the primal objective), avoiding the instability of ICNN/Brenier dual approaches (CellOT) and enabling conditioning through a contextualized loss — see optimal-transport.
A single conditional model replaces many condition-specific models: nine CMonge-Dose models matched 36 condition-specific Monge models on SciPlex despite using 4× fewer models.
Two drug-condition encodings compared: structure-based RDKit fingerprints (194 features from SMILES) and data-driven mode-of-action (MoA) embeddings (10-dim MDS over pairwise Wasserstein distances of perturbed populations). MoA is strongest, but RDKit closes the gap as conditions scale.
Enables out-of-sample (OOS) prediction for unseen drugs/doses; combinatorial perturbations handled via a permutation-invariant DeepSets average-pooling layer.
Highly parameter-efficient vs single-cell foundation-models and vs chemCPA: CMonge models range from ~23,000 to ~580,000 parameters vs chemCPA’s ~1.37 million.
Validated across two modalities: SciPlex3 scRNA-seq (3 human cancer cell lines, 187 drugs, 4 doses) and the 4i multiplexed protein-imaging dataset (40-plex, 2 melanoma lines, 35 therapies incl. combinations).

Notable claims / data

SciPlex OOS leave-nine-drugs-out, highest dose (10,000 nM): CMonge-DD-MoA R²=0.900, Wasserstein=3.873, MMD=0.013; CMonge-DD-RDKit R²=0.781, MMD=0.020; chemCPA R²=0.760, MMD=0.195 — CMonge widely outperforms chemCPA especially on distributional (heterogeneity) metrics.
At full scale CMonge trained on all 187 drugs / 748 conditions; in-sample achieves avg R² > 0.8 and R² > 0.6 for all drugs with both RDKit and MoA embeddings.
CMonge captures distributional heterogeneity (spread/multimodality) better than 36 condition-specific Monge models (Wasserstein), whereas chemCPA predictions often collapse to the mean.
OOS performance correlates with similarity of the test drug’s effect to nearest training drugs; trametinib (MEK/kinase MoA) and mocetinostat (most distant MoA embedding) were hardest.
Pathway enrichment from learned transport maps recovers ground-truth pathways for trametinib (MAPK signalling); hypergeometric overlap P=1.0×10⁻⁴⁵.
Caveats: small effect sizes in 4i (identity baseline R² > 0.6) limited gains; CMonge struggled on single-drug 4i OOS; highest dose hardest; simple average pooling may miss higher-order synergistic effects; generalization to new cell types / OOD conditions / batch effects remains open.
Implementation: Sinkhorn divergence + Monge Gap regularizer, squared Euclidean cost, 4-layer MLP, GELU, AdamW, OTT-JAX; SciPlex uses a frozen autoencoder to a 50-dim latent where OT operates.

Connections

artificial-intelligence — AI/ML method for biology; a generative/predictive modelling of cell-state distributions.
optimal-transport — extends the Monge Gap (Uscidda & Cuturi) to a conditional/global estimator.
single-cell-perturbation-modeling — sibling methods compared/cited: chemCPA (main conditional baseline), CellOT, scPRAM, scGen, PerturbNet, GEARS; contrasts with single-cell foundation-models (scGPT, scBERT) on parameter efficiency. Shared concept with TxPert and SIGnature.
ai-in-genomics — single-cell RNA-seq perturbation response prediction.

Open questions

How well does CMonge generalize to entirely new cell types and out-of-distribution conditions, beyond unseen drugs/doses within seen cell lines?
Would more expressive permutation-invariant pooling (attention) meaningfully improve combinatorial/synergistic perturbation modelling?
Can unbalanced OT or flow-matching extensions add robustness to outliers, batch effects, and rare conditions?
How does it scale and perform when fine-tuned on giga-scale screens or patient-derived organoids?

wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.title: editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer
wiki/sources/editorial-2025-rna-splicing.content:
RNA splicing — a central layer of gene regulation
One-line: A framing editorial introducing a Nature Reviews online collection, surveying how recent technological and computational advances (cryo-EM, sequencing, machine learning) have deepened understanding of splicing as a central layer of gene regulation.

Note: This is a short framing editorial introducing a journal collection across Nature Reviews Genetics and Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology. It is best used as an overview/orientation piece rather than a primary research source.

Key takeaways

Most eukaryotic genes are organized as a “mosaic” of introns and exons; splicing removes introns and ligates exons to produce mature mRNA, and is essential for accurate translation of DNA sequence information.
The spliceosome is a dynamic ribonucleoprotein complex of five snRNAs and numerous proteins that recognizes conserved motifs at exon–intron boundaries and carries out a two-step transesterification reaction; recent cryo-EM has resolved multiple conformational states.
alternative-splicing (regulated inclusion/exclusion of exons) greatly expands the coding potential of the genome, producing isoforms that affect mRNA stability, localization and translation.
Timing matters: splicing occurs cotranscriptional-splicing or post-transcriptional-splicing, and depends on nuclear compartmentalization via biomolecular condensates such as nuclear speckles and paraspeckles, which concentrate splicing-factors.
Genetic variation disrupting splice-sites or splicing-regulatory-elements underlies splicing-and-disease; splicing-therapeutics (antisense oligonucleotides, small molecules) is an increasingly validated treatment strategy.

Notable claims / data

More than 95% of human multi-intron genes undergo alternative-splicing.
“Split genes” were discovered in 1977 (Richard Roberts and Phillip Sharp), earning the 1993 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
The spliceosome comprises five small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs) plus numerous associated proteins.
Spliceosome catalysis is a two-step transesterification reaction.
Genetic variants can cause exon skipping, intron retention, or activation of cryptic splice sites; mutations in core spliceosomal proteins can compromise splicing fidelity or efficiency.
The splicing-code is defined as “a set of rules, an algorithm or a computational model that could predict the splice isoforms, and their frequencies, produced from any transcribed gene in a specific cellular context,” encompassing cis-regulatory elements, RNA structure and trans-acting contributors.

Connections

splicing-therapeutics: ASOs and small molecules applied to genetic disorders, neurodegenerative diseases and certain cancers.
splicing-code / machine learning: deep learning is now a powerful tool for modelling the code and predicting variant effects on splicing, though interpretability remains a challenge; this can guide drug development and clinical diagnostics.
splicing-and-evolution: long-read sequencing reveals striking differences in alternative splicing extent between eukaryotes, and heritable variation in alternative-splicing patterns may contribute to adaptive evolutionary change.
splicing-and-chromatin / transcription: long-read sequencing and imaging illuminate splicing timing and its dynamic interplay with transcription and RNA processing.
rna-binding-proteins / splicing-factors organized within phase-separated condensates regulate splicing.

Open questions

How to improve the interpretability of deep-learning models of the splicing code.
How splicing shapes phenotypic outcomes and contributes to human disease (the editorial frames researchers as “poised to further decode” this).
The full regulatory logic linking splicing timing, nuclear compartmentalization, and isoform output.

wiki/sources/fu-2014-context-dependent-c.title: fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing
wiki/sources/fu-2014-context-dependent-c.content:
Context-dependent control of alternative splicing by RNA-binding proteins
One-line: A foundational Nature Reviews Genetics review (Fu & Ares) laying out the emerging “rules” by which sequence-specific RNA-binding proteins combinatorially and context-dependently control alternative splicing, and why a simple readable “splicing code” remains out of reach.
Key takeaways

The central thesis is that each alternative splicing event is governed by multiple rna-binding-proteins acting combinatorially, and because every cell type expresses a distinct array of RBPs, the regulatory meaning of any given RNA target sequence is exceedingly cell-type-dependent. RBPs also modulate one another’s binding activities on splicing-regulatory-elements.
There is no clean division of positive vs negative regulators: whether an RBP enhances or silences splicing depends heavily on the location of its binding site relative to the regulated exon (exon vs upstream-intron vs downstream-intron).
A truly readable “splicing code” is not yet attainable from genome sequence: the same short degenerate motif can be bound by multiple proteins/isoforms, the same protein reads the same sequence differently in different cell types, and machine-learning predictions are limited because bulk tissues superimpose splicing responses of multiple cell types.
splicing-regulatory-elements (SREs) are distinguished from the core splicing signals (5’SS, branchpoint, 3’SS) and are subdivided into ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS depending on exonic vs intronic location and enhancer vs silencer function.
Classic trans-acting splicing-factors are the SR proteins (generally exon-binding, purine-rich, positive) and the hnRNPs (e.g. hnrnpa1, ptbp1/PTB, generally repressive), but loss-of-function profiling shows roughly equal numbers of induced inclusion and skipping events for both classes.
Regulators ultimately act through the core spliceosome — by modulating snRNP assembly/maturation, competing for limiting machinery, blocking the exon-to-intron-definition transition, or modulating U1 dynamics.

Notable claims / data

“Exon definition”: a weak 3’SS is more efficiently recognized when a strong 5’SS lies within ~100-150 nucleotides downstream (U1 snRNP at 5’SS and U2 snRNP at 3’SS mutually enhance recognition).
Most regulatory studies focus on 200-300 nt adjacent to splice sites, but remote/distal regulatory sequences can be just as important (Rbfox RNA “bridges”), implying exome sequencing misses many disease-relevant deep-intronic SRE mutations.
Proteomic crosslinking studies indicate up to ~1,000 proteins can be directly crosslinked to mRNA in HeLa cells or mouse ES cells, many lacking canonical RNA-binding domains.
CLIP requires RNA and protein to be within ~1 Å for UV crosslinking; refinements include PAR-CLIP (higher crosslinking efficiency) and iCLIP (individual-nucleotide resolution). Table 1 catalogs CLIP-derived binding motifs for ~25 regulators (e.g. NOVA YCAY; RBFOX1/2 GCAUG; MBNL1/2 YGCY; PTB CU-rich; srsf1 GGAGGA; hnRNP A1 GNNAGN; U2AF65 pyrimidine-rich).
U1 snRNP is ~10-fold more abundant than other splicing snRNPs in mammalian cells; extended U1-5’SS base-pairing (induced by hnRNP L and hnRNP A1) can act as a repressive complex.
Position-polarity rule (first shown for NOVA): intronic binding upstream of an exon represses inclusion while binding downstream activates inclusion; mechanism still unresolved (tethered RBFOX1 work shows distinct domain requirements for activation vs repression).
hnRNP C directly competes with U2AF65 at Alu-associated 3’SSs to protect the transcriptome from Alu exonization.
Signaling-to-splicing: the Akt-SRPK-SR axis transduces EGF signaling to global splicing changes (RNAi of SRPK1/2 abolishes most EGF-induced events); SR protein phosphorylation is controlled by cytoplasmic SRPKs and nuclear CLKs; Ca2+/CaMK IV regulates splicing via the CaRRE motif and hnRNP L.
Caveat threaded throughout: minigene/reporter and in vitro selection results often fail to generalize because one reporter samples only one regulatory context; affinity purification shows SRE-to-RBP relationships are frequently not one-to-one (direct vs indirect interactions are hard to separate).

Connections

This is an upstream/foundational review for the wiki’s regulatory-biology cluster: it gives mechanistic grounding for rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors, and splicing-regulatory-elements, and complements the other regulation reviews rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
The “splicing code” framing and its limits connect to computational/ML directions discussed in capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and to the broader cataloguing of RBP motifs (CLIP/Bind-n-Seq) that underpins tools like spliceai.
Specific entities it discusses already have wiki pages: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, srsf1; the proteome-expansion theme links to nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome.
RNA-structure dependence of RBP access connects to RNA secondary structure / dscam-style competing-structure regulation and to G-quadruplex work in peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes.

Open questions

What exactly is the molecular mechanism for the intronic position-polarity effect (why upstream = repress, downstream = activate), and which auxiliary proteins mediate it?
How can multi-RBP, cell-type-specific combinatorial codes be deconvolved from bulk tissue data so that a predictive splicing code becomes feasible?
How much disease-relevant regulatory information lies in distal/deep-intronic SREs that exome sequencing cannot capture, and can CRISPR genome engineering in native context map it?
How are RBPs with multiple jobs (splicing plus transcription/stability/localization, e.g. SRSF2 in Pol II pause release) functionally disentangled?
Do kinetic (vs thermodynamic) binding preferences of RBPs account for regulatory specificity currently missed by standard binding assays?

wiki/sources/gold-2026-scoring-gene-impo.title: gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting
wiki/sources/gold-2026-scoring-gene-impo.content:
Scoring gene importance by interpreting single-cell foundation models
One-line: SIGnature scores each gene’s functional importance to a single cell by computing gradient-based attributions over the latent embeddings of pretrained scRNA-seq single-cell-foundation-models, yielding a standardized, cross-dataset metric that beats expression-based signature scoring and surfaces shared disease mechanisms.
Key takeaways

Reframes gene importance as a model-interpretability problem: applies explainable-AI attribution methods (integrated gradients, input×gradient, DeepLIFT) to single-cell-foundation-models to measure each gene’s contribution to a cell’s position in latent space. Attribution magnitude need not track expression (e.g. high for the TF FOXP3, low for the abundant transcript MALAT1).
Attributions emphasize regulatory/marker genes (including lowly-expressed transcription factors), deemphasize ribosomal/mitochondrial genes, and are robust to technical artifacts (sequencing depth, UMI counts, 50% dropout → 93% top-gene overlap), enabling comparison across experiments against a single standardized FM embedding.
Shipped as the SIGnature Python package (built on Captum) supporting a suite of FMs and explainability methods; in-depth analyses use the SCimilarity FM + integrated gradients. Attributions are precomputed and queried as fast vector operations.
Demonstrated by querying the ~100-gene MS1 monocyte signature (severe-COVID-19/sepsis-linked) across atlas-scale data, revealing MS1 activation in three previously unassociated hyperinflammatory diseases: Kawasaki disease (KD), severe fever with thrombocytopenia syndrome (SFTS), and hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH).
Generated a testable hypothesis that was experimentally validated: serum from KD patients induces the MS1 phenotype in vitro (emergency myelopoiesis, increased HLA-DRlow monocytes).

Notable claims / data

Scores 22 million cells in minutes (vs hours/days for conventional methods); the MS1 query ran across 412 human disease studies, focusing on 2.3M predicted monocytes/macrophages (top 10% MS1 score = “MS1-like”).
Signature-scoring benchmark: mean attributions achieved the highest F1 in 23/32 cell-type prediction tests, beating background-based (ANS, Scanpy) and rank-based (JASMINE, UCell) methods. CD8+ signature: attributions cleanly separate CD8+ from CD4+ T cells across studies.
Cross-study NMF: 90% of attribution configs recovered a Treg-associated factor (98% with FOXP3 in top-10 loadings) vs 58%/4% for expression-based NMF. Benefit is FM-representation-dependent: helps for well-represented cell types (lung/HLCA) but not poorly-represented ones (corneal epithelium).
Benchmarking: seven FMs (scFoundation, scGPT, SCimilarity, scTab-SSL + variants, scVI-CZI) × three XAI methods; MLP-based FMs much faster than transformers; cell-type-label models (SCimilarity, fine-tuned scTab) best boost marker-gene importance.
KD case: MS1-like cells significantly decrease after IVIG (Wilcoxon P<0.01); expression-based consensus NMF independently recovers a KD MS1 factor (S100A9, RETN). IgG alone did not change MS1 induction → IVIG mechanism remains unclear.
Caveats stated: attributions are interpretive (not ideal for clustering), have less-interpretable units than raw counts, and inherit the quality/biases of the underlying FM and XAI method.

Connections

artificial-intelligence, ai-in-genomics, foundation-models — this work is about INTERPRETING single-cell foundation models via attribution, not training a new generative FM.
New concepts: single-cell-foundation-models, model-interpretability (XAI/attribution methods: IG, IxG, DeepLIFT, contrasted with perturbation-based SHAP/LIME).
Tool entity: signature-tool (the SIGnature Python package).
Sibling single-cell methods in the wiki: CMonge and TxPert (perturbation/response modeling) — SIGnature is complementary, providing interpretation/feature-attribution rather than perturbation prediction; relies on existing FMs (SCimilarity, scGPT, scVI, scTab, scFoundation).

Open questions

How well do attributions transfer to FMs/cell types poorly represented in training data, and can task-specific FMs improve them?
What is the mechanism by which IVIG reduces MS1-like cells in KD, given IgG alone showed no effect?
Can the framework extend robustly to perturbation-based explainers (SHAP via gene masking) and other modalities (chromatin accessibility, spatial/multimodal omics)?
How should attribution units (a complex transformation of expression) be made more directly interpretable for biologists?

wiki/sources/groover-2026-mapping-cis-re.title: groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at
wiki/sources/groover-2026-mapping-cis-re.content:
Mapping cis-regulatory mutations at scale in sorghum enables modulation of gene expression
One-line: A protoplast-based massively-parallel-reporter-assay (MPRA) measures the effects of >30,000 CRISPR-mimicking mutations across native promoters and 5′ UTRs of three sorghum photosynthesis genes (PsbS, Raf1, SBPase), yielding base-pair-resolution maps of gene-specific cis-regulatory control and compact, nontransgenic edits that strongly boost protein output.
Key takeaways

Gene expression is most tunable within a ~500-bp “core promoter” window around the TSS (roughly −400 bp onward); the distal promoter (<−400 bp) shows low mutational variance and low replicate reproducibility — an instance of cis-regulatory-grammar.
Mutation effects in the core promoter are reproducible across biological replicates (Pearson r=0.68 overall; r=0.75 for deletions, r=0.62 for insertions) and predictive of protein output (r=0.80 vs orthogonal nanoluciferase assay).
Compact deletions and motif insertions can strongly activate expression (>30-fold over WT), outperforming a 585-bp industry-standard transgenic enhancer (3×ENH).
Mutation–effect relationships are gene specific: the same heuristic does not transfer across PsbS, Raf1 and SBPase — arguing for tailored, per-gene regulatory maps.
A fine-tuned genomic language model (GPN) gives only modest variant-effect prediction (best for disruptive 10–15-bp deletions, r=0.76; weak for activating insertions, r=0.39) and fails to capture hypermorphic effects, underscoring the value of direct MPRA measurement.

Notable claims / data



30,000 mutations total across 3 genes (~10,000/gene): PsbS ~10,096, Raf1 ~7,796, SBPase ~13,974.


Mutation classes emulate CRISPR editing outcomes: deletions (~3,398/library), A>G/T>C substitutions emulating base editors (~4,858/library), and cis-genic motif insertions (8–25 bp) emulating prime editing (~2,179/library).
Workflow: tens of millions of etiolated S. bicolor cv. RTx430 mesophyll protoplasts transfected with barcoded plasmid libraries (full gene context: 2-kb promoter + 5′ UTR + introns + 1-kb downstream + 15-bp barcode); barcode RNA/DNA sequencing; linear regression infers per-variant effect.
PsbS: hypomorphic deletions cluster at −180 to −120 (conserved G-box/I-box light-response motifs); top hypermorphic edit is a 57-bp deletion at −90 (~33-fold), beating the 3×ENH enhancer (~12-fold).
SBPase can only be upregulated by motif insertions (not deletions); a 10-bp insertion at +30 and a 15-bp dual-I-box insertion at −45 both exceed 16-fold and beat 3×ENH (~8-fold).
Hypomorphic PsbS deletions reproduce NPQ phenotypes in rice in planta; hypermorphic edits not yet validated in whole sorghum plants.

Connections

functional-single-cell-genomics — hub topic; this is a functional-genomics MPRA paper.
cis-regulatory-elements — directly maps and dissects cis-regulatory DNA (promoters, 5′ UTRs, G-box/I-box motifs, TFBS).
splicing-methods — shares the MPRA methodology referenced there; this is a plant promoter/UTR MPRA. See massively-parallel-reporter-assay.
New concepts: massively-parallel-reporter-assay (core method), cis-regulatory-grammar (gene-specific position/motif rules); applied toward quantitative trait engineering (QTE) for nontransgenic crop improvement.

Open questions

Do MPRA-identified hypermorphic edits (esp. the 57-bp PsbS deletion at −90) translate to gene-edited whole sorghum plants and yield gains in the field?
How tissue- and condition-dependent are these maps, given protoplasts capture mainly tissue/condition-independent regulation?
Can genomic language models be trained (transcriptome + variant data jointly) to robustly predict activating variants across genes?
What is the mechanism of strong activating deletions — disrupting repressive modules, reconfiguring core enhancers, or creating de novo activating elements?

wiki/sources/han-2026-generalizable-muta.title: han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across
wiki/sources/han-2026-generalizable-muta.content:
Generalizable mutation-effect prediction across adaptive immune recognition via unified multimodal framework
One-line: uniair is a modular, multimodal sequence–structure framework that predicts mutation-induced binding free-energy changes (ΔΔG) generalizably across antibody–antigen and TCR–pMHC immune recognition, reaching state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal task-specific tuning.
Key takeaways

Introduces uniair, a unified framework with three parts: a standardized mutation-centric interface-construction toolkit, the ESSM sequence–structure fusion model (S-Former co-attention transformer coupling a protein language model (ESM2) with geometric structure), and scalable extensions (MoFPE expert mixture + a latent adapter for predicted structures).
Frames mutation-effect-prediction for immune-recognition as a single multimodal problem rather than per-task models, addressing the heterogeneity that limits prior sequence-only or structure-only approaches — relevant to ai-in-genomics and foundation-models.
MoFPE (mixture-of-frozen predictive experts) integrates four frozen structure-pretrained experts (ESSM, GearBind, RDE-DDG, PPIformer) via a structure-aware gating network, avoiding backbone retraining or manual weighting — an instance of multimodal-learning.
UniAIR-LT adds a lightweight latent adapter aligning predicted-structure (ESMFold/OpenFold) embeddings to experimental ones, extending use to cases where experimental structures are unavailable or incomplete.
Demonstrated across antibody maturation (SARS-CoV-2), viral antigen escape (Lassa GPC), TCR–pHLA peptide optimization (KRAS-G12D) coupled with FEP, and zero-shot mutational-pattern analysis.

Notable claims / data

On SKEMPIv2 (n=5,820 mutations, 340 complexes, threefold CV) UniAIR reached state of the art: PCC 0.684, SPC 0.589, RMSE 1.575, MAE 1.077; consistent on single- vs multipoint mutations (PCC 0.682 vs 0.677). ESSM backbone alone: PCC 0.635 / SPC 0.533, beating PLMs ESM2 (0.523/0.410) and SaProt (0.546/0.438).
Robust generalization on two independent, non-overlapping test sets: HER2 antibody–antigen (n=419, PCC 0.521) and TCR–pMHC ATLAS (n=87, PCC 0.736), where competing methods (e.g. RDE-DDG) degraded on one set.
Zero-shot antibody optimization of P36-5D2 vs SARS-CoV-2 spike: of 19 experimentally validated affinity-enhancing mutants, 63.2% ranked in top 10% and 89.5% in top 20%; quadruple mutant T31W/N57L/R103M/L104F ranked top 2.07%.
KRAS-G12D peptide optimization: UniAIR alanine scan correlated with FEP at PCC 0.556 / SPC 0.513; FEP validated 6/13 single-, 3/8 double-, 5/7 triple-point designs with improved peptide–HLA and TCR–pHLA binding, supported by 300 ns triplicate MD.
UniAIR-LT improved predicted-structure ΔΔG (ESMFold PCC 0.590→0.661; OpenFold 0.663→0.673) and rescued the worst mis-predicted complex 5C6T (PCC 0.053→0.421). Caveat: ΔΔG-to-phenotype mapping is system/context dependent; protein–protein interfaces only (no small molecules/nucleic acids).
Beat LLM agent-with-tools workflows on SKEMPIv2; dropping structure-dependent experts roughly doubles inference speed with minimal loss (0.581 vs 0.589).

Connections

Sits in artificial-intelligence; builds on protein-language-models (ESM2) and the foundation-models paradigm applied to ai-in-genomics.
Adaptive-immune mutation focus relates to data-quality-and-bias (structural data scarcity/imbalance for TCR–pMHC, high-affinity dataset bias) and contrasts with agent-based tool aggregation.
Resource/model sibling to other AI-method papers; safety-relevant since it touches viral escape and immunogen design — cf. hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation.

Open questions

Extending beyond protein–protein interfaces to small molecules and nucleic acids that also shape immune recognition.
Calibrating the ΔΔG→immune-phenotype mapping (T-cell activation thresholds, escape) with phenotypic supervision per system.
Prospective wet-lab validation (SPR, T-cell activation assays) and integration with clinical/translational datasets.

wiki/sources/hassan-2026-a-safety-centri.title: hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation
wiki/sources/hassan-2026-a-safety-centri.content:
A safety-centric perspective on innovation and risk in the use of artificial intelligence in genomics
One-line: A safety/governance Perspective arguing that generative-ai foundation-models for biological sequences (ai-in-genomics) amplify data bias and dual-use-risk across the whole innovation pipeline, and proposing layered technical safeguards plus an institutional risk–benefit framework to embed ai-safety and ai-governance from project inception.
Key takeaways

foundation-models for DNA/RNA/protein (e.g., Nucleotide Transformer, Evo family) are transformative for ai-in-genomics — identifying regulatory elements, enabling multimodal DNA–RNA–protein design (CRISPR-Cas systems, transposon arrays) and generating the first viable AI-designed bacteriophage genome — but the same capabilities raise dual-use-risk from theoretical to immediate.
data-quality-and-bias: genomic datasets are dominated by European individuals, so models perform poorly on under-represented populations; polygenic risk scores from European cohorts lose 15–20% predictive accuracy in African groups, and the gap persists as datasets grow — a systemic risk that can propagate inequity into clinical tools.
Misuse risk must be analysed across the entire biorisk chain (design–build–test–learn), not as a single point; current bottlenecks (scarce biological data, lab expertise, restricted materials) confine credible end-to-end misuse mostly to state-level or highly resourced actors, while nonstate actors lack means to translate AI designs into physical harm.
ai-safety technical safeguards (data filtering to remove human pathogens, tamper resistance, weight pruning, concept erasure / “SafeGenie”, safety wrappers, Guaranteed Safe AI) are all useful but insufficient alone — they rely on known risk profiles and fail on novel sequences with no natural analogues.
biosecurity framing: the paper deliberately avoids a single ethical/governance framework, instead using a biosecurity perspective grounded in biosafety and dual-use principles, plus a “One Health” approach covering human, animal, and environmental threats.
ai-governance: the EU AI Act classifies AI-driven genomics as “high-risk” (mandating bias assessment, transparency, risk management), yet current FM development largely fails to meet these standards; the authors propose an institutional risk–benefit framework (adapted from the Robert Koch Institute DURC process) applied from project inception.

Notable claims / data

AI adoption: ~1 in 9 industry job postings (as of May 2025) require AI-related skills; the transformer architecture was introduced in 2017; the term “foundation models” was coined in 2021 (GPT/BERT-style).
Distribution-shift failures: Nucleotide Transformer embeddings lose 23% accuracy on rare tumour types absent from pre-training; models trained mostly on European genomes perform 18–41% worse on South Asian regulatory elements; even Evo fails to generalise to eukaryotes, producing nonviable sequences in 94% of yeast chassis trials unless trained across the full tree of life.
Raising the “ceiling of harm”: ML-designed viral libraries yield ~10× more successful variants than traditional NNK libraries; ML-assisted directed evolution finds high-fitness variants more efficiently than standard methods. FMs may overcome evolutionary trade-offs (transmissibility vs virulence), enabling engineered pathogens worse than natural ones — including low-probability, existential-scale threats (caveat: largely speculative).
King et al. created and lab-tested the first AI-generated phages; some showed greater fitness and faster lysis than wild-type — proving genomic design is practically feasible. Fine-tuning has been shown to restore generation of sequences excluded from training data (e.g., Evo excluded eukaryotic viruses, but neither Evo 1 nor Evo 2 documents post-fine-tuning generative capability).
Screening is fragile: AI-designed biosequences are more diverse than natural ones and may evade database-comparison screening; “GeneBreaker” jailbreaks DNA language models via pathogenicity guidance; adversarial changes to mass spectra can make models misclassify cancer biomarkers.
Model opacity: interpretability tools (gradient attribution, in silico mutagenesis, attention visualisation, latent probing) give post-hoc diagnostics but suffer noisy attributions, lack of causality, poor generalisation; architectures like ExplaiNN and ARGMINN embed motif-level priors to make interpretability integral.

Connections

This is the first source of a new artificial-intelligence topic, distinct from the existing splicing topic; it establishes the AI-safety/governance backbone and seeds all eight new-vocab concept pages.
Safety concerns connect across the innovation pipeline: data-quality-and-bias at training → opacity/interpretability at the model layer → dual-use-risk / misuse at deployment → biosecurity screening at the synthesis stage → ai-governance (EU AI Act, Ethics by Design, institutional risk–benefit) wrapping the whole lifecycle.
generative-ai and foundation-models are the technical substrate; ai-safety supplies the mitigation toolkit; ai-governance and biosecurity supply the institutional/regulatory layer — the paper’s thesis is that no single layer suffices.

Open questions

Can interpretability move beyond correlation to genuine causal mechanistic insight, and how do we validate that interpretable features reflect true biology rather than artifacts?
How to resolve the paradox that interpretable AI both exposes population-specific markers for fairness correction and increases misuse/discrimination risk?
Can Guaranteed Safe AI frameworks with quantitative guarantees be implemented for real-world synthesis pipelines and lab automation without inhibiting beneficial innovation?
What institutional decision-making and escalation protocols best handle risks that emerge dynamically during model development?
What empirical evidence distinguishes theoretical risks from demonstrated capabilities in multiobjective biological engineering?
How can FMs be trained across all evolutionary domains to stay functional, and what architectural innovations address eukaryotic genome modelling?

wiki/sources/hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pr.title: hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pre-mrna-splicing-methods
wiki/sources/hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pr.content:
Spliceosomal Pre-mRNA Splicing: Methods and Protocols
One-line: An edited Methods in Molecular Biology volume (vol. 1126) that bundles seven introductory reviews on splicing biology with twenty-one bench and computational protocol chapters, serving as a practical handbook for studying spliceosome assembly, catalysis, and alternative pre-mRNA splicing.
Key takeaways

A protocol compendium, not a single study: Part I gives theory across seven chapters (the splicing reaction, diversity/evolution of spliceosomal systems, spliceosomal assembly, alternative splicing, its regulation, cotranscriptional splicing, and chromatin–splicing), while Part II provides 21 step-by-step experimental protocols.
Framing in the preface: of ~25,000 human genes, more than 90% are believed to produce alternatively spliced transcripts; early estimates put ~15% of single-base mutations as splicing-altering, while newer estimates suggest up to 60% of known disease mutations could act through changes in pre-mRNA splicing.
In vitro biochemistry protocols cover preparation of splicing-competent spliceosome nuclear extracts (HeLa) and yeast whole-cell extracts, in vitro splicing assays in mammalian extract, kinetic analysis of splicing in HeLa nuclear extract, coupling RNAP II transcription to splicing/polyadenylation, isolation of spliceosomal assembly intermediates, and U4 snRNA complementation in S. cerevisiae extracts.
Protein/RNA tooling protocols include splinted ligation of synthetic RNA with RNA ligase, expression/purification of splicing-factors from mammalian cells, single-molecule (SNAP-tag/TIRF) approaches to spliceosome assembly and catalysis, and quantifying the stoichiometric ratio of spliceosome components assembled on pre-mRNA.
Cell-based and manipulation protocols cover cell-based splicing of minigenes, antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) methods to modulate splicing (exemplified by splicing correction in spinal muscular atrophy via smn2), and yeast genetics (temperature-sensitive prp/RNA mutants, Synthetic Genetic Array) to dissect splicing mechanisms.
High-throughput / readout protocols cover medium-throughput fluorescently labeled RT-PCR for alternative-splicing, ChIP approaches to establish the cotranscriptional nature of splicing (see cotranscriptional-splicing), iCLIP-Seq for global protein–RNA interaction mapping at single-nucleotide resolution, computational mining of public databases (UCSC Table Browser, SAMtools, liftOver, GenomicTools), linking RNA secondary structure to splicing regulation, and a review chapter classifying RNA-Seq tools for studying splicing.

Notable claims / data

Preface estimates: >90% of human genes alternatively spliced; ~15% of single-base mutations historically estimated to alter splicing; up to 60% of known disease-causing mutations may act via pre-mRNA splicing changes.
Chapter 26 (Alamancos, Agirre & Eyras) reviews and classifies the “plethora” of RNA-Seq tools by operation — read mapping/assignment, event/isoform quantification (with or without annotation), transcript reconstruction/de novo assembly, differential splicing/expression comparison across conditions, and visualization — providing a decision framework rather than a single new algorithm.
Chapter 28 (Barash & Vaquero Garcia) addresses computational prediction of alternative splicing, building on machine-learning “splicing code” work; Part I likewise discusses a computationally derived neuronal splicing code (adapted from Barash et al.) for exon skipping vs. inclusion.
Genome statistics quoted in the evolution chapters (e.g., human and Arabidopsis intron/genome comparisons) ground the cross-species framing.
Caveat: the chapters are presented as guides to commonly used, established methods; the volume’s value is reusable protocol knowledge rather than a single novel hypothesis-driven result.

Connections

Belongs to the splicing-methods cluster as a foundational protocol reference predating modern RNA-Seq event tools; complements method/tool sources such as shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (rMATS / percent-spliced-in) and the tool-survey angle of sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Its review chapters on alternative splicing and its regulation overlap thematically with lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative.
Cotranscriptional/chromatin chapters connect to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin; ASO/SMA and disease framing connect to splicing-therapeutics, splicing-and-disease, and smn2.
iCLIP and splicing-factor purification chapters connect to rna-binding-proteins and splicing-factors; RNA-structure chapter connects to rna-structure-and-splicing.

Open questions

How well do the 2014-era in vitro and RT-PCR readouts integrate with current long-read and single-cell RNA-Seq pipelines?
The RNA-Seq tool classification (Ch. 26) predates many later tools (e.g., rMATS, MAJIQ, SUPPA) — which of its operational categories remain the right taxonomy today?
Which of the biochemical assembly-intermediate and single-molecule protocols have since been superseded by cryo-EM structural approaches to the spliceosome?

wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.title: kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated
wiki/sources/kalsotra-2011-functional-co.content:
Functional consequences of developmentally regulated alternative splicing
One-line: A 2011 Nature Reviews Genetics review surveying how regulated alternative splicing networks reshape transcriptomes during development, differentiation, and cell stress across yeast, worms, flies, and vertebrates, and what physiological consequences those splicing transitions actually have.
Key takeaways

Frames the central question of the field: of the ~19,000 alternatively spliced human genes, what fraction of mRNA isoforms have a real functional impact? Much diversity is low-abundance, non-conserved stochastic noise, but a growing set of transitions are physiologically determinative.
Distinguishes two roles for alternative-splicing: determinative (the splicing change itself drives the biological outcome) versus permissive (splicing provides mRNA/protein variability that a downstream mechanism then uses).
Splicing transitions accompanying physiological change are disproportionately conserved (>40% of heart/muscle transitions conserved between mammals and birds, vs <20% genome-wide human-mouse), arguing for functional importance.
Genome-wide studies repeatedly find two largely separate gene sets: one regulated mainly at the level of total mRNA expression, the other mainly at the level of splice-isoform choice without changing total output.
A splicing regulatory network is defined as the set of events directly regulated by one RNA-binding protein; ~50 mammalian auxiliary regulators are characterized, each governing hundreds of events but determinative for only a subset.
Reviews concrete networks across biology: yeast meiosis (Mer1/Nam8/Tgs1), cell cycle/apoptosis (SRSF1, BCL-X, MCL1), stem-cell pluripotency (OCT4, RBFOX2, PTB/nPTB), EMT (SRSF1, ESRP1/2, FGFR2), heart development (CELF/MBNL/SR switch), brain development (DSCAM1, ROBO3, NOVA, RBFOX1), and stress responses (heat shock, genotoxic stress).

Notable claims / data

Alternative splicing affects ~90% of human genes, ~60% of Drosophila genes, ~25% of C. elegans genes (RNA-seq estimates).
In Drosophila development (27 stages) >60% of alternative splicing undergoes developmental change; in C. elegans (17 stages) >30% does, often in coordinated co-regulated sets.
Budding yeast: only 5% of genes (290 of 6,000) have introns, but introns are enriched in highly expressed genes — >1/4 of the ~38,000 RNAs transcribed per hour are spliced. ~45 intron-containing genes are inefficiently spliced in vegetative growth; 13 of 20 intron-containing meiotic genes are efficiently spliced only during meiosis. At least three overlapping meiotic splicing programs (Mer1 activates 4 single-intron genes; Nam8- and Tgs1-dependent subnetworks).
Cell cycle: a genome-wide RNAi screen with BCL-X/MCL1 splicing reporters found knockdown of 52 genes induced pro-apoptotic splicing of both; aurora kinase A loss degrades SRSF1; importantly the splicing response preceded mitotic arrest (parallel, not secondary).
ESCs: splice-isoform diversity is high in undifferentiated cells and decreases on differentiation. OCT4 yields three isoforms (OCT4A/B/B1) with distinct functions; RBFOX2 is essential for human ESC viability but not for differentiated/transformed cells. PTB represses neuronal nPTB via NMD-coupled exon-10 skipping; miR-124 silences PTB — first miRNA→splicing-network hierarchy.
EMT: SRSF1 upregulation produces pro-invasive ΔRON (RON exon-11 skipping); ESRP1/2 downregulation switches CD44v→CD44s (essential for EMT); FGFR2 IIIb/IIIc mutually exclusive choice is steered by ESRPs, RBFOX2, and PTB recruited via the H3K36me3 mark through adaptor MRG15 — linking the histone code to splicing.
Heart: postnatal development shows three temporally coordinated splicing-transition sets; >40% conserved mammal-to-bird. CELF1/CELF2 drop >10-fold postnatally while MBNL rises ~4-fold; forcing CELF gain + MBNL loss in adult hearts reverts >half the transitions to the embryonic pattern. miR-23a/miR-23b drive the >10-fold CELF1/CELF2 downregulation (Dicer1 deletion re-expresses fetal isoforms). SR-protein KOs (SRSF1, SRSF2 conditional → dilated cardiomyopathy by ~8 weeks; SRSF10 constitutive → mid-gestation lethality) each misregulate only a few unique transcripts.
Brain — permissive: Drosophila Dscam1 produces >19,000 extracellular variants; individual neurons express 14-50 stochastically; a single isoform suffices for homophilic repulsion but >1,000 isoforms are required population-wide for self/non-self discrimination. ROBO3’s two isoforms act as a binary midline-crossing switch (spatial restriction is post-transcriptional, not splicing-based).
Brain — determinative: NOVA regulates ~700 splicing events; uses a conserved YCAY positional activity code shared with Drosophila Pasilla; NOVA2 KO disrupts Dab1/reelin-pathway splicing causing neocortical migration defects. RBFOX1 (A2BP1) controls neuronal excitation; linked to epilepsy, intellectual disability, and autism.
Stress: heat shock inhibits splicing partly via rapid SRSF10 dephosphorylation (reversed within ~1 h) and partly via nuclear stress bodies (peak ~3 h, reversed over 10-12 h). Genotoxic stress: UV-induced MDM2/MDM4 exon skipping removes the p53-binding region (activating p53); two co-transcriptional mechanisms — reduced EWS-RNAPII coupling (more skipping) and slowed RNAPII elongation (more inclusion).
AS-NMD (alternative splicing coupled to nonsense-mediated decay): general mammalian rule — a stop codon >50 nt upstream of the last intron triggers NMD. SR and hnRNP gene families autoregulate via ultraconserved-element AS-NMD switches with “polar” feedback (SR activators: activation→NMD; hnRNP repressors: repression→NMD).
Caveat (Box 3): many RBP binding sites may be non-functional “sinks” that sequester the regulator rather than directing splicing — paralleling the miRNA-target paradox. Cell-heterogeneity caveat: <20% of heart cells are cardiomyocytes, so whole-tissue splicing transitions can be ambiguous; single-cell transcriptomics is proposed as the fix.

Connections

Foundational/contextual companion to existing review-type sources in this wiki: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — this 2011 review predates and underpins much of their framing of regulatory networks and physiological splicing.
Deep on splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins and the splicing-code; the SRSF1, hnRNPA1, PTBP1, and DSCAM entities all appear as central examples: srsf1, hnrnpa1, ptbp1, dscam.
Touches splicing-and-chromatin (FGFR2/H3K36me3/MRG15), cotranscriptional-splicing (RNAPII elongation rate, EWS), splicing-and-evolution (conserved YCAY code, NOVA functional divergence), and splicing-and-disease (RBFOX1/autism-epilepsy, cardiomyopathy, cancer/EMT).
Complements method-oriented sources capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the and sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying: it relies heavily on CLIP + splicing microarrays/RNA-seq + computational network inference (Box 1), the experimental substrate those methods/tools formalize.

Open questions

Which binding sites of an RBP are genuine “physiological targets” versus inert molecular “sinks,” and what criteria (conserved sites, robust loss/gain-of-function response, regulation during conserved physiological change) reliably distinguish them?
For each network, is a natural change in RBP activity actually used to drive the transition in vivo, versus the regulator merely being permissive/required?
How are multiple stress mechanisms with different kinetics (e.g., SRSF10 dephosphorylation vs nuclear stress bodies) integrated during recovery?
Is alternative splicing “reset” during somatic-cell reprogramming to pluripotency, as transcription is?
How will single-cell transcriptomics resolve which cell type within a heterogeneous tissue actually undergoes a measured splicing transition?

wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.title: katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna
wiki/sources/katz-2010-analysis-and-desi.content:
Analysis and design of RNA sequencing experiments for identifying isoform regulation
One-line: Introduces MISO (mixture-of-isoforms), a Bayesian statistical model that quantifies alternative-splicing and isoform expression from RNA-seq with confidence intervals, detects differential regulation, and yields design guidelines for paired-end RNA-seq experiments.
Key takeaways

MISO recasts isoform quantification as a Bayesian inference problem: it models the generative process of RNA-seq read sampling (an isoform is chosen by its length-rescaled abundance, then a read is sampled uniformly along the mRNA) and infers the posterior over isoform abundances Ψ.
Beyond the standard splice-junction estimator (Ŷ_SJ), MISO’s estimator Ŷ_MISO also uses reads on flanking constitutive exons (latent information) and paired-end insert-length distributions, both unbiased estimators of percent-spliced-in.
Incorporating mRNA fragment (insert) length distribution from paired-end data substantially improves splicing estimates and gives concrete RNA-seq design guidelines (favor paired-end, longer inserts of ~300+ nt, low dispersion, and matched insert sizes across compared libraries).
MISO detects differentially regulated exons/isoforms via a Bayes factor (BF) computed from the posterior over ΔΨ using the Savage-Dickey density ratio.
Applied to hnRNP H knockdown human cell data, MISO implicated the splicing factor hnRNP H1 in regulation of alternative cleavage and polyadenylation (APA), validated by CLIP-seq.
Exon-centric (two-isoform) case has an analytic solution; the general isoform-centric case uses a hybrid MCMC sampler (Metropolis-Hastings + Gibbs with a logistic-normal proposal on the simplex). Implements miso.

Notable claims / data

For a gene at median GA2 coverage (~220 RPK), the s.d. of estimated Ψ dropped more than twofold, from 0.21 (Ŷ_SJ) to 0.09 (Ŷ_MISO).
Against qRT-PCR for 52 alternatively skipped exons, Ŷ_MISO gave Pearson r = 0.87; restricting to high-confidence exons with 95% CI width < 0.25 (n = 23) raised it to r = 0.96.
Differential-splicing detection: 100% of exons (6/6) with BF ≥ 20 were confirmed differentially regulated by qRT-PCR vs 21% (4/19) with BF < 20 (P < 0.0004, Fisher’s exact). Overall ~15% of alternative exons changed at BF ≥ 20 on hnRNP H knockdown.
CLIP-seq validation: percentage of hnRNP-H-enhanced exons among those with flanking-intron CLIP tags rose from 60% to >90% as BF threshold increased, plateauing near BF = 5 (5:1 odds); effect stronger for downstream-intron binding and reversed for exonic CLIP tags.
Paired-end with insert-length info reduced Ψ error by ~2-5x; at 200 RPK, error fell from ~8% (standard) to ~4%, and to ~2% at higher coverage.
Low-dispersion paired-end length distributions raised the fraction of assignable reads from ~15% to >50%, potentially tripling splicing information at fixed depth. Libraries here had dispersion d between 0.6 and 1.5.
Caveat: read-coverage fluctuations along exons depend on fragment size, so comparisons are most accurate when compared libraries share similar insert lengths; very short mRNAs (a few hundred bases) lose ~20-40% coverage in longer-insert libraries. Genes with multiple distant alternative events still need longer reads/inserts to resolve.

Connections

A foundational isoform-quantification method predating rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both estimate percent-spliced-in and differential splicing but rMATS uses a replicate-aware likelihood-ratio framework whereas MISO uses single-sample Bayes factors.
Fits the methods/tools strand of the wiki (splicing-methods); relevant to alternative-splicing quantification and splicing-factors mechanism studies.
The hnRNP H application connects splicing-factor biology to alternative cleavage and polyadenylation; complements RBP-mechanism sources.
Builds on the earlier Jiang & Wong (2009) alternative-splicing quantification method, extending it with paired-end information.

Open questions

How well does the single-sample Bayes-factor differential test hold up versus replicate-based approaches when biological variability is high?
What is the practical magnitude/impact of increased local read-coverage fluctuations at insert lengths well beyond 300 nt?
Can short-read paired-end data, even with insert-length modeling, ever resolve isoforms in genes with many distant alternative events, or is long-read sequencing strictly required?

wiki/sources/lau-2019-splice-junction-ba.title: lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of
wiki/sources/lau-2019-splice-junction-ba.content:
Splice-Junction-Based Mapping of Alternative Isoforms in the Human Proteome
One-line: An RNA-seq-guided proteomics method (and open-source tool, jcast) that translates alternative splice-junction pairs into compact tissue-specific protein databases to detect noncanonical and undocumented protein isoforms by mass spectrometry across 12 human tissues and during iPSC-cardiomyocyte differentiation.
Key takeaways

Bridges the transcript-to-protein gap in alternative splicing: most splicing isoforms detected by RNA-seq have never been confirmed at the protein level, and standard MS protein databases (SwissProt ~1.1 isoforms/gene) preclude their identification.
Builds junction-centric databases by extracting AS events with rMATS (rmats) — A3SS, A5SS, MXE, SE (skipped exon), RI (retained intron) — filtering on skipped-junction read counts (Gaussian mixture model cutoff, ~4 reads in heart) and replicate consistency (p ≤ 0.01), then enforcing one-frame in-silico translation (annotated frame or a frame avoiding premature termination codons) to avoid the false-positive inflation of 3-/6-frame translation.
Translated junction pairs are stitched back to full-length SwissProt canonical sequences via a 10-aa overhang so that junction-spanning peptides are searchable; orphan slices are discarded.
Reanalyzing ~80 million public mass spectra (5 ProteomeXchange datasets) across 12 human tissues recovered >1,500 noncanonical protein isoforms, including ~400 sequences undocumented on TrEMBL/RefSeq.
Demonstrates a functional consequence of splicing: alternative isoform regions preferentially overlap intrinsically disordered regions and post-translational-modification (PTM) sites, suggesting AS regulates protein function via disordered-region rewiring and PTM-site toggling.

Notable claims / data

From ENCODE RNA-seq of 12 GTEx tissues: median 72,194 AS events/tissue (adrenal fewest 66,160; testis most 91,895). AS-type distribution: SE 65.1%, A3SS 10.8%, RI 10.1%, MXE 7.3%, A5SS 6.7%.
Custom databases are far sparser than standard ones: heart database 13,816 entries vs SwissProt human 42,237 (20,225 canonical + 22,012 isoform), TrEMBL 93,555, RefSeq 113,620 — only 6.3%–17.0% the size of RefSeq. Mean across 12 tissues 11,911 entries (pancreas fewest 6,309; testis most 19,285).
Reanalyzed 1,927 MS files / 79.6 million MS2 spectra. In heart left ventricle, 13,900 distinct peptides at 1% FDR (reverse decoy + Percolator); 397 (2.9%) absent from SwissProt canonical, 142 not in SwissProt canonical+isoform, 39 not in TrEMBL.
Overall: 3,418 distinct uniquely-mapped peptides not in canonical SwissProt → up to 1,555 noncanonical isoforms in 1,189 genes. 1,385 peptides (681 genes) absent from SwissProt(canonical+isoform); 566 also absent from TrEMBL; 453 (366 genes) not in RefSeq. Testis and heart most enriched in noncanonical isoforms.
MXE has higher-than-expected translational rate (16.3% of peptides vs 7.2% of RNA events); RI lower (5.6% vs 10.0%); SE proportionate (~64.6% vs 64.8%). Titin (TTN) yielded the most isoforms.
Validation lines of evidence: 70.8% of undocumented peptides not matchable to RefSeq even with 2 mismatches (so not SAAVs); MSFragger open search (–200/+400 Da) on left ventricle found only 1 of 51 undocumented peptides confidently at 1% FDR, while 267/394 (67%) TrEMBL-noncanonical were matched — indicating added identification power. Targeted PRM with heavy synthetic standards verified PKM1/PKM2 and detected 6/12 undocumented endogenous junction peptides in heart lysate.
Example: a MYBPC3 SE isoform (peptide RTDSHEDTGILDFSSLLK) differs from canonical by only 10/1,274 residues but lies in a disordered region enriched for PKA phosphorylation sites (Fisher p=0.02; S275/S284/S304), with cardiac diastolic relevance. MYOM1 SE isoform region shows higher disorder (Mann-Whitney p=3.3e-50). Proteome-wide, alternative regions are significantly more disordered.
iPSC-cardiomyocyte differentiation (original 10-plex TMT shotgun proteomics, 3 iPSC lines, 14-day protocol): 87 quantified noncanonical isoforms (14 not in SwissProt), with stage-specific regulation (e.g., ACTN4, TPM1 isoforms incl. TPM1-kappa, NCAM1 N-CAM 140/180, PDLIM5). Transcript-vs-protein isoform logFC correlated (Pearson r 0.57–0.74 noncanonical; 0.52–0.57 canonical).
Caveats stated by authors: label-free single-junction-peptide quantification is imprecise; short-read RNA-seq produces combinatorially redundant/ambiguous junctions; bottom-up MS cannot resolve full-length isoforms; some undocumented matches with unexpected retention times are likely false positives; long-read RNA-seq and top-down MS would mitigate.

Connections

Method is built directly on rMATS (rmats) for AS event quantification and uses the percent-spliced-in (PSI) framework; complements PSI-based transcript tools by adding a protein-level readout.
A proteomics counterpart to other splicing-methods sources in this wiki (e.g., shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); where most catalogued tools quantify splicing at the RNA level, this one asks which junctions are actually translated.
Supports the broader theme of alternative-splicing expanding proteome diversity and links splicing to protein structure/function via disordered regions and PTMs.
The named software deliverable is jcast.

Open questions

How many additional translatable junctions remain hidden due to short-read combinatorial ambiguity, and how much would long-read RNA-seq + top-down/middle-down proteomics recover?
Can elution-time (retention-time) prediction be improved enough to adjudicate borderline undocumented isoform peptides and lower false-positive risk?
Are MXE events genuinely more translatable, or is the apparent enrichment a reading-frame-conservation artifact?
Better NMD prediction from frameshifted/PTC-containing junctions to decide which transcripts truly yield protein.

wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.title: lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative
wiki/sources/lee-2015-mechanisms-and-reg.content:
Mechanisms and Regulation of Alternative Pre-mRNA Splicing
One-line: A broad review of how the spliceosome assembles and catalyzes intron removal as a ribozyme, how alternative-splicing is regulated by cis-elements, RNA-binding proteins, RNA structure, and chromatin, and how splicing defects cause disease and offer therapeutic targets.
Key takeaways

splicing removes introns via the spliceosome, a ~4.8 MDa dynamic RNP machine of five snRNPs (U1, U2, U4/U6, U5) that assembles stepwise (E → A → B → Bact/B* → C) on every intron and recycles.
The spliceosome is a ribozyme: the catalytic center is RNA (the U2–U6 snRNA base-paired complex), structurally and mechanistically analogous to self-splicing group II introns; metal-ion-rescue experiments in yeast U6 snRNA show RNA, not the prp8 protein, coordinates the catalytic divalent metals.
Alternative splicing scales with organismal complexity and expands the proteome; recent RNA-seq estimates indicate >95–100% of human multiexon genes produce at least two isoforms (see numbers below).
alternative-splicing is set up by cis-acting splicing-regulatory-elements (exonic/intronic enhancers and silencers) bound by splicing-factors — SR proteins (activators) and hnRNPs (repressors), plus tissue-specific rna-binding-proteins (Nova, PTB, Rbfox, Muscleblind/CELF) whose effect (activator vs. repressor) is position-dependent.
Splice-site choice can also be dictated by RNA–RNA base pairing (Drosophila DSCAM docking/selector sites) and, in trans, by a snoRNA (HBII-52) regulating the serotonin receptor 2C transcript.
splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks, “adapter” proteins (CHD1–U2 snRNP; MRG15–PTB), and RNA polymerase II elongation speed couple chromatin state to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing outcomes; some transcripts are spliced post-transcriptional-splicing.
splicing-and-disease: mutations at splice-sites or in silencers/enhancers cause disease (β-globin thalassemia, SMN2/spinal muscular atrophy, ALS via hnRNPA1/TDP-43/FUS prion-like domains); recurrent somatic mutations in 3′-splice-site factors drive myeloid cancers.
splicing-therapeutics: small molecules (spliceostatin targeting SF3b), antisense oligonucleotides (TSUNAMI correcting SMN2), and CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing are emerging interventions.

Notable claims / data

Human spliceosome: ~4.8 MDa, 40–60S; >200 proteins in metazoans, ~100 in yeast. Transition B → Bact loses U1/U4 snRNAs and ~35 proteins while adding 12; Bact → C loses 2 proteins and adds 9.
Average human gene: ~8 exons, 7 introns, ≥3 alternatively spliced isoforms; RNA-seq estimates average ~7 mRNA isoforms per gene.
Ensembl (pre-RNA-seq) alternative-splicing fractions of multiexon genes: C. elegans 25%, Drosophila 45%, mouse 63%, human 88%; ~63% of mouse genes vs. >95–100% of human genes show alternative splicing.
Drosophila DSCAM can generate >36,000 isoforms (~3× the fly gene count); exon 6 cluster has 48 mutually exclusive exons selected by docking/selector RNA base pairing, with hnRNP hrp36 enforcing mutual exclusivity.
Cancer: recurrent somatic mutations in U2AF1 (U2AF35), SRSF2 (SC35), SF3B1 (SAP155), ZRSR2 (URP) in myelodysplastic syndrome/CLL; SRSF1 (ASF/SF2) overexpression acts as an oncogene in mice (mTORC1-pathway targets).
U1 snRNP is the most abundant snRNP (~1 million copies/cell) and suppresses premature cleavage and polyadenylation (“telescripting”); blocking U1 causes premature polyadenylation within ~1 kb of transcription start sites.
RNA pol II elongation ~3–4 kb/min; “slow” polymerase mutants alter splicing patterns.
Structural milestones cited: U1 snRNP reconstituted/crystallized (5.5 Å; native 4.4 Å), U6 snRNP core (1.7 Å), U4 snRNP core; prp8 has RNase H- and reverse-transcriptase-like domains near the active site.
Caveat: deep sequencing may detect low-abundance isoforms that could be spliceosome errors rather than functional products.

Connections

Foundational support for spliceosome, splicing, and alternative-splicing as a single integrated topic; ties splicing-factors and splicing-regulatory-elements to the position-dependent splicing-code (Nova RNA map).
Links splicing-and-chromatin and cotranscriptional-splicing via RNA pol II speed and histone marks; connects post-transcriptional-splicing for slow/long introns.
Connects splicing-and-disease and splicing-therapeutics (cancer splicing-factor mutations → SF3b-targeting drugs; SMN2 → antisense).
Supports splicing-and-evolution: spliceosome evolved from group II self-splicing introns; alternative splicing correlates with organismal complexity.

Open questions

Which specific “causal” mis-spliced mRNAs (not just global pattern shifts) drive particular cancer phenotypes?
How prevalent is trans-acting small-RNA / Argonaute control of alternative splicing beyond HBII-52, given nuclear Ago-2 binds chromatin and splicing targets?
How much detected low-abundance alternative splicing is functional versus spliceosomal noise?
How does in vivo transcriptome RNA structure (SHAPE/DMS probing) shape splicing, and how do disease mutations disrupt it?

wiki/sources/li-2018-annotation-free-qua.title: li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna
wiki/sources/li-2018-annotation-free-qua.content:
Annotation-free quantification of RNA splicing using LeafCutter
One-line: Introduces leafcutter, an annotation-free, intron-excision-centric suite for quantifying RNA splicing from short-read RNA-seq that detects differential splicing and maps splicing QTLs at far greater scale and power than contemporary methods.
Key takeaways

LeafCutter quantifies splicing by focusing on intron excisions inferred from split (junction) reads, not isoforms or exons — obviating transcript annotations and sidestepping the statistical difficulty of isoform/exon abundance estimation in complex events.
It builds intron clusters: overlapping introns sharing a donor or acceptor splice site are connected into a graph whose connected components are clusters of alternatively excised introns; usage is summarized as counts/proportions within a cluster.
Two downstream analyses: (1) differential splicing between user-defined groups via a Dirichlet-multinomial generalized linear model (likelihood-ratio test); (2) sQTL mapping by feeding intron-excision proportions to standard QTL tools (fastQTL, MatrixEQTL).
Ships with LeafViz, an interactive R Shiny browser app for visualizing differential-splicing output, labeling clusters “annotated” vs “cryptic”.
Applied at scale to GTEx (2,192 samples, 14 tissues) and GEUVADIS lymphoblastoid cell lines; substantially boosts sQTL discovery and the interpretation of GWAS/disease-associated variants.

Notable claims / data

Identified 1.4–2.1× more sQTLs than contemporary methods; in GEUVADIS, 1.36–1.46× more than Cufflinks2 and 1.83–2.06× more than Altrans.
In 372 CEU GEUVADIS samples: 42,716 intron clusters, 5,774 sQTLs at 5% FDR (vs 620 trQTLs in the original GEUVADIS study — ~9× more), 4,543 at 1% FDR.
>10% of alternatively spliced introns per tissue were unannotated (10.8% pancreas to 19.3% spleen; testis an outlier at 48.5%); 31.5% overall unannotated. 86% of novel junctions were reproduced in independent SRA samples (Intropolis). 4,616 (~15–25%) novel splice sites conserved across vertebrates (phastCons ≥ 0.6).
Speed/scale: completed all YRI/CEU comparisons within 1 h, while Cufflinks2, rMATS, and MAJIQ took up to 7.8 h, 55.7 h, and 66.2 h on the largest comparison; <400 MB RAM vs MAJIQ’s >50 GB — an order-of-magnitude+ improvement over MAJIQ.
Better P-value calibration (near-uniform under permutation, like rMATS; Cufflinks2 overly conservative, MAJIQ posteriors miscalibrated) and lower false-negative rates in simulation.
5,070 tissue-regulated GTEx clusters at 10% FDR (|effect| > 1.5); detects differential splicing even with few replicates (885 clusters from 4 vs 4 samples; 98% replicated in a 220-sample analysis). Tissue-biased intron excisions are broadly conserved across mammalian organs.
sQTLs are highly shared across tissues (75–93% replication across pairs), and S-PrediXcan over 40 complex-trait GWASs increased associated disease genes at 5% FDR by an average 2.1-fold vs gene expression alone (e.g., CD40 newly implicated in rheumatoid arthritis).
Caveats: does not capture alternative transcription-start sites or polyadenylation (not reflected in intron excision); WASP filtering of allele-specific reads is needed before sQTL mapping; long reads may help resolve clusters for gene families with multi-mapping.

Connections

Defines the leafcutter entity; sits in the splicing-methods / percent-spliced-in family of differential-splicing callers alongside rmats (which it benchmarks against, finding comparable P-value calibration) and the isoform-assembly tool cufflinks (Cufflinks2, benchmarked and outperformed on sQTLs).
Avoids the library-size normalization required by dexseq’s exon-count model via the implicit normalization of its multinomial likelihood.
Its intron-centric, split-read view is shared with MAJIQ; LeafCutter’s main edge is scalability and adaptation to sQTL mapping.
Heavily relevant to splicing-and-disease and population/sQTL genetics: links genetic variation to mechanism via GWAS interpretation.

Open questions

How would LeafCutter’s intron-excision representation extend to long-read technologies, especially for resolving clusters in multi-mapping gene families?
What fraction of the thousands of conserved novel splice sites are genuinely functional vs splicing noise, and how would one test this?
How do intron-cluster quantifications compare to event-based ΔPSI callers when there is no one-to-one mapping between methods’ splicing events?

wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.title: lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method
wiki/sources/lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-compre.content:
PSI-Sigma: a comprehensive splicing-detection method for short-read and long-read RNA-seq analysis
One-line: Introduces PSI-Sigma, a splicing-detection tool with a new PSI index (PSIΣ) that quantifies Percent Spliced-In for individual alternative exons across all isoforms, benchmarked against rMATS, SUPPA2 and Whippet using real (non-simulated) RNA-seq data from synthetic RNA Sequins.
Key takeaways

PSI-Sigma computes percent-spliced-in using a modified PSI (Σ) equation that considers splice-junction reads of all isoforms between two constitutive exons, so a single alternative exon can carry multiple PSI values depending on the isoform it belongs to.
Unlike two-isoform tools (rmats, MISO) and isoform-resolution tools (SUPPA2), PSI-Sigma handles multiple-exon-skipping (MES) events and intron-retention (IR) events, where other tools fail.
Benchmarking used real RNA-seq data from spliced synthetic genes (RNA Sequins, 164 polyA RNAs in two mixtures with known concentration ratios = ground truth), avoiding the in silico-simulation limitation of prior splicing-methods evaluations.
PSI-Sigma also works on nanopore long-read RNA-seq (the other three tools are short-read only), demonstrated on U87 glioblastoma RNA spiked with RNA Sequins.
Tool entity: psi-sigma. Software at github.com/wososa/PSI-Sigma.

Notable claims / data

Short-read RNA Sequins benchmark (DPSI ≠ 0 criterion): recall — PSI-Sigma 1.0, Whippet 1.0, rMATS 0.64, SUPPA2 0.50; precision — PSI-Sigma 0.53, Whippet 0.40, rMATS 0.56, SUPPA2 0.52. rMATS/SUPPA2 missed up to ~50% of AS genes (cannot detect MES; actual recall for MES events was 0 for both).
DPSI accuracy vs ground truth: PSI-Sigma most accurate (r = 0.9441, P = 3.93e-30); SUPPA2 least accurate (r = 0.5700). For 8 IR events, PSI-Sigma highest correlation (r = 0.9465).
False positive rate at 10% DPSI cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0.12, Whippet 0.28, rMATS 0.08, SUPPA2 0.18; at 30% cutoff: PSI-Sigma 0, Whippet 0.04, rMATS 0, SUPPA2 0.10. PSI-Sigma FPRs differ significantly from Whippet (P=0.0002) and SUPPA2 (P=3.09e-8) but not from rMATS (P=0.0721).
Human transcriptome (Ensembl 94): PSI-Sigma identified MES events in 7029 genes (90.18% protein-coding) and IR events in 9175 genes vs only 3574 for rMATS v4.0.2; PSI-Sigma recovered 98.35% of rMATS’s IR genes plus 5660 additional. Validated AFMID exon-6 isoform switch in HCC (TCGA-LIHC, 50 normal + 369 HCC) where rMATS/Whippet could not report the two opposite DPSI values.
Long-read: nanopore GridION runs gave 2.20M (Run 1) and 2.51M (Run 2) reads; error rate ~12.5% vs <0.2% short-read; only 48 reads (0.0022%) of Run 1 ambiguous between human/Sequins genomes. Long-read DPSI vs concentration r=0.9245; short- vs long-read gene-expression r=0.9045. ~0.76M long reads covered ≥1 read for 11,420 protein-coding genes; ~1M long reads ≈ 10–100× fewer reads needed than short-read for PSI analysis.
Speed/caveat: PSI-Sigma single-threaded (~10 h for a 3-vs-3 comparison on pre-aligned BAMs) vs Whippet ~5 h and rMATS v4.0.2 ~30 min (8 threads). Whippet generates no P-values; PSI-Sigma and rMATS give adjusted P-values for multi-sample statistical comparison.

Connections

Direct benchmark competitor to rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); both perform similarly on single-alternative-exon events, but PSI-Sigma extends to MES/IR.
Methodological sibling to other splicing-quantification tools catalogued under splicing-methods; central concept is percent-spliced-in (PSI/DPSI).
Touches splicing-and-disease / cancer biomarkers via AFMID, TERT, SRSF2 examples and TCGA-LIHC analysis; mentions splicing factors srsf1 (SRSF1 last-intron IR) and SRSF2.
Methods/tools-survey context overlaps with sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Open questions

Does PSI-Sigma’s per-isoform PSI generalize to whole human cohorts at scale, given its lack of multithreading?
How does the ~12–13% nanopore error rate cap reliable IR/MES detection as long-read depth and base-calling improve?
Could rMATS/SUPPA2 close the MES/IR gap by adopting the PSIΣ index, as the authors suggest?
How robust is the RNA Sequins ground truth as a general benchmark for newer splicing tools beyond the four compared here?

wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.title: marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative
wiki/sources/marasco-2022-the-physiology.content:
The physiology of alternative splicing
One-line: A review arguing that most of the transcriptomic complexity generated by alternative-splicing in multicellular eukaryotes is reflected at the protein level and shapes diverse physiological and pathological processes, from tissue differentiation to thermal regulation, cancer and approved splicing-correcting therapies.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing occurs in ~95% of human genes and is “more a rule than an exception” in multicellular organisms; the central question is how much is functional versus noise. The authors argue most is functional, citing four decades of single-gene studies.
The apparent contradiction between sensitive RNA-seq and low-sensitivity mass spectrometry (which suggested ~85% of alternative exons are irrelevant) is attributed to technical limits of splicing proteomics (“absence of evidence is not evidence of absence”); ribosome profiling shows ~75% of medium/high-abundance skipped-exon mRNAs are ribosome-engaged.
cotranscriptional-splicing: splice-site choice depends on cis-elements (splicing-regulatory-elements) AND trans splicing-factors AND coupling to Pol II elongation. Kinetic coupling model — slow elongation promotes inclusion of class I exons and skipping of class II exons. splicing-and-chromatin: histone marks (H3K9ac, H3K9me2) modulate elongation and thus splicing outcomes.
Global alternative-splicing changes drive cell proliferation, cancer (splicing-and-disease), ASD and thermal regulation; gene-specific switches drive neuron self-avoidance, infrared sensing, telomere maintenance and the Warburg effect.
splicing-therapeutics: mastering splicing control yielded clinically approved ASO/small-molecule drugs for SMA (nusinersen, risdiplam) and DMD (eteplirsen).
splicing-and-evolution: alternative splicing per se has adaptive value as a readily available pool of mRNA variability that can be co-opted without eliminating gene functions or making new genes; it prevails in K-selected multicellular eukaryotes.

Notable claims / data

Drosophila Dscam can generate up to 38,000 mRNA variants (19,000 distinct extracellular domains) — more than the ~15,000 genes in the fly genome — via mutually exclusive exons in 4 regions; basis of stochastic neurite self-avoidance via homophilic repulsion. Vertebrates achieve self-avoidance differently, via clustered protocadherin promoters (~60 variants), no splicing.
CLK1 is a body-temperature sensor: activity is >5-fold higher at 35 °C than 38 °C; its 25-aa activation domain becomes flexible at high temperature (domain-swap with temperature-insensitive SRPKs confirms the activation domain is the thermosensor). Drives global SR-protein phosphorylation and splicing. Conserved in poikilotherms (alligators, turtles, Drosophila); links to fly circadian tim siesta behavior.
Warburg effect: PKM E9/E10 mutually exclusive exons (each 56 aa) → PKM1 (constitutive tetramer) vs PKM2 (low-activity, favors anabolic intermediates, cancer/embryonic). Regulated by hnRNPA1, hnRNPA2, PTBP1, whose genes are activated by MYC.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) infrared sensing: trigeminal-ganglion-specific TRPV1 splice variant (E14a, truncated C-terminus) lowers heat threshold from 43 °C to 30 °C — “sees” prey while retaining noxious-heat protection.
Telomeres: hTERT E2 skipping (vs SON-promoted inclusion) is a developmental switch controlling telomerase; an individual with a SON mutation had shorter telomeres.
ASD: neural microexons (3–27 nt, usually multiples of 3) are highly conserved, promoted by SRRM4 (reduced in ASD); examples in eIF4G1/eIF4G3 and CPEB4 (microexon 4). Gut-microbiota transplant from ASD donors into germ-free mice altered >500 splicing events (52 autism-related) with few gene-expression changes.
Cancer: SRSF1 (SF2/ASF) is a proto-oncogene; SRSF2/U2AF1/SF3B1 mutations in myeloid malignancies converge on similar splicing changes; PRMT5 methylates SRSF1 (AML target) and removes detained introns.
SMA: SMN1/SMN2 differ in 11 nt (key C>T in SMN2 E7 causes skipping). Nusinersen (FDA 2016/EMA 2017) blocks intron-7 ISS bound by hnRNPA1/A2 → E7 inclusion; first splicing-corrective drug. Marasco et al. found SMN2 E7 is a class II exon, and the ASO paradoxically induces H3K9me2 — HDAC inhibitors (VPA) synergize by relieving the elongation roadblock. Risdiplam = small molecule affecting snRNA–5′SS pairing.
DMD: E49–E50 deletions = 13% of cases; eteplirsen (30-mer ASO) targets an ESE in E51 to force E51 skipping, restoring frame (Becker-like internally truncated dystrophin).
