Quantification: a generative statistical model where the probability of observing each fragment is a linear function of transcript abundances; it incorporates the fragment-length distribution to disambiguate which isoform a paired-end fragment came from. Reports abundances in FPKM (fragments per kilobase per million mapped), extending the unpaired Jiang–Wong model to the paired-end case. Confidence intervals via Bayesian importance sampling; reports the MAP estimate.
Demonstrated on a C2C12 mouse myoblast differentiation time series (>430 million paired 75-bp reads), detecting both known and novel transcripts and revealing widespread isoform/promoter switching during myogenesis.
Coupling transcript discovery WITH abundance estimation matters: including novel isoforms strongly changed abundance estimates of known isoforms in many genes.

Notable claims / data

Sequenced >430 million paired 75-bp reads. Of 215 million fragments, 171 million (79%) mapped to the mouse genome; 46 million spanned ≥1 putative splice junction; 70% of spanned junctions were in known annotations (UCSC/Ensembl/VEGA).
Recovered 13,692 known isoforms and 12,712 new isoforms of known genes; estimated 77% of reads came from previously known transcripts. Of new isoforms, 7,395 (58%) contain novel splice junctions; 11,712 (92%) have an ORF (8,752 ending at an annotated stop codon).
3,724 novel isoforms present in multiple time points (the conservative high-confidence novel set); 581 at all time points. 62% of multiple-time-point novel isoforms supported by independent EST/RefSeq evidence or homologous genes in other species (expected FDR <5%).
High-confidence quantification set: 11,079 genes, 17,416 isoforms (13,692 = 79% known; 3,724 = 21% novel).
Accuracy: spike-in controls R²=0.99; simulated data (FluxSimulator) R²=0.96; excluding novel isoforms degraded known-isoform estimates (R²=0.90), showing transcript discovery and quantification must be coupled.
Dynamics: 330 genes showed complete switches in dominant TSS or splice isoform; subtler shifts in 1,304 others. Information-theoretic test (square root of Jensen–Shannon divergence, points on a logarithmic spiral in Hilbert space) found significant (FDR<5%) differential transcription in 882/3,486 (25%) and differential splicing in 273/843 (32%) candidate genes; 70 genes both. 10% of multi-promoter genes switched major primary transcript; 7% of alternatively spliced primary transcripts switched major isoform.
Robustness: ~75% of moderately expressed transcripts (≥15 FPKM) recovered with <40 million 75-bp paired-end reads; at 40M reads, moderately expressed transcripts estimated within 15% of final FPKM.
Case studies: Myc (transcriptional → post-transcriptional control shift between 60–90 h); FHL3 (novel proliferation-dominant TSS validated by TAF1 and RNA Pol II ChIP-Seq); tropomyosin I/II isoform switching. 153/185 putative novel TSSs validated against ChIP-Seq.
Caveat: novel single-time-point isoforms conservatively excluded (hard to distinguish full-length transcripts from partial assemblies); trajectory classification too coarse for per-gene prioritization or significance testing, motivating the JS-divergence metric. Genes with novel isoforms excluded from coding-sequence analysis, likely underestimating the impact on coding potential.

Connections

Pipeline companion to TopHat (Trapnell, Pachter, Salzberg 2009) for spliced read mapping; ships cufflinks plus Cuffcompare and Cuffdiff utilities. New tool entity: cufflinks.
A foundational splicing-methods paper for isoform-level RNA-Seq quantification; conceptual ancestor of later differential-splicing tools such as rmats (see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection).
The assembler explicitly weights its reachability graph using the percent-spliced-in metric (see percent-spliced-in), borrowed from prior tissue-transcriptome work, to phase distant exons.
Relevant to broader tooling surveys such as sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.

Open questions

How well does the parsimony (minimum-path-cover) assumption hold for genes with many lowly expressed or rare isoforms, where the minimal explanatory set may omit real transcripts?
Cufflinks here is annotation-free assembly on a single model system; how do error rates change across organisms, deeper alternative-splicing complexity, or short-read-only (unpaired) data?
The JS-divergence/Hilbert-space metric formalizes differential splicing vs. promoter use — how does it compare in power to later event-based PSI methods?

wiki/sources/van-nostrand-2020-a-large-s.title: van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional
wiki/sources/van-nostrand-2020-a-large-s.content:
A large-scale binding and functional map of human RNA-binding proteins
One-line: The ENCODE Phase III RBP resource — an integrative atlas of binding, in vitro specificity, function, chromatin association, and subcellular localization for 356 human RNA-binding proteins in K562 and HepG2 cells, producing 1,223 replicated datasets.
Key takeaways

Systematically characterizes 356 human rna-binding-proteins using five assays (eCLIP, RNA Bind-N-Seq, knockdown RNA-seq, immunofluorescence, ChIP–seq), producing 1,223 replicated datasets — the largest such effort to date at the time.
98 RBPs (28%) function in alternative-splicing regulation; 162 (46%) have more than one reported RNA function; 83 (23%) have no verified mechanistic RNA function in humans; 57% contain well-characterized RNA-binding domains (RRM, KH, zinc finger, helicase, etc.).
eCLIP (enhanced CLIP) of 150 RBPs (223 datasets) identified 844,854 enriched peaks covering 18.5% of the mRNA transcriptome and 2.6% of the pre-mRNA transcriptome, using stringent IP-versus-input enrichment and IDR reproducibility.
In vitro RNA Bind-N-Seq specificities (78 RBPs) are highly correlated with in vivo binding; eCLIP peaks that contain the high-affinity in vitro motif (RBNS+) confer stronger splicing regulation than peaks lacking it.
Per-RBP “RNA splicing maps” confirm classical antagonism: SR proteins generally repress and hnRNP proteins generally promote cassette-exon inclusion; relevant to ptbp1 and RBFOX2 maps.
Introduces the named encode-rbp-map resource (data at the ENCODE DCC and the RBP Image Database).

Notable claims / data

488 eCLIP experiments performed → 223 high-quality datasets for 150 RBPs (120 in K562, 103 in HepG2, 73 in both). Peaks required fold enrichment ≥8 and P ≤ 0.001, reproducible by IDR, with 57 blacklist regions removed.
Knockdown RNA-seq: 472 KD–RNA-seq profiles for 263 RBPs (235 in K562, 237 in HepG2). 375,873 differential-expression instances (20,542 genes affected by ≥1 RBP KD); 221,612 differential-splicing cases (38,555 alternatively spliced events). AQR knockdown alone drove >13,000 splicing changes (threefold more than any other RBP).
Only 5,214 genes had eCLIP peaks AND were knockdown-responsive for the same RBP → much of expression response is indirect.
RBNS (78 RBPs): single clusterable 5mer motif for 37/78; two motifs for 32/78; three or more for 9. For repressed cassette exons, RBNS+ exon-proximal peaks gave ~25% larger change in exon inclusion than RBNS– peaks.
Cross-cell-type: RBFOX2 HepG2 peaks averaged 6.2-fold enrichment in K562 when target expression was within fivefold; peak discrepancies mostly reflect cell-type-specific RNA expression, not differential binding.
Splicing-map finding: the upstream 5′ splice site of cassette exons shows greater RBP enrichment than the directly flanking introns — “an underappreciated region for splicing regulation.” QKI co-regulates RBFOX2-excluded exons (R² = 0.19, P = 1.2×10⁻⁵).
ChIP–seq of 37–58 nuclear RBPs: 792,007 peaks covering 3.8% of genome; RBPs occupy ~30% of open-chromatin regions and ~70% of gene promoters; ChIP–eCLIP target overlap is low (avg 6% of eCLIP, 2.4% of ChIP peaks) except specific pairs (e.g., HNRNPK–PCBP1/2, RBFOX2).
Immunofluorescence of 274 RBPs (HepG2) / 268 (HeLa): 217,412 images; speckle-localized RBPs affect more splicing events; mitochondrial-localized RBPs (e.g., DHX30, GRSF1, FASTKD2) enriched on mtRNAs; DHX30 binds an unannotated H-strand stem–loop possibly marking a transcription termination signal.
Caveat: eCLIP did not include visualization of protein-associated RNA, so orthogonal validation (in vitro motifs, KD response) is essential; GC-content biases in KD–RNA-seq required Salmon + CQN normalization.

Connections

This is the foundational ENCODE RBP resource underlying many splicing-factor analyses; complements mechanistic reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of.
Its splicing-map methodology and SR/hnRNP antagonism connect to fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing and to factor pages ptbp1, srsf1, hnrnpa1, u2af1.
The RBNS in-vitro-specificity component derives from Dominguez et al. 2018 (Mol. Cell); the eCLIP method from Van Nostrand et al. 2016 (Nat. Methods).
Provides nucleotide-resolution candidate binding sites useful for predicting variants that alter RNA processing — links conceptually to splice-variant prediction tools like spliceai.

Open questions

Why do RBNS-motif (longer-dwell) peaks confer stronger repression than activation of splicing?
What is the mechanism and topology behind HNRNPK/PCBP overlap of ChIP and eCLIP within gene bodies?
Does the DHX30-bound H-strand stem–loop genuinely mark the unknown mitochondrial H-strand transcription termination signal?
Extending coverage beyond ~10% of predicted human RBPs and beyond two cell lines, and large-scale functional validation of these RNA elements via CRISPR.

wiki/sources/vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-spl.title: vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous
wiki/sources/vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-spl.content:
RNA splicing analysis using heterogeneous and large RNA-seq datasets
One-line: Introduces the majiq-v2 software suite, a set of algorithms and visualization tools for detecting, quantifying, and visualizing local splicing variations from large, heterogeneous RNA-seq datasets (thousands of non-replicate samples), benchmarked against existing methods and applied to GTEx v8 brain subregions.
Key takeaways

Existing local-event splicing tools (original MAJIQ, rmats, LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet) are ill-suited to large, heterogeneous datasets: the assumption of a shared per-group PSI is violated even with few non-replicate samples, methods don’t scale or support incremental/multi-group analysis, and de novo splice complexity is hard to represent.
MAJIQ v2 contributes: (1) MAJIQ HET, a non-parametric per-sample differential-splicing test (TNOM, InfoScore, or Mann–Whitney U) that quantifies PSI per sample rather than assuming a shared group value; (2) an incremental splicegraph builder that reads BAM coverage once into reusable SJ files; (3) a faster, more accurate intron-retention quantification algorithm; (4) a “simplifier” to prune lowly-used junctions/introns; (5) the VOILA Modulizer, which parses overlapping LSVs into single-entry/single-exit alternative splicing modules (ASMs) classified into 14 basic AS event types; (6) VOILA v2, a cross-platform server-capable visualization app.
Builds on the local splicing variation (LSV) formulation from the original 2016 MAJIQ, capturing complex (>2 junction) and de novo events plus intron retention; quantification is in percent-spliced-in (Ψ) and dPSI (ΔΨ) via a Bayesian posterior model.
Applied to 2,335 GTEx v8 RNA-seq samples (374 donors, 13 brain subregions); cerebellar tissues show distinct splicing patterns, and the regulatory analysis recovers known neuronal splicing regulators including ptbp1, RBFOX family, SRRM4/nSR100, SRSF11, and QKI.
MAJIQ is the only benchmarked method offering de novo intron-retention detection and quantification.

Notable claims / data

On a large synthetic dataset (~300+ samples mimicking GTEx cerebellum and skeletal muscle, quantified by RSEM): MAJIQ and MAJIQ HET maintained low FDR (~0.3%) with low FNR; rMATS, Whippet, SUPPA2 had FDR ~15–30% and high FNR (60%, 32%, 49% respectively). SUPPA2 reported >6000 differentially spliced genes (thousands more than others); Whippet reported ~727 (far fewer).
MAJIQ and LeafCutter both reach MCC 0.97–0.99, but MAJIQ reports 34% more changing genes (2337 vs 1739) and 6% more non-changing (7110 vs 6713); at the event level the gap widens (4267 vs 2169 changing events) due to finer LSV-based event resolution.
Upset/unique-gene analysis (10 vs 10): SUPPA2’s 1713 unique genes have >25% FPR; rMATS and Whippet unique sets have 47% and 16% FPR; both MAJIQ unique sets have ~0 FPR; LeafCutter unique set 9.5% FPR.
On real GTEx data, MAJIQ HET improves reproducibility-ratio (RR) detection power over MAJIQ dPSI at equal reproducibility; intra-to-inter ratio (IIR, a real-data FDR proxy) was 2–7% for MAJIQ/HET/LeafCutter vs 13% (rMATS), 26% (SUPPA2), 23.7% (Whippet) on small groups.
RT-PCR PSI correlation (gold standard, >100 mouse experiments): MAJIQ, Whippet, rMATS ~0.97–0.98; LeafCutter 0.915, SUPPA 0.869.
Builder step is ~6.6x slower (wall-time) than quantification, motivating the one-time builder + reusable coverage design; all methods run in 0.5–4 GB memory.
GTEx brain analysis with E[Ψ] ≥ 5% simplification threshold defined 32,435 AS modules; 70.6% contain ≥1 unannotated junction/intron; 59.2% are binary events, ~40% contain multiple AS events; non-classical event types make up 13.1% of modules / 11.6% of events. Intron retention was the most common event type, consistent with high neuronal IR.
Cerebellar vs other-brain comparison: 3995 unique changing AS modules, >7500 changing AS events; intron retention most prevalent, then cassette exons.
QKI mechanism: cerebellar cassette-exon events 5.6x more likely to show QKI regulation than non-changing events (binomial p < 2.8e-31; ENCODE HepG2 QKI shRNA knockdown); decreased cerebellar QKI expression proposed to drive both inclusion (loss of upstream repression) and exclusion (loss of downstream enhancement).
Caveat: the cerebellar regulatory analysis is from bulk GTEx tissue and may be confounded by cell-type composition (cerebellum has higher neuronal fraction); validation tools aim only at a “bronze” reproducibility level.

Connections

A splicing-methods / percent-spliced-in tool paper; direct successor/competitor framing against rmats (rMATS turbo), LeafCutter, SUPPA2, Whippet, and the original MAJIQ.
Mechanistic findings link to splicing-factor biology: ptbp1 regulation during neurogenesis, plus RBFOX, SRRM4, SRSF11, and QKI as cerebellar regulators — sibling to RBP-mechanism splicing sources in the wiki.
Heavy use of intron retention quantification complements other splicing-quantification entities; the Modulizer’s AS event typology parallels event definitions in MISO and rMATS.
Companion resources released: the largest synthetic RNA-seq splicing benchmark to date (RSEM-based) and the validationstools comparison package.

Open questions

How much does bulk-tissue cell-type heterogeneity confound the cerebellar regulatory signatures, and would single-cell or deconvolution change the QKI/RBFOX/SRRM4 conclusions?
Can long-read integration extend MAJIQ’s local-event quantification toward full isoform-level deconvolution?
How well does MAJIQ HET translate to clinical RNA diagnostics and to improved sQTL analysis, both flagged as future directions?
Are the novel non-classical AS module subtypes (putative alternative first/last exons, putative alt 3’/5’ from simplification) biologically validated beyond pattern-matching heuristics?

wiki/sources/wang-2008-alternative-isofo.title: wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human
wiki/sources/wang-2008-alternative-isofo.content:
Alternative isoform regulation in human tissue transcriptomes
One-line: A foundational deep-sequencing (mRNA-Seq) survey of 15 human tissues and cell lines that established alternative splicing as essentially universal (92-94% of human genes) and showed that most splicing and polyadenylation events are tissue-regulated and coordinately controlled.
Key takeaways

mRNA-Seq of 10 diverse human tissues plus 5 mammary epithelial / breast-cancer cell lines (32-bp cDNA reads, 12-29 million reads per sample, >400 million reads total) produced a digital inventory of gene and mRNA isoform expression.
Mapping reads to exon-exon junctions estimated that 92-94% of human multi-exon genes undergo alternative splicing; ~86% of all human genes produce two or more appreciable isoform populations (minor isoform >=15%). This is the headline claim that made alternative splicing “nearly universal.”
Most alternative splicing and alternative cleavage/polyadenylation (APA) events vary between tissues; variation between individuals was roughly twofold-to-threefold less common than variation between tissues.
Defined the “per cent spliced in” (PSI / Psi) metric as the ratio of inclusion-read density to the sum of inclusion and exclusion read densities — a quantity now central to splicing quantification (see percent-spliced-in).
“Switch-like” exons (large between-tissue PSI differences) show higher sequence conservation in the exon and flanking introns and preferentially preserve the reading frame, indicating they generate full-length protein isoforms rather than truncations.
Provides early genome-scale “tissue RNA maps” linking regulatory motif position to tissue-specific activity for factors such as FOX-1/FOX-2 and PTBP1 (see ptbp1).

Notable claims / data

~60% of reads mapped uniquely to the genome (up to 2 mismatches), +4% to splice junctions; ~two-thirds of reads assigned unambiguously; estimated mis-mapping ~0.1%; only ~3% of reads from rRNA. Read density >100-fold higher in exons than introns/intergenic.
Asymptotic estimate of alternatively spliced multi-exon genes: ~98% excluding cell lines, ~100% with all samples; 94% of RefSeq genes are multi-exon. No alternative splicing detected in the 6% single-exon genes.
1,413 high-confidence new exons identified (FDR ~1.5%) plus thousands of putative new junctions — showing mRNA-Seq’s discovery power, with the caveat that very deep coverage is needed for low-abundance isoforms.
A comprehensive set of ~105,000 events of eight types (skipped exon, retained intron, A5SS, A3SS, MXE, AFE, ALE, tandem 3’ UTR) was assembled; >22,000 found tissue-regulated (Fisher’s exact test, 5% FDR), far exceeding prior sets numbering in the hundreds to low thousands. Estimated true tissue-regulation frequencies ranged 52-80% across event types.
Inter-individual variation assessed via six cerebellar cortex samples from unrelated men: ~10-30% of events showed individual-specific variation (depending on type), broadly consistent with a prior ~21% estimate but below tissue-level variation (47-74%). Caveat: differentially expressed isoforms reflect mixed cell-type composition and inter-individual differences, not pure cell-intrinsic regulation.
Example: SLC25A3 mutually exclusive exons 3A/3B — 3A dominant in heart and skeletal muscle, 3B in testes/liver, consistent with heart/muscle symptoms of exon-3A mutation. TPM1 exon 2: PSI 2% in heart vs 95% in skeletal muscle.
Switch score for MXEs shifted higher than for skipped exons (K-S test P = 3.7e-5). UGCAUG (FOX-1/FOX-2 motif) enriched in introns downstream of exons with increased inclusion in heart/muscle/brain/cerebellum; PTBP1/PTBP2-like UCUCUC/CUCUCU motifs upstream of cerebellum-included exons.
Evidence for coordinated splicing-APA regulation: SVD of inclusion ratios cleanly separated cell lines from tissues across all event types; pairwise SVD distances for splicing events and tandem-3’UTR events were highly correlated. Splicing-associated motifs (UGCAUG, (CUG)n for CELF/MBNL, ACUAAC for QKI with intronic S:B >7:1) showed high conservation in both introns and 3’ UTRs, suggesting dual splicing / 3’UTR roles for these factors.

Connections

A foundational anchor for the splicing topic: the empirical basis behind the “nearly universal” framing in reviews such as lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing.
Operationalizes the percent-spliced-in metric used by downstream quantification tools like rmats (shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection); this paper is methodologically upstream of those PSI-based event detectors and of splicing-methods.
Connects regulatory motif maps to splicing-factors and rna-binding-proteins, notably ptbp1 and the FOX family, and to splicing-regulatory-elements / cis-regulatory-elements via conserved intronic and 3’UTR motifs.
Supports the splicing-and-evolution hypothesis that alternative splicing drives mammalian phenotypic complexity.
Complements wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source on isoform diversity as a source of functional variety.

Open questions

How much of the detected “alternative splicing” reflects allele-specific / individual-genotype effects rather than true tissue regulation, given the single-donor-per-tissue design?
Are FOX, CELF/MBNL and QKI genuinely “moonlighting” in 3’UTR/APA roles, or do the conserved 3’UTR motifs reflect distinct factors with identical specificity? Direct functional validation of coordinated splicing-APA regulation is needed.
How do bulk-tissue cell-type composition differences confound the tissue-specific isoform estimates — a question later addressable with single-cell isoform sequencing.
What is the mechanistic basis for tandem-3’UTR tissue regulation (APA choice vs differential isoform stability)?

wiki/sources/wang-2008-splicing-regulati.title: wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts
wiki/sources/wang-2008-splicing-regulati.content:
Splicing regulation: From a parts list of regulatory elements to an integrated splicing code
One-line: A 2008 RNA review by Wang & Burge framing the long-term goal of a predictive “splicing code,” synthesizing what was then known about splicing cis-regulatory elements (ESE/ESS/ISE/ISS), their context-dependent activities, and early attempts to integrate them into splicing-simulation algorithms.
Key takeaways

Outside the core splice site motifs, most of the information needed for splicing is held in exonic/intronic cis-regulatory elements that recruit sequence-specific RNA-binding proteins to activate or repress adjacent splice sites — i.e. the splicing-regulatory-elements view of the splicing-code.
In mammals, splice sites are predominantly recognized in pairs across the exon via exon definition (short exons, large introns); intron definition dominates in most other metazoans, with pufferfishes intermediate — supporting exon definition as the critical regulated step in mammalian splicing regulation.
Core splice signals carry only about half the information needed to accurately define exon/intron boundaries; the rest comes from SREs that distinguish authentic exons from abundant “decoy” splice sites and pseudoexons.
ESEs mostly recruit SR proteins (via RRM/RS domains) to promote splicing; ESSs are typically bound by hnRNP-class repressors such as hnRNP I and hnRNP A1.
SRE activity is strongly context-dependent — both location-dependent (same motif acts as enhancer or silencer depending on exonic vs intronic position) and gene-dependent (activity in one gene lost when moved to another). RNA structure and trans-factor availability modulate which of the many candidate motifs are actually functional.
Proposes a “bottom-up” program: catalogue cis-elements, learn their context-dependence rules, and integrate them into simulation algorithms — embodied here by the exonscan splicing simulator.

Notable claims / data

Typical human exons are ~50-250 bp, separated by introns of hundreds to thousands of bp, with introns averaging >90% of the primary transcript.
Core human splice site motifs estimated (via Monte Carlo motif-insertion simulations) to contain only ~half the information needed to define boundaries (Lim & Burge 2001).
A cell-based fluorescence-activated screen (FAS) identified 133 active ESS decanucleotides in human cells (Wang et al. 2004); FAS-ESS elements help distinguish authentic exons from pseudoexons and regulate alt splice-site usage and intron retention.
~1000 hexamers estimated to be able to function as either ESSs or ESEs (Stadler et al. 2006), so every pre-mRNA carries many candidate motifs, only a fraction recognized — analogous to TF-binding-site over-prediction near promoters.
Intronic regions flanking alternative exons are far more conserved than those around constitutive exons, out to ≥150 bp (Sorek & Ast 2003); UGCAUG (Fox-1/2 site) repeatedly emerges as the most conserved near-AS motif and is conserved from nematodes to mammals.
The Nova “RNA map” (Ule et al. 2006) predicts the direction of Nova-dependent regulation with high accuracy — a key demonstration that a factor’s full set of activities can be rationalized; YCAY clusters act as ESS, ISE, or ISS depending on position.
Caveat: the authors note their own analyses found statistically- vs experimentally-derived ESE and ESS sets to be largely non-overlapping, arguing against a single sequence commonly being both ESS and ESE — in tension with the “ESR” claim (Goren et al. 2006).
Caveat: array-based global AS platforms identify a subset of events with high confidence but have an unknown, probably substantial false-negative rate.

Connections

Foundational/conceptual companion to other splicing-code and regulation reviews in the wiki: rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the (which extends the cis-element/RBP “map” idea into the modern computational/CLIP era).
Predates and motivates modern sequence-to-splicing predictors; the exonscan log-odds simulator is an intellectual ancestor of deep models like spliceai.
Discusses many RBP entities already in the wiki: ptbp1, hnrnpa1, srsf1 (SR proteins), and the smn2 therapeutic-target intronic elements; also touches dscam mutually-exclusive splicing via intronic secondary structure (rna-structure-and-splicing).
Relates to cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin by framing the splicing regulatory network as a subnetwork of gene regulation coupled to Pol II.

Open questions

How can one predict which of a pre-mRNA’s many candidate motifs will actually be recognized in a given cell type/context — the central barrier to a working splicing code?
How general is the role of pre-mRNA secondary structure in gating SRE/splice-site accessibility, and how to measure RNA structure in vivo at high throughput (SHAPE then only in vitro)?
How to generalize cis-elements, factors, and context rules learned in one cell type or developmental stage to others?
Will the splicing code resemble the authors’ analogy — less a simple table (like the genetic code) and more an “IRS form 1040” of conditional tables and arithmetic?

wiki/sources/wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolut.title: wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolutionary-tool
wiki/sources/wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolut.content:
RNA-Seq: a revolutionary tool for transcriptomics
One-line: Foundational 2009 perspective introducing RNA-Seq as a deep-sequencing approach to transcriptome profiling, laying out the workflow, advantages, and computational challenges — including junction-read detection of splicing — that underpin most later splicing-by-RNA-seq methods.
Key takeaways

RNA-Seq converts an RNA population (total or poly(A)+) into a library of adaptor-tagged cDNA fragments that are deep-sequenced (reads typically 30-400 bp, single- or paired-end), then aligned to a reference or assembled de novo to yield a genome-scale transcription map of structure and expression.
Relative to hybridization (tiling microarrays) and Sanger EST/tag methods (SAGE, CAGE, MPSS), RNA-Seq offers single-base resolution, very low background, large dynamic range, low RNA input, and the ability to distinguish isoforms and allelic expression.
Reads are classified as exonic, junction, or poly(A) end-reads; junction reads spanning splice sites are the basis for detecting and quantifying splicing diversity, connecting alternative-splicing analysis to short-read sequencing.
Key informatics challenges are highlighted: mapping reads across splice junctions, multi-mapping reads, sequencing errors/polymorphisms, and the coverage-versus-cost tradeoff — recurring themes for downstream splicing-methods.

Notable claims / data

Splice/exon-exon junctions can be identified by the GT-AG dinucleotide consensus flanking splice sites and confirmed by low intronic expression; for complex transcriptomes, mapping junction-spanning reads is harder owing to extensive alternative splicing and trans-splicing. A partial solution is mapping to a compiled junction library of known and predicted junctions.
In humans, 31,618 known splicing events were confirmed (11% of all known splicing events) plus 379 novel events (ref. 24); a separate human study found 94,241 junctions (4,096 novel) and showed exon skipping is the prevalent form of alternative splicing (ref. 41). Extensive alternative splicing was observed for 3,462 mouse genes, and 42 events joining exons from multiple mouse genes (trans-splicing-like) were detected.
Dynamic range: >9,000-fold estimated from 16M mapped reads in S. cerevisiae; five orders of magnitude for 40M mouse reads; microarrays span only ~100-300-fold. RNA fragmentation gives even gene-body coverage but depletes transcript ends, whereas oligo-dT-primed cDNA fragmentation is 3’-biased.
Coverage/cost: ~80% of yeast genes detected at 4M uniquely mapped reads (plateau thereafter); 30M 35-nt poly(A) reads observe >90% of genes in simple yeast transcriptomes; in S. pombe, 122M reads across six conditions detected >99% of annotated genes.
Caveat: a 2009 perspective on a then-nascent technology — short reads (30-400 bp), no strand-specific or paired-end as default, and isoform-level quantification was still aspirational; no benchmark of a specific splicing tool is provided.

Connections

Methodological ancestor to the RNA-seq-based splicing-quantification tools catalogued in this wiki such as rmats and reviewed in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the; the junction-read concept underlies later percent-spliced-in estimation.
Provides the data-generation substrate for studying splice-sites, alternative-splicing, and splicing-and-disease (it notes RNA-Seq’s promise for comparing diseased vs normal tissue).
Mentions early short-read aligners (ELAND, SOAP, MAQ, RMAP) as the mapping layer; complements the regulatory/biology reviews like lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative and rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing by supplying the measurement technology.

Open questions

How to computationally identify novel splicing events between distant sequences or across two different genes (trans-splicing) at scale — flagged here as a future challenge.
How much additional isoform and alternative-splicing complexity remains undetected at then-current depths, and how paired-end / longer / strand-specific reads would resolve it.
How to define transcriptome “coverage” rigorously given unknown true isoform number and highly variable transcription activity.

wiki/sources/wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-ef.title: wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible
wiki/sources/wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-ef.content:
rMATS-turbo: an efficient and flexible computational tool for alternative splicing analysis of large-scale RNA-seq data
One-line: A step-by-step Nature Protocols guide to rmats-turbo, the fast, scalable re-implementation of rmats that keeps the original statistical framework and CLI but adds a decoupled prep/post workflow enabling alternative-splicing analysis on RNA-seq datasets with tens of thousands of samples.
Key takeaways

rMATS-turbo (rMATS v4.0.1+) is a re-engineering of rmats that maintains its statistical model and user interface while dramatically improving speed and data-storage efficiency, scaling to massive RNA-seq datasets. It was first released in 2017 (v4.0.1); open-source versions (v4.1.0+) on GitHub since 2020.
Core architectural novelty: a decoupled two-step workflow. The prep step processes each FASTQ/BAM file independently into a weighted splicing-graph file (.rmats); the post step integrates .rmats files across samples to discover and quantify events. Prep can run in parallel/distributed on a cluster; adding new samples only requires re-running post.
Uses a weighted splicing-graph data structure (exons = nodes, splice junctions = edges weighted by read counts) stored in the compact .rmats format.
Discovers and quantifies the five canonical alternative-splicing patterns — skipped exon (SE), alternative 5′ splice-sites (A5SS), alternative 3′ splice sites (A3SS), mutually exclusive exons (MXE), retained intron (RI) — and quantifies them with percent-spliced-in (PSI/Ψ), normalized by effective isoform lengths. Read counts come in junction-only (JC) and junction+exon-body (JCEC) flavors.
Two worked application scenarios: (1) a two-group differential-splicing comparison (PC3E vs GS689 prostate-cancer cell lines, EMT phenotypes); (2) large-scale quantification across ~1,000 samples (1,019 CCLE cancer cell lines) on a compute cluster.
The companion rmats2sashimiplot tool visualizes rMATS-turbo output as sashimi plots.

Notable claims / data

Procedure 1 (PC3E vs GS689, n=3 each, --task both, --novelSS on): ~5.5 h wall-clock, 11 GB peak RAM. Identified 690,089 AS events total via junction reads; 155,056 after filtering (≥10 reads, non-extreme PSI), SE most prevalent; 14,210 differential events after FDR ≤0.01 and |ΔPSI| ≥0.05. Example: USO1 exon 13 PSI 91% (PC3E) vs 5% (GS689), FDR ≤1.0×10⁻¹⁵; a 21-nt novel “micro-exon” in MAST3 (PSI 9% vs 41%, FDR 2.03×10⁻⁹) found only because --novelSS was enabled.
Procedure 2 (1,019 CCLE cell lines, decoupled prep/post, --statoff): ~3 d wall-clock, 24 GB peak RAM, on a CHOP HPC cluster (82 nodes × 24 cores × 128 GB, one core per job). Identified 1,428,168 AS events (1,078,334 SE); 52,797 high-confidence SE after filtering (≥20 mean reads, 5th-percentile PSI ≥0.05 and 95th ≤0.95); 162 SE events with PSI correlated (R²>0.4) with EMT score. Per-sample prep step typically <1 h.
New/expanded features vs original rMATS: single-group mode (omit --b2/--s2), --statoff to decouple statistics, --task stat to re-run only the test on extracted counts, --novelSS for unannotated splice sites/exons (with --mil/--mel), --fixed-event-set, --paired-stats invoking the PAIRADISE paired model, plus --variable-read-length and --allow-clipping.
Recommended significance criteria (two-group): mean read count ≥10 in both groups; filter PSI <0.05 or >0.95 in both; FDR ≤0.01; |ΔPSI| ≥0.05.
Positioning vs alternatives: AS tools fall into isoform-based (Cufflinks, kallisto), exon-based (DEXSeq), and event-based categories; event-based is most widely used. rMATS-turbo is reported to perform favorably or comparably to other tools (MISO, SpliceTrap, SUPPA/SUPPA2, MAJIQ, LeafCutter) in multiple benchmarks, with high citation count attributed to ease of use and scalability. Different tools tend to give concordant PSI estimates.
Caveat: organism-agnostic; designed for short reads only — handles only binary (basic) events, cannot resolve complex multi-choice events or long-range coupling between distant events. Authors note long-read RNA-seq may overcome these limits but needs new tooling. Y.X. and Z.X. receive licensing income for commercial use of rMATS-turbo.

Connections

Direct successor to shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (the original rmats statistical-model paper); this protocol is the first systematic description of the turbo engine, which the field had already broadly adopted in practice.
Defines the practical percent-spliced-in measurement workflow underpinning event-based splicing-methods; complements the prediction-side tools (spliceai) and the broader toolkit framing in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying.
Both worked examples target EMT in cancer cell lines → relevant to splicing-and-disease; the protocol also notes downstream use for sQTL discovery from population-scale RNA-seq.

Open questions

How do the speed/storage gains of the prep/post decoupling quantify against contemporaries (SUPPA2, LeafCutter, MAJIQ) on identical large cohorts — the protocol cites favorable benchmarks but runs none itself?
What is lost by the binary-event restriction in genes with complex/coupled splicing, and how will the announced long-read successor tooling address it?
How robust is --novelSS micro-exon discovery (e.g., the 21-nt MAST3 exon) to alignment choices and false positives at population scale?

wiki/sources/wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d.title: wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure
wiki/sources/wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d.content:
Predicting RNA 3D structure and conformers using a pre-trained secondary structure model and structure-aware attention
One-line: Introduces trrosettarna2, an end-to-end deep learning method for RNA 3D structure and conformer prediction that injects a pre-trained secondary-structure prior into a structure-aware attention model, a parameter-efficient approach to rna-structure-prediction.
Key takeaways

trrosettarna2 addresses RNA 3D-data scarcity by pre-training a secondary-structure module (trRNA2-SS) on the large bpRNA database (~12× more entries than RNA structures in the PDB) and feeding base-pairing priors into the 3D pipeline — a concrete rna-structure-prediction strategy.
The pipeline is four-stage: an SS prior module, an RNAformer two-track transformer (12 blocks), an SS-aware invariant-point-attention structure module (8 blocks), and PyRosetta relaxation/energy minimization; it offers both an end-to-end (E2E) and a PyRosetta-minimization variant.
The embedded SS module doubles as a standalone state-of-the-art secondary-structure predictor, trRNA2-SS, trained via transfer learning (bpRNA pre-train then PDB fine-tune).
A key novelty is “SS-weighted softmax”: base-pairing probabilities multiply the attention maps (with an annealing temperature schedule) so secondary structure directly biases 3D attention — analogous in spirit to other AI-for-biology methods like spliceai that bake domain priors into the network.
By varying the SS input, trrosettarna2 can sample distinct RNA conformers without experimental data, recapitulating RNase P RNA conformational heterogeneity.

Notable claims / data

On the TS28 benchmark (28 non-redundant, temporally split RNAs), final trRosettaRNA2 reaches average RMSD 8.66 Å — a 24% improvement over predecessor trRosettaRNA (11.35 Å) and better on 71.4% of targets; competitive with AlphaFold 3 (9.14 Å RMSD), with better RMSD/lDDT but slightly lower TM-score/INF.
Far fewer steric clashes than AlphaFold 3 (clashscore ~2 vs >10) and no geometric entanglements (verified via RNAspider).
Parameter/compute efficiency: ~30M parameters (1/2 of RoseTTAFoldNA, 1/4 of RhoFold+, 1/12 of AlphaFold 3); trained in ~33 days on a single A100/A800 GPU (~56 GPU-days) vs AlphaFold 3’s ~20 days on 256 A100 GPUs (5,120 GPU-days).
CASP16 blind test: Yang-Server was the top automated server group (1/16; 4th of 64 overall), surpassing AF3-server (9th) and dNAfold/DRfold2 (24th); on CASP15 it ranked second only to the top human group AIchemy_RNA2.
RNase P RNA conformers: the trRosettaRNA2 ensemble (11 SS inputs × 4 recycles) gives overall RMSF 12.5 Å and matches per-residue AFM dynamics (PCC 0.894), strongly outperforming AlphaFold 3 (RMSF ~4.7 Å, homogeneous, much lower PCC).
Caveats: cannot model full RNA–protein/ligand complexes (a limitation vs AlphaFold 3); crop size capped at 384 nt limited applicability to >400 nt CASP16 targets (AlphaFold 3 predictions were substituted for some large targets); struggles on synthetic RNAs; conformer “ground truth” rests on AFM-derived ensembles.

Connections

Sits within artificial-intelligence as an AI-for-structural-biology method; conceptually a sibling to genomics deep-learning models such as spliceai/splicing-code, all of which encode biological priors into neural architectures.
Extends the protein/biomolecular structure-prediction lineage (AlphaFold2 invariant point attention, AlphaFold 3, RoseTTAFoldNA, RhoFold+) into the RNA-specific, data-scarce regime.
The data-scarcity-driven design (leveraging abundant secondary-structure annotations to compensate for few 3D structures) is a concrete instance of the data-quality-and-bias theme in ai-in-genomics.

Open questions

Can the SS-prompting strategy be coupled with experimental inputs (cryo-EM, AFM) to push conformer accuracy and handle large/synthetic RNAs?
How well does diverse-SS-input conformer sampling generalize beyond RNase P to other dynamic RNAs, and is the AFM-derived ensemble a sufficient ground truth?
Could end-to-end complex (RNA–protein/ligand) modeling be added without sacrificing the parameter efficiency that distinguishes the method?

wiki/sources/wenkel-2026-txpert-using-mu.title: wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge
wiki/sources/wenkel-2026-txpert-using-mu.content:
TxPert: using multiple knowledge graphs for prediction of transcriptomic perturbation effects
One-line: txpert is a latent-transfer deep learning model that fuses multiple gene–gene knowledge-graphs (curated databases plus high-throughput perturbation screens) to predict transcriptomic responses to unseen single, double and cross-cell-line genetic perturbations — a single-cell-perturbation-modeling method.
Key takeaways

txpert couples two modules: a basal-state encoder (MLP best for unseen/double tasks) embedding batch-matched control profiles, and a GNN perturbation encoder that reads gene(product)–gene(product) interaction graphs; embeddings are combined by additive latent shift and decoded to the perturbed expression profile.
It targets three out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks in one framework: unseen single perturbations within a cell line, novel double perturbations from seen singles, and perturbations in unseen cell lines (zero-shot cross-context).
Combining complementary graphs is the central finding: database-derived graphs (STRING, GO) plus screen-derived proprietary Recursion “maps” (PxMap from phenomics/microscopy; TxMap from single-cell transcriptomics screens) yields best performance; incrementally adding all four graphs peaks performance.
It also delivers methodological best-practices: batch-matched (not global-mean) controls to avoid mistaking batch variance for perturbation effect, and retrieval metrics alongside Pearson Δ.

Notable claims / data

For unseen single perturbations, TxPert approaches split-half experimental reproducibility in 3 of 4 cell lines (K562, Jurkat, HepG2); it improves Pearson Δ for unseen singles by 8–25% over existing methods.
Best per-task architectures: Exphormer-MG graph transformer integrating STRING+GO+PxMap+TxMap for unseen singles; GAT-MLG for doubles. Adding the 4th graph source beats best 3-source (t-test P < 0.027).
Cell lines from Replogle genome-scale Perturb-seq (K562, RPE1, HepG2, Jurkat; >2,000 perturbations each); doubles evaluated on the Norman dataset using GEARS predefined splits.
Baselines beaten: GEARS (falls below the nonlearned general/additive baseline), scLAMBDA (GPT-3.5 text embeddings), batch-only model, and a general additive+mean statistical baseline; cross-cell-line, TxPert beats general baseline in all 4 held-out lines.
Caveats: a documented failure mode is that the model does not predict the typical downregulation of the perturbation target’s own mRNA; cross-cell-line retrieval gap to split-half reproducibility is large; trained on largely cancer-derived immortalized lines, limiting generalization to human primary tissue. Robust until >60% of STRING edges are removed.

Connections

artificial-intelligence, ai-in-genomics, foundation-models (contextualizes a “reality check” on transcriptomics foundation models such as scGPT/scVI as basal encoders).
txpert (tool entity).
Sibling single-cell perturbation methods: CMonge and foundation-model interpretation SIGnature, sharing the single-cell-perturbation-modeling concept.
knowledge-graphs as inductive-bias priors for biology.

Open questions

Can few-shot/active-learning extensions beat the zero-shot cross-cell-line setting, and will giga-scale atlases improve cross-context generalization?
How to add metrics that explicitly score conditionality/specificity of effects in novel contexts, and fix the target-gene downregulation failure mode?
Will the proprietary PxMap/TxMap graphs (and the model) be broadly reproducible/reusable outside Recursion’s data?

wiki/sources/witten-2011-understanding-s.title: witten-2011-understanding-splicing-regulation-through-rna
wiki/sources/witten-2011-understanding-s.content:
Understanding splicing regulation through RNA splicing maps
One-line: A conceptual review arguing that integrating genome-wide protein–RNA interaction data (CLIP/iCLIP) with splicing profiles into “RNA splicing maps” reveals shared, position-dependent principles by which RNA-binding proteins regulate alternative splicing.
Key takeaways

Interactions between rna-binding-proteins (RBPs) and pre-mRNA are presented as the primary regulators of alternative-splicing decisions; the review focuses on protein–RNA interactions over other modes (RNA–RNA, riboswitches, ncRNAs).
An “RNA splicing map” integrates genome-wide RBP-binding positions with splicing profiles to determine the position-dependent regulatory effect of binding, building a composite cassette exon centered on the 5’ and 3’ splice-sites and branch point.
A single genome-wide dataset is insufficient: binding sites are often far from alternative exons, motifs are degenerate (e.g. Nova’s YCAY), and many high-affinity binding events are non-functional — so independent binding AND splicing datasets must be integrated.
Across diverse, non-homologous RBPs a shared positional logic emerges: binding near branch points, splice sites, or within exons tends to silence inclusion, whereas binding downstream of exons tends to enhance inclusion. This contributes to the broader notion of a positional splicing-code / splicing-regulatory-elements grammar.
SR proteins are the notable exception, enhancing inclusion when bound within the exon; relates to splicing-factors and splicing-regulatory-elements.

Notable claims / data

Estimated proportion of human genes producing alternative isoforms rose from 35% (1999) to 94% (2008) as genome-wide depth increased.
The Dscam transcript (dscam) in Drosophila contains exon clusters of 12, 48 and 33 mutually exclusive exons that could theoretically yield 38,016 isoforms, regulated by RNA–RNA secondary structure.
Three independently derived Nova RNA splicing maps (bioinformatic+microarray; HITS-CLIP+microarray; RNA-seq for the orthologue Pasilla) differ in detail but agree on the same primary enriched-binding positions; conservation between mouse Nova and Drosophila Pasilla shows position-dependent effects are evolutionarily conserved.
PTB (ptbp1) illustrates dataset dependence: a CLIP+RT-PCR map suggested PTB mostly silences inclusion when bound downstream, while an independent microarray-based map indicated it more often enhances inclusion when bound downstream — integration of independent data is required.
Silencing RBPs near the exon: Nova, hnrnpa1 (hnRNP A1), hnRNP C/L/H, Fox, PTB, Mbnl1; enhancing RBPs downstream: Nova, hnRNP L, Fox, PTB, Mbnl1, TIA. hnRNP C (binds RNA as a homo-tetramer, ~230–240 nt) and TIA show more restricted maps.
TIA proteins interact with U1 snRNP (via U1-C); their U1 association may restrict binding to downstream positions and lets them silence distal exons by acting on splicing kinetics (H→E→A complex transitions; commitment at A complex).
Mechanistic models discussed for transcription–splicing coupling: recruitment model (SR proteins bind Pol II CTD), first-come-first-served model (challenged by fibronectin exon 33 data showing the downstream intron splices first even under slow elongation), and the splicing kinetics model (temporal window for RBP assembly governs choice).
Caveat: the review is conceptual; RNA splicing maps had been derived for only a handful of individual RBPs and combinatorial multi-RBP maps remained future work.

Connections

Foundational mechanistic complement to wiki RBP entities hnrnpa1, ptbp1 and to dscam; underpins concept pages splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code, splicing-factors, splice-sites.
Pairs with regulation-focused reviews lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative, rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing and choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of as an earlier, CLIP-era account of position-dependent regulation.
The CLIP/iCLIP methodology it surveys is the experimental basis later mined by computational splicing-code efforts surveyed in capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.
Touches cotranscriptional-splicing and splicing-and-chromatin via the recruitment / kinetics / first-come-first-served coupling models.

Open questions

Can combinatorial RNA splicing maps (multiple RBPs on the same pre-mRNA, e.g. via high-resolution iCLIP) capture competitive/cooperative regulation across the genome?
What biochemical mechanism explains the shared “enhance when downstream” principle — U1 snRNP stabilization, TIA-mediated complexes, RNA-structure change, or intron-definition looping?
Is fast splicing kinetics of flanking introns a general feature of exons sensitive to distal RBP binding or transcriptional elongation?
How do SR-protein RNA splicing maps (binding both exons and introns) refine or break the basic positional rules?

wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.title: wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source
wiki/sources/wright-2022-alternative-spl.content:
Alternative splicing as a source of phenotypic diversity
One-line: A Perspective arguing that alternative-splicing is a dynamic, evolutionarily labile process that — despite mostly producing non-functional transcripts — can fuel adaptation, speciation and organismal complexity.
Key takeaways

alternative-splicing generates multiple transcripts (and protein isoforms) per gene, expanding coding capacity and enabling quantitative regulation of protein levels; ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS.
The relative importance of AS event types varies by lineage: exon skipping dominates in metazoans, intron retention in plants and fungi. AS prevalence correlates with organism complexity (splicing-and-evolution).
The mode of spliceosome assembly shapes regulation: intron definition tends to give intron retention; exon definition (more prevalent in animals) gives exon skipping/inclusion and yields viable alternative protein-coding isoforms.
Most AS events are probably noise/errors and non-conserved, but a functional subset diversifies the proteome and is tied to development; coupled AS–NMD via splicing-and-disease-relevant poison exons drives autoregulation of splicing-factors.
Novel splice patterns arise via cis-effects (exon duplication, transition, exonization, intronization at splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements) and trans-effects (mutations in splicing-factors / rna-binding-proteins); cis effects appear more common in interspecies divergence.
Divergent splicing underpins documented adaptations and contributes to splicing-and-evolution and speciation, often by producing non-functional truncated proteins rather than new functions.

Notable claims / data

An estimated 90–95% of human genes undergo AS; 88% of GWAS heritability-associated variants are in non-coding regions.
Long-read RNA-seq identified 7,874 complete transcript isoforms of Dscam1, thousands essential for neural circuit development.
~75% of human exon-skipping events seen by RNA-seq were also detected by ribosome profiling; ~40% of RNA-level splicing changes were concordant with translation changes in human glia/glioma.
Of one-third of human tissue-specific AS isoforms detected proteomically, 95% were highly conserved across vertebrates. Mutations in consensus splice sites account for ~10% of human inherited disease.
FOXP1 exons 18/18b: AS switch controls DNA-binding and pluripotency (OCT4, NANOG) vs differentiation; conserved across 46 vertebrate species (splicing-code).
Turtle temperature-dependent sex determination: CLK1/4 phosphorylates SR proteins (active <26°C → male; inactive >31°C → female), e.g. intron retention in chromatin modifier Jarid2 (splicing-and-chromatin).
Drosophila sex-determination cascade: Sxl → tra → dsx/fru/msl-2; dsx sex-specific splicing conserved across insects (Bombyx mori, Bactrocera oleae, Tribolium castaneum).
Stickleback MSX2A: a single exon-1 mutation creates a poly-G/ISE that activates an alternative 5′ splice site → frameshift + PTC → truncated protein → shorter spines in freshwater fish.
Vampire bat (Desmodus rotundus) TRPV1: trigeminal-specific exon 14a inclusion (PTC) yields a short isoform activating ~30°C, enabling infrared prey detection while full-length heat sensing is retained in dorsal root ganglia (~40% short transcripts in TG).
Capsella rubella selfing: two SNPs make CYP724A1 introns splice efficiently → more brassinosteroid → smaller petals (outbreeding→selfing transition).
Cavefish (Astyanax mexicanus) per2 exon-21 skipping (PTC) → truncated Per2 → enhanced lipogenesis via Pparγ; convergent in a second cavefish and Phreatichthys andruzzi.
TBXT (hominoid tail loss, preprint): Alu insertion in intron 6 forms a stem-loop driving ~50% exon-6 skipping; mouse heterozygotes lose tails; linked to neural tube defects.

Connections

alternative-splicing, splicing, spliceosome — core machinery and the central subject.
splice-sites, splicing-regulatory-elements, splicing-code — weak splice sites of alternative exons, enhancers/silencers, and RBP-interpreted codes (rna-binding-proteins, splicing-factors).
cotranscriptional-splicing — splicing is largely co-transcriptional; nascent-RNA data show long introns spliced before downstream exon transcription.
splicing-and-chromatin — chromatin marks/Pol II processivity affect AS; SR proteins regulate Jarid2 in turtle sex determination.
splicing-and-evolution, splicing-and-disease — divergent splicing, drift vs selection, and disease links (consensus-site mutations, poison exons, myelodysplasia).

Open questions

What fraction of AS events are genuinely functional vs noise, and how to distinguish them experimentally (CRISPR, long-read, single-cell)?
Relative contribution of cis vs trans regulatory mutations to AS evolution remains unclear.
Whether NMD acts as an evolutionary capacitor buffering AS variation needs experimental evidence.
How AS divergence relates causally to speciation and adaptation in most documented cases is still untested.
AS scaling with complexity rests on small species samples and needs broader validation across the tree of life.

wiki/sources/wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method.title: wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify
wiki/sources/wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method.content:
SpliceTrap: a method to quantify alternative splicing under single cellular conditions
One-line: Introduces splicetrap, a Bayesian method that quantifies local exon-inclusion ratios from paired-end RNA-seq, treating each exon as an independent inference problem rather than reconstructing full-length transcripts.
Key takeaways

SpliceTrap estimates the inclusion level of every exon from paired-end RNA-seq using a Bayesian inference framework, in contrast to full-transcript tools that infer isoform expression.
It works under a single cellular condition without requiring a background/reference set of reads to call relative splicing changes — useful for building maps of AS activity within one sample.
It covers the major classes of alternative splicing (see alternative-splicing): cassette exons (CA), alternative 5’ splice sites (AD), alternative 3’ splice sites (AA), and intron retention (IR).
Relies on a precompiled human exon database, TXdb, built from RefSeq (hg18) and the EST-based AS database dbCASE, decomposed into 3-exon “trios” (and “duos” for AD/AA/IR) used as independent mappable units.
Outputs the inclusion ratio (analogous to percent-spliced-in) for each exon; the authors position it as a tool to study AS regulation and to help discover nearby cis-regulatory elements.

Notable claims / data

TXdb (hg18) totals 224,995 exon trios/duos spanning 182,560 human exons: 167,445 cassette-exon (CA) candidates (11,812 annotated CA, 155,633 constitutive/CS to be tested), plus 8,667 AA, 4,838 AD, and 1,170 IR candidates.
Trio/duo representation captures 74.14% of RefSeq single-exon AS events (64.2% when ESTs included) as a single trio/duo; +9.82% (RefSeq) describable by combining two TXdb entries; CA/AA/AD/IR together account for ~75% of known human AS events in RefSeq.
Simulation accuracy (Pearson correlation, 36 nt / 75 nt reads): SpliceTrap 0.87 / 0.83, MLE (uniform priors) 0.84 / 0.79, Cufflinks 0.83 / 0.78, RPKM 0.76 / 0.75, Scripture 0.72 / 0.61. Lowest mean absolute error too (0.11 / 0.13). 5555 simulations, 1000 repeats each.
The full Bayesian model (with FSM fragment-size prior and IRM inclusion-ratio prior, smoothed with beta distributions) beats the maximum-likelihood-only variant, evidencing the value of priors.
On >60M 36-nt paired-end HeLa reads: inclusion-ratio distribution is “U”-shaped (exons mostly fully included or fully skipped). Replicate reproducibility: SpliceTrap PCC 0.74–0.77 across thresholds while reporting 97,068 exons; Cufflinks reaches PCC 0.70 only at FPKM=10 with just 11,606 exons; Scripture is reproducible (0.83–0.92) but averages ratios ~0.5 (implausibly calling most exons alternative).
SpliceTrap inclusion ratios are independent of expression level (constant ~0.95), whereas Cufflinks ratios decline with expression — evidence SpliceTrap is insensitive to transcript abundance.
Practical: ~3 h per lane (20×2M 36-nt pairs) on a 2 GHz AMD CPU, <500 MB RAM, 10–30 GB disk; supports SGE qsub; available via CSH Galaxy and at rulai.cshl.edu/splicetrap.
Caveats: no ab initio exon prediction (limited to ~200k annotated exons in TXdb); limited power on complex multi-exon AS; assumes uniform read distribution within a trio/duo; slight 3’-end bias from dbCASE-unique exons (~6%).

Connections

Computes exon-level inclusion ratios, the same quantity later standardized as percent-spliced-in (PSI); conceptually a precursor in the lineage of splicing-methods for RNA-seq AS quantification.
Complements/precedes rmats (Shen 2014), which also quantifies exon inclusion and detects differential splicing from RNA-seq — see shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection; SpliceTrap differs by scoring single conditions without a background set.
Benchmarked against full-transcript tools Cufflinks and Scripture, which it treats as the state of the art for isoform-level quantification at the time.
Authored by the Krainer and M.Q. Zhang groups; relevant to the broader splicing-regulation toolkit surveyed in sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying and capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the.

Open questions

How does SpliceTrap’s trio/duo Bayesian inclusion ratio compare quantitatively to the modern PSI estimators (rMATS, MISO, SUPPA) on the same data?
Could TXdb be regenerated on a current human assembly with junction-mapper-derived novel exons to remove the hg18 / dbCASE 3’-bias limitations?
How much does the uniform-read-distribution assumption bias estimates for exons with strong coverage non-uniformity, and would a learned coverage model help?

wiki/sources/wu-2026-single-cell-proteom.title: wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of
wiki/sources/wu-2026-single-cell-proteom.content:
Single-cell proteomic landscape of the developing human brain
One-line: Label-free single-cell mass spectrometry resolves quantitative proteomes (~800 proteins/cell) of individual cells from the prenatal human cortex, revealing pervasive transcriptome–proteome discordance that pinpoints the intermediate progenitor-to-neuron transition as an autism-vulnerable window.
Key takeaways

An optimized workflow couples label-free single-cell-proteomics (one MS run per cell, Orbitrap Eclipse Tribrid, DIA-NN quantification) with precise sample prep to profile very small primary human cells, including ~7–10 μm prenatal neurons (~5–50 pg protein) — far below inputs validated in prior single-cell MS.
Achieves deep coverage (~800 proteins/cell on average) across major developing-brain cell types, the largest label-free single-cell proteomics dataset of primary human cells to date.
Documents extensive, cell-type-specific transcriptome–proteome discordance, strongest in neurodevelopmental-disorder genes; proteins are markedly more cell-type-specific than their mRNAs.
Reconstructs a proteome-level RG→IPC→EN trajectory and identifies a co-expression module (module 4) activating at the intermediate-progenitor-to-neuron transition that is evolutionarily conserved and enriched for autism risk — flagging this transition as a genetically vulnerable phase.
Argues mRNA is an imperfect proxy for protein; direct single-cell proteomics is needed to localize disease mechanisms to specific cell types.

Notable claims / data

2,310 independent MS runs → 2,310 single-cell quantitative proteomes (post-QC); ~800 proteins/protein-groups per cell on average (excluding erythroid cells).
Samples: fresh prenatal human brain at gestational weeks (GW) 13, 15, 19; GW15/GW19 microdissected into germinal zone (GZ) and cortical plate (CP); GW13 whole frontal telencephalon. FACS into low-bind microwells, label-free LC–MS.
1,505 brain cells used for cell-type analysis; cell types: RG, oRG, IPC–EN, EN, CGE-INs, OPC, microglia, vascular; coverage comparable to typical scRNA-seq gene coverage (1,000–2,000 genes); contrasts with 4,000–6,000 proteins/cell in cultured/cancer lines.
Cell diameter ~7–10 μm (fetal cortical neuron somata); prior primary single-cell MS targeted size-advantaged cells (cardiomyocytes 100–150 μm, hepatocytes 20–50 μm).
Bulk benchmark: 28 bulk proteomes, avg 8,042 proteins/sample. Paired scRNA-seq: 31,639 cells; plus population-scale snRNA-seq reference (232,328 cells).
Discordance validated by RNAscope/immunostaining: TBR1, SCGN, TRIM33 (R=0.93 with VGLUT1), ZBTB18, CHD3 (EN-restricted protein despite broad mRNA), MAPT, MKI67 (protein half-life ~1–1.5 h vs mRNA ~5 h). Even in ENs (highest concordance), mRNA–protein Spearman ρ=0.36 (<0.4).
Two discordance groups: group A (low RNA/high protein, n=143, high translational efficiency) and group B (high RNA/low protein, n=159, dosage-sensitive, high pLI, enriched for SFARI confidence-1 ASD genes and de novo truncating mutations in ASD/NDD).
New method ORBIT (Observability-Regularized, Biochemistry-Integrated Transformation) adjusts DIA-NN intensities for detectability for within-condition cross-protein ranking. WGCNA along pseudotime → 6 protein modules; modules 4 and 5 evolutionarily conserved (low dN/dS) and ASD-associated.

Connections

functional-single-cell-genomics — this is the single-cell-omics atlas anchor for the new topic.
single-cell-proteomics — the primary methodology this paper advances (label-free single-cell mass spectrometry).
Pairs with single-cell/single-nucleus RNA-seq atlases of the developing human neocortex (Kriegstein/Li lab) as a protein-level complement.
Disease-genomics connections: SFARI autism genes, de novo mutation cohorts, NDD/ASD risk genes (CHD3, PHIP, NOVA2, module-4 genes).

Open questions

Cross-modal comparisons were pseudobulk (cell-type level), not within the same single cell — can true same-cell RNA+protein co-measurement confirm the discordance map?
Coverage (~800 proteins/cell) and soluble-protein bias mean membrane proteins and chromatin remodelers are under-recovered — how much biology is missed?
Findings limited to early/midgestation cortex and few donors/regions; do they generalize to later development and other brain regions?
Will throughput-oriented approaches (plexDIA, isobaric multiplexing) preserve the quantitative fidelity that one-cell-per-run achieves here?

wiki/sources/yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-.title: yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions
wiki/sources/yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-.content:
Explicit dynamic cross-strand interactions for DNA sequence language modelling
One-line: crossdna is a parameter-efficient DNA language model that explicitly and dynamically models cross-strand (forward/reverse-complement) interactions, achieving orientation-robust, competitive performance against far larger DNA foundation models.
Key takeaways

Existing dna-language-models either treat DNA as unidirectional text (single-strand: NTv2, HyenaDNA, GROVER, DNABERT-2) or enforce reverse-complement (RC) symmetry implicitly/statically via data augmentation or equivariant architectures (Caduceus, JanusDNA). CrossDNA instead moves symmetry enforcement to the representation layer with explicit, context-dependent interstrand information exchange.
Architecture: a dual-branch model (Students A and B, identical architecture, non-shared parameters) where forward and RC segments are alternately routed to branches in a rotating (“cross-view”) schedule. Backbone combines the Comba bilinear recurrent module (long-range memory) with sliding-window attention (SWA, window 512, FlashAttention-2). A lightweight TokenBridge module exchanges cross-strand context at token resolution; a temperature-controlled gated fusion merges strands; EMA teacher self-distillation stabilizes representations against rotation-induced semantic discontinuity.
Single-nucleotide tokenization; pretrained on human reference genome GRCh38 with a BERT-style masked-LM objective (15% masked). Variants at 8.1M, 71.6M, 519M parameters (plus a 408K compact variant for matched-scale Genomic Benchmark).
Central claim: explicit dual-strand modelling delivers near-orientation-invariant predictions (forward vs RC scores tightly aligned) without data augmentation or hard equivariance constraints, plus strong parameter efficiency.
Adaptable to classification, regression, prediction, and zero-shot embedding tasks; supports interpretability (in silico mutagenesis), non-coding disease-variant prioritization, and de novo enhancer discovery.

Notable claims / data

Genomic Benchmark (8 tasks, matched compact ~264K–470K params): CrossDNA (408K) best on 5/8 tasks, mean accuracy 88.2% (top overall). Same 408K variant vs much larger models: 88.2% vs GROVER 86.4% (87M), AlphaGenome 85.9% (450M), NTv2 84.4% (500M), Evo2 82.4% (7B) — i.e. 2–4 orders of magnitude fewer params.
NTv2 benchmark (18 datasets, forward+RC reported): CrossDNA (8.1M) best/second-best on 33/36 strand-specific evaluations. Orientation gaps stayed below 0.042 vs e.g. NTv2 dropping to 0.176 on RC Splice-all (0.797 orientation gap) — illustrating CrossDNA’s orientation robustness; splice-site F1 up to 0.987.
Chromatin profiles (DeepSEA, 919 features): +1.9% mean AUC over GROVER; exceeds task-specific DeepATT. DNALONGBENCH eQTL (450,000-bp window): 9 tissues, AUC mean 0.841, up to +26% over Enformer/HyenaDNA/Caduceus.
Dual- vs single-strand contexts: CrossDNA’s largest gain is in double-strand (heterochromatin) regions (+17.90% over single-strand baseline); +37.3% ΔMCC for splice-site prediction in antisense-transcribed regions.
Caveats: poor at individual-level expression prediction (eQTL effect sizes near zero), attributed to single-reference-genome pretraining; regression shrinkage at high-activity enhancers; histone-mark prediction not optimal; scaling 71.6M→519M shows attenuating, near-saturating gains.
Interpretability: ISM recovers TF motifs; prioritizes a CHD-associated variant (rs113716316) linking the AHDC1 locus to a cardiac enhancer; discovers 748 high-confidence K562 enhancer candidates enriched for haematopoietic motifs.

Connections

Core entity: crossdna.
A foundation-models instance specialized for genomics — sits within ai-in-genomics and the broader dna-language-models lineage; explicitly benchmarked against DNABERT-2, NTv2, HyenaDNA, GROVER, Caduceus, JanusDNA, AlphaGenome, Evo2, Enformer.
Sequence-to-regulatory-function modelling connects to the splicing-code / spliceai lineage: CrossDNA reports splice-site acceptor/donor prediction as a downstream task and shows large gains for splice sites in antisense regions, extending the sequence-model approach to regulatory grammar from a cross-strand perspective.
A generative-ai adjacency: evaluated on DREAM-generated synthetic enhancers, bridging generative sequence design and discriminative scoring.

Open questions

Does population-aware pretraining (multi-genome data, variant-frequency-weighted masking) close the gap on individual-level/eQTL effect-size prediction?
Would parameter-sharing between the two branches improve lagging tasks (histone marks) without sacrificing orientation invariance?
How much of the orientation robustness and parameter efficiency transfers to out-of-distribution species and complex multilabel regulatory tasks?

wiki/sources/zhang-2019-deep-learning-au.title: zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis
wiki/sources/zhang-2019-deep-learning-au.content:
Deep-learning augmented RNA-seq analysis of transcript splicing
One-line: Introduces DARTS, a computational framework that couples a deep neural network trained on large-scale public RNA-seq data with a Bayesian hypothesis test, so that differential alternative splicing can be inferred reliably even from low-coverage RNA-seq.
Key takeaways

A central limitation of RNA-seq splicing analysis is its reliance on high sequencing depth; genes with low expression cannot be reliably quantified. DARTS addresses this by injecting deep-learning priors into the inference.
DARTS has two core parts: the DARTS DNN, which predicts differential splicing between two conditions from exon-specific cis sequence features plus trans RBP mRNA levels; and the DARTS BHT, a Bayesian hypothesis-testing model that combines that prediction (as a prior) with observed RNA-seq read counts (as the likelihood).
Two BHT modes: BHT(flat) uses an uninformative prior (RNA-seq data only, equivalent to a likelihood-ratio test) and is used during training to generate high-confidence labels; BHT(info) uses the DNN prediction as an informative prior for augmented analysis.
The DNN’s distinguishing design is that it models trans effects — it ingests RBP expression in both conditions, letting it reason about how altered RBP levels reshape splicing, unlike prior cis-only sample-specific exon-inclusion predictors.
Trained on ENCODE shRNA RBP-knockdown data and Roadmap Epigenomics tissue/cell-type data; combined training generalizes best across both leave-out sets.
DARTS uses rmats for splicing event detection and read counting, and operates on exon-inclusion levels (percent-spliced-in, psi). It builds a learned model of splicing regulation, contributing to the broader effort of a predictive splicing-code.

Notable claims / data

DARTS DNN (exon skipping) reached AUROC 0.97 in cross-validation and 0.86 in held-out testing; average AUROC 0.87 on 58 leave-out RBPs never seen in training.
DNN trained on large-scale ENCODE data beat baseline methods (per-dataset DNN, L2 logistic regression, random forest, and absolute predicted |psi_KD − psi_CTRL|) in 57 of 58 leave-out experiments; the per-dataset DNN was the worst, underscoring the value of large diverse training data.
Training set: 196 RBPs knocked down in both K562 and HepG2 (408 KD-vs-control comparisons); 58 RBPs in only one cell line held out. Feature set: 2,926 cis features (exon skipping) and 1,498 annotated RBPs (2,996 = 1,498×2 trans expression features per comparison).
BHT(info) beat BHT(flat) in all HepG2-vs-K562 pairwise comparisons; the gain was negatively correlated with RNA-seq depth (Spearman rho = −0.69, P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), i.e. biggest gains on low-coverage samples.
DNN extended beyond exon skipping to alternative 5’ splice sites, alternative 3’ splice sites, and retained introns (feature vectors of 2,973 / 2,971 / 1,748), with high leave-out accuracy.
EMT application: best AUROC 0.82 (day 6 vs day 0, combined ENCODE+Roadmap model) on an inducible H358 lung-cancer EMT time course. DARTS DNN “rescued” events were enriched for the ESRP1/2 splicing-factor consensus motif downstream of alternative exons. DNN scores between H358 and PC3E/GS689 EMT systems correlated at Spearman rho = 0.87 (P < 2.2×10⁻¹⁶), suggesting a captured core EMT splicing signature.
RASL-seq validation: DARTS DNN recovered 52 additional differential splicing events beyond the 77 called by RNA-seq alone; rescued events sat in genes with significantly lower expression (P = 0.001) and lower RNA-seq coverage (P = 2.1×10⁻⁷). BHT(info) outperformed BHT(flat), DNN alone, rMATS, and SUPPA2 on inconclusive events.
Architecture detail: skipped-exon DNN has 4 hidden layers (1,200/500/300/200 units, ReLU), Softmax output, ~7.92M parameters, dropout (0.6/0.5/0.3/0.1), batch-norm, RMSprop; Keras with Theano backend. Spike-and-slab prior with tau0 = 0.03, tau1 = 0.3; significance threshold p(|Δpsi| > 0.05) > 0.9.
Caveat: methods/labels are bootstrapped from BHT(flat) itself, so “ground truth” labels inherit that model’s assumptions; validation against independent RASL-seq partly mitigates this. ENCODE training data are modest depth (~32M read pairs/replicate, replicates pooled).

Connections

Uses and benchmarks against rmats (Shen et al. 2014, the shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection method) for event detection and as a comparison baseline, alongside MISO and SUPPA2.
Operates on percent-spliced-in (psi) quantification, the standard metric for exon inclusion used across the splicing-methods literature.
Conceptually a data-driven splicing-code model: it learns the cis + trans determinants of differential splicing, complementing sequence-only predictors such as spliceai but uniquely conditioning on RBP expression state.
Fits the methods/tools cluster of the splicing topic as a reusable software resource (darts).

Open questions

How well does the trans-RBP-expression prior transfer to primary human tissues and disease cohorts versus the cell-line / knockdown training regime?
Training labels are derived from BHT(flat) — how much do systematic errors in that labeler propagate into the learned prior?
Could modern sequence/foundation models replace the hand-compiled 2,926 cis features and improve generalization to unseen splicing contexts?
How does performance scale to long-read RNA-seq, where coverage limitations differ?

wiki/sources/zhang-2020-regional-variati.title: zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls
wiki/sources/zhang-2020-regional-variati.content:
Regional Variation of Splicing QTLs in Human Brain
One-line: A population-scale sQTL analysis across 13 human brain regions (GTEx, 1,209 samples) showing that splicing QTLs are either “regionally ubiquitous” or “regionally specific,” and that regional variation can be exploited to fine-map likely causal variants and their trans-regulators.
Key takeaways

sQTLs were mapped separately in each of 13 GTEx brain regions using exon inclusion levels (percent spliced in, PSI; see percent-spliced-in) quantified by rmats (rMATS-turbo) and WGS genotypes; between 387 and 849 significant sQTL events (p < 10⁻⁵) per region, with 133 significant across all 13 regions.
Two classes emerge: “regionally ubiquitous” sQTLs (significant in ≥10 regions; 148 total) are enriched for SNPs at the 5′/3′ essential splice sites, whereas “regionally specific” sQTLs (758 total; 86% significant in only 1–2 regions) are enriched for SNPs in exonic and proximal/distal intronic regions — pointing to distinct molecular mechanisms.
SNP position tracks significance and breadth: SNPs nearer the alternative exon (5′SS > 3′SS > exonic > proximal intronic > distal intronic) are more significant; sQTLs with SNPs at the splice-site dinucleotide are mostly pan-regional (53% significant in all 13 regions, 93% in ≥7).
An integrative strategy combining RBP binding prediction (DeepBind), region-specific RBP expression, and sQTL signal fine-maps causal cis variants and their trans regulators — illustrated for MAPT, CXXC5, and POU6F1.
The flagship example: SNP rs17651213 (G>A) creates a downstream RBFOX2 binding site that increases inclusion of MAPT (tau) exon 3, specifically in cerebellar tissues where RBFOX2 is highly expressed; the variant is in high LD with 11 PD and 1 AD GWAS variants — a concrete instance linking a brain region-specific sQTL to neurodegenerative disease (see splicing-and-disease).

Notable claims / data

Inputs: GTEx v7 RNA-seq, ultimately 1,209 samples with matched RNA-seq + WGS genotype across 13 brain regions; 6,317,213 SNPs after MAF ≥ 0.05 and sex-chromosome filtering.
Splicing events retained after filtering: 10,665 skipped exons (SE), 1,443 alternative 5′SS (A5SS), 2,434 alternative 3′SS (A3SS). Total association tests: ~111.7M (SE), ~14.7M (A5SS), ~25.1M (A3SS).
Significance: two-tier cutoff — permutation FDR < 10% (corresponding to p ≈ 10⁻⁶ per region) and uncorrected p < 10⁻⁵. sQTL SNP = closest SNP with most significant association within a 200-kb window per side.
Brain region (not age or sex) is the dominant driver of variation in expression and splicing (t-SNE + linear mixed model, FDR < 5%); validated against known KHDRBS3/KHDRBS1 (T-STAR/Sam68) regulation of neurexin exon 4.
Disease annotation via LD (r² > 0.8) to NHGRI-EBI GWAS Catalog variants across 14 neurological disorders; neurodegenerative-disease sQTLs tend to be region-specific, neuropsychiatric ones more shared. No significant enrichment of brain sQTLs in brain disorders vs non-brain phenotypes (BMI, height) — consistent with sQTL pleiotropy / sharing across tissues.
Colocalization (coloc): 278 brain sQTL-associated GWASs, 27 with harmonized summary stats; 124 tests on 43 sQTL-GWAS pairs, 77 supporting a single shared causal variant (posterior ≥ 75%) — e.g., PGAP3 exon 4 / rs1565922 with bipolar disorder.
Additional examples: RTEL1 exon 23 / rs6062302 (regionally ubiquitous, glioma GWAS); SLC26A10 exon 12 / rs1871417 (cerebellum-specific, LD with immune-disease GWAS); CXXC5 exon 2 / rs6580200 (disrupts HNRNPK site; see hnrnpa1 family context); POU6F1 exon 4 / rs4077093 (disrupts a NOVA1 YCAY motif).
Caveats stated by authors: one sQTL SNP per event ignores multiple independent causal SNPs; bulk tissue mixes cell types and may dilute power for cell-type-specific sQTLs (RBFOX2 is neuron-enriched and cerebellum is neuron-dense, so the MAPT signal may be neuronal).

Connections

Method backbone is rmats (rMATS-turbo) for PSI quantification; conceptually this is an application/population-genetics study built on splicing-quantification tooling rather than a new tool — contrast with method papers like shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection.
Extends the “splicing variation in human populations” line of work (Park/Xing 2018) into a brain-region-resolved sQTL atlas; complements disease-focused splicing reviews such as marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative and the editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer.
Mechanistic story sits squarely in splicing-and-disease, splicing-factors, and rna-binding-proteins (RBFOX2, NOVA1, HNRNPK as trans regulators); the splice-site vs regulatory-element distinction connects to splice-sites and splicing-regulatory-elements.
Uses a deep-learning RBP-binding predictor (DeepBind) for cis-variant prioritization, relevant to ai-in-genomics and splicing-code efforts to predict splicing impact of variants.

Open questions

Would purified-cell-type or single-cell population RNA-seq sharpen (or reassign) the MAPT/RBFOX2 cerebellar signal to specific neurons?
