How many sQTL events are driven by multiple independent causal SNPs that the single-SNP-per-event design misses?
Why is a cerebellum-specific sQTL (SLC26A10) in strong LD with immune-disease GWAS variants — is the same sQTL active in immune cell types?
Can the integrative RBP-motif + region-specific-expression + sQTL strategy generalize beyond brain to fine-map tissue/cell-type-specific causal variants in other systems?

wiki/sources/zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-.title: zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex
wiki/sources/zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-.content:
Digital twins of ex vivo human lungs enable accurate and personalized evaluation of therapeutic efficacy
One-line: Builds hybrid physics-informed + machine-learning digital-twins of individual ex vivo human lungs from the largest clinical ex vivo lung perfusion (EVLP) dataset, forecasting >75 multimodal parameters and serving as per-organ untreated controls to evaluate drug efficacy and safety.
Key takeaways

Uses EVLP (a closed circuit perfusing an isolated human lung at 37 °C on an ICU-grade ventilator) to generate multiscale data — physiology, biochemistry, radiography, transcriptomics, metabolomics, proteomics — then trains organ-level digital-twins (DTs) that simulate future lung function without altering circuit settings.
Hybrid architecture: physics-based lung-mechanics equations combined with data-driven models (XGBoost for hourly/imaging data, GRU for per-breath time series). One bespoke model per parameter — an instance of multimodal-learning.
Two complementary DT modes run simultaneously: a “static” twin (baseline-only, long-range forecast) and a “dynamic” twin (continuously recalibrated with real-time data, short-range forecast). Dynamic generally matches or beats static for physiology/biochemistry.
Headline application: the DT acts as a personalized, paired untreated counterfactual control for the same lung, enabling individual treatment-effect inference. Demonstrated on alteplase (tPA) for pulmonary embolism — the DT detected efficacy that a conventional control cohort missed.
A reusable resource (code, trained models, web app, demo data) rather than a single hypothesis test — the topic’s translational/biomedical multimodal “digital twin” entry, built on ex vivo organ perfusion, slightly outside pure genomics.

Notable claims / data

Cohort: 1,000 consecutive Toronto General Hospital EVLP cases (2008–2024); 49 excluded → 951 isolated human lungs for DT training (donors disease-free, mean age 46 ± 16 y, 64% male). Independent test cohort of 45 clinical EVLP cases (2024–2025). No imputation — complete-record cases only.
Accurately models >75 parameters across 6+ modalities. ~200,000 individual breaths semi-supervised-labeled; ventilator data at 100 Hz.
Performance (dynamic DT MAPE): physiology 2–11%; biochemistry 0.46–7.2% (pH MAPE 0.46%); metabolites lactate 6.9% / glucose 4.9%; transcriptomic Hallmark pathways 0.68–3% MAPE and 79–100% accuracy for direction of gene-set enrichment change (hypoxia pathway 100%); proteins (IL-6/8/10/1β) median MAPE ~22–37%. DT absolute error consistently below the %CV of an empirical 5-lung control group; no systematic bias.
Modeling scale: 216 XGBoost models (grid search) + 96 GRU configurations (20-fold CV × 5 seeds).
Therapeutic test: 16 EVLP cases received 20-mg alteplase (left out of training; 14 analyzed; n=6 suitable, n=8 unsuitable). DT-control paired analysis showed significantly lower PAP 2 h post-treatment (P=0.031, Wilcoxon) and an edema trend (P=0.063) in suitable lungs; conventional cohort analysis found nothing (PAP P=0.36/0.78). Estimated ~18 cases (3× sample size) needed to reach the same conclusion conventionally. Unsuitable lungs showed no efficacy.
Deliverables: code at github.com/Sage-Lab-ai/DT_Lung; models + demo data at huggingface.co/SageLabUHN; web app at dt-lung.streamlit.app. See entity dt-lung.

Connections

Hub: functional-single-cell-genomics — the topic’s translational/biomedical multimodal “digital twin” entry; a human-tissue platform that includes a transcriptomics (microarray Hallmark gene-set) modality but is broader than pure genomics.
New concepts: digital-twins, multimodal-learning (joint/hybrid modeling across imaging, omics, and time-series physiology via physics-informed + data-driven ML); enabling platform = ex vivo organ perfusion (EVLP).
Incorporates ML (XGBoost/GRU/ResNet50) but does not invoke foundation models; connects loosely to ai-in-genomics via the transcriptomic-forecasting component.

Open questions

Generalizability beyond a single center and disease-free donor lungs; external validation still required. How well does the framework transfer to other ex vivo organs (liver, heart, kidney)?
Transcriptomics limited to <100 cases and microarray Hallmark gene sets — would single-cell/full RNA-seq sharpen the genomic modality?
Forecasts cover only the first 3 h; can longer EVLP enable evaluation of slower therapeutics?
DT models the target organ only (no whole-body biodistribution/off-target safety); data are not publicly shareable (re-identification concerns), limiting reproducibility to request-based access.

wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.title: alternative-splicing-functionality-debate
wiki/syntheses/alternative-splicing-func.content:
Alternative Splicing: Functional or Noise? (Contradiction resolution)
Resolves the flag raised in overview and alternative-splicing:
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative argues most alternative-splicing
is functional, while wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source argues
most events are probably noise (with a conserved functional subset). Grounded
strictly in the ingested sources.
Verdict
Not a genuine factual contradiction — largely a difference in (1) the definition
of “functional”, (2) the denominator being counted, and (3) which evidence is
weighted. Both reviews actually agree on the central empirical points; they
disagree on how to classify the large tail of low-abundance, non-conserved
isoforms, which neither can yet settle with available data.
Where they actually AGREE

Prevalence: ~90–95% of human genes undergo AS
(wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source,
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative).
A conserved, functional core exists and drives development and physiology —
both accept this. Wright: of proteomically-detected tissue-specific isoforms,
95% are highly conserved across vertebrates; Marasco: highly conserved
neural microexons (SRRM4). Both treat conservation as evidence of function.
The same ribosome-profiling number: ~75% of (medium/high-abundance)
exon-skipping mRNAs are ribosome-engaged — this figure appears in both reviews
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source).
A low-abundance error tail exists: deep sequencing detects rare isoforms
that may be spliceosome errors (lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative) —
Marasco doesn’t deny this; he denies it’s the majority of meaningful events.

Why the headline conclusions differ
1. Different definition of “functional”

Marasco ≈ reflected at the protein level / has a physiological role. He
emphasizes that mass-spec undercounts real isoforms (“absence of evidence is
not evidence of absence”) and that single-gene physiology (Dscam, CLK1 thermo-
sensing, PKM/Warburg, TRPV1, hTERT) shows pervasive function.
Wright ≈ encodes a preserved/selected protein function. Crucially, Wright
notes many adaptive AS events work by producing non-functional truncated
proteins (PTC-bearing) — stickleback MSX2A, cavefish per2, bat TRPV1.
So in Wright’s framing a transcript can be “non-functional” (at the protein
level) yet phenotypically and evolutionarily consequential. This dissolves
much of the apparent conflict: “non-functional protein” ≠ “no effect”.

2. Different denominator

Wright counts all RNA-seq-detected events, including the large pool of
low-abundance, non-conserved, lineage-specific variants → “most are noise.”
Marasco focuses on medium/high-abundance events and conserved switches →
“most [that matter] are functional.” They are describing different slices of the
same distribution.

3. Different evidence weighting

Marasco leans on ribosome profiling + the insensitivity of proteomics.
Wright leans on cross-species conservation as the functionality filter, and
treats AS as an evolutionarily labile, mostly-drift process from which
selection occasionally recruits useful variants.

Synthesised position
A defensible reconciliation consistent with all three sources:

~90–95% of human genes undergo AS, but isoform output is a steep
abundance/conservation distribution.
The head (abundant, conserved, often tissue-specific) is largely
functional — and both reviews agree here.
The long tail (rare, non-conserved) is dominated by stochastic
spliceosome noise/error — Wright’s emphasis, compatible with Lee’s error caveat.
“Functional” must be split into senses: translated stable protein /
conserved-selected / quantitative regulator via AS–NMD
poison exons (even untranslated isoforms regulate protein level — both accept).
Disagreement shrinks once the sense is fixed.

Residual genuine uncertainty
The real open question — what fraction of the tail is functional — is
unresolved because current tools can’t settle it. Both reviews propose the same
fixes: long-read sequencing, single-cell, and CRISPR perturbation to test
isoforms directly (marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source). Until then this is a
difference of emphasis over an unmeasured tail, not a contradiction of fact.
Sources

marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — “mostly functional”; proteomics
undercounts; ribosome profiling; physiological case studies; long-read proposal
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source — “mostly noise + conserved
subset”; conservation as filter; adaptation via non-functional truncated proteins
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — low-abundance isoforms may
be spliceosome errors (the error-tail caveat)

wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.title: cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing
wiki/syntheses/cotranscriptional-vs-post.content:
Co-transcriptional vs Post-transcriptional Splicing
A comparison of the two timing modes of intron removal. Grounded in the locally
ingested sources. See the concept pages cotranscriptional-splicing and
post-transcriptional-splicing.
The core distinction
Both modes use the same spliceosome and the same assembly steps
(E → A → B → Bact/B* → C); what differs is when splicing happens relative to
transcription and 3′-end processing
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).

Co-transcriptional — intron removed during synthesis, while the nascent
pre-mRNA is still tethered to elongating RNA Pol II.
Post-transcriptional — removal completes after transcription and
polyadenylation, typically while the polyadenylated transcript is still
chromatin-associated.

Side-by-side


















































AxisCo-transcriptionalPost-transcriptionalTimingDuring transcription, on nascent RNAAfter transcription + polyadenylationLocationElongating Pol II / chromatinChromatin-associated (poly(A)+ RNA), nuclear specklesExtent~70% of mammalian introns excised on chromatin (much co-transcriptional)~27–45% of introns (up to ~40% retained after termination); >70% of genes have ≥1SpliceosomeIdentical assembly stepsIdentical assembly steps (only timing differs)Primary control knobPol II elongation rate (kinetic coupling) + chromatincis features (GC, length, ss strength, RNA structure) + trans factors + modificationsMechanistic model”Window of opportunity”: slow Pol II favors inclusion of class I / skipping of class II exonsDelay buys time for splice-site choice; splicing completion gates transcript releaseFunctional consequenceSets alternative-splicing outcomes via kineticsGates nuclear export & translation; regulatory reservoir (detained introns)Key sourcesmarasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative, lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternativechoquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of
What drives each

Co-transcriptional → kinetic coupling. Splice-site choice depends on
cis-splicing-regulatory-elements, trans splicing-factors, and Pol II
elongation rate (~3–4 kb/min). A slow polymerase widens the window for an
upstream splice site to be recognized before a competitor
appears — slow elongation promotes class I exon inclusion and class II exon
skipping. Elongation is itself tuned by chromatin
(histone marks, nucleosomes) and the Pol II CTD
(marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative).
Post-transcriptional → delayed, gated removal. Timing is set by cis
features (GC content, intron length, splice-site strength, RNA secondary
structure) and trans factors/rna-binding-proteins (PTBP1, SR proteins, U2
snRNA, SNIP1), modulated by CLK/SRPK phosphorylation, PRMT methylation and m6A.
Detained introns are a specialized held-until-signaled class (neurons,
spermatogenesis, erythropoiesis, T-cell activation, heat shock)
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).

How they relate (not a strict binary)
The two are endpoints of a timing continuum, not mutually exclusive
categories — a single transcript’s introns can be removed at different times
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of). Notably, introns flanking
alternative exons are preferentially post-transcriptional (67% vs 30% for
constitutive introns in mESCs), plausibly buying time for correct splice-site
choice — so the post-transcriptional delay is itself a regulatory device feeding
alternative-splicing (choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of).
Supporting data (post-transcriptional)

Median chromatin half-life of mRNA ~50 min in K562 (some genes >170 min),
often exceeding transcription time itself.
A β-globin reporter intron was excised ~10× faster and more often (87%) after
release from the transcription site; for 6 of 10 endogenous human introns
post-transcriptional excision was more probable than co-transcriptional.
Most retained introns are eventually spliced productively; nuclear decay is the
minority fate (~4% of genes, “PUND” transcripts).
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Open questions

What triggers the eventual post-transcriptional reaction, and does the delay
occur before or after spliceosome engagement?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)
Do exons regulated by transcription rate (co-transcriptional kinetics) overlap
with those regulated by delayed removal of flanking introns?
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of)

Sources

choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of — primary: extent, kinetics,
detained introns, release gating, determinants of post-transcriptional splicing
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative — kinetic coupling model;
class I/II exons; elongation–chromatin link
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative — identical assembly
steps; Pol II ~3–4 kb/min; slow-polymerase effects

wiki/topics/3d-genome.title: 3d-genome
wiki/topics/3d-genome.content:
3D Genome
Topic hub for the three-dimensional organization of the genome — how
chromatin folds in the nucleus (loops → TADs → compartments), how that
architecture is measured, and how it evolves. This is the wiki’s third topic
(alongside splicing and artificial-intelligence); it will grow as more
sources are ingested.
Why it matters
The nuclear genome is folded into precise 3D structures that compact DNA while
controlling access to genetic information — tuning enhancer–promoter proximity
and therefore gene regulation. Treating this genome-architecture as an
evolvable trait reveals how form and function co-vary across scales
(che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome).
Sources (1)















SourceYearAngleche-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome2026Micro-to-macro framework for 3D-genome evolution: cancer, between species, across kingdoms (Trends in Genetics)
Concept map

The hierarchy — genome-architecture · chromatin-compartments · topologically-associating-domains · chromatin-loops
How we measure it — chromatin-conformation-capture
How it changes — 3d-genome-evolution · cis-regulatory-elements · 3d-genome-and-disease

Current synthesis (1 source)

Architecture is an evolvable trait across scales. A micro-to-macro
framework links the diversity of genome folding to the evolution of form and
function (3d-genome-evolution).
Cancer = fastest 3D evolution. Frequent mutation + plastic epigenetic
marks rapidly rewire loops, TADs and enhancer–promoter contacts; a transient
“structural bottleneck” can funnel cells toward higher fitness
(3d-genome-and-disease).
Close species diverge through sequence, not structure. Architectural
divergence between related species is locus-restricted and driven mainly by
cis-regulatory-elements within conserved folding frameworks — only the
information that re-forms structures is heritable, not the structures
themselves.
Convergence across kingdoms. Similar higher-order architectures recur via
distinct molecular machinery (e.g. insect insulators, ctenophore CTEP1/2,
bacterial NAPs) — selection acts on function, not components. Plants cluster
genes linearly and lack CTCF / canonical compartments.

Open questions

How does architectural complexity relate causally to organismal complexity
(shown mainly within animals)? (che-2026-multiscale-evolution-of-the-3d-genome)
What are the distinct mechanisms behind convergent higher-order features?


Cross-topic note: chromatin and Pol II elongation also shape splicing
(see splicing-and-chromatin) — genome architecture touches gene regulation
broadly.


Claims grounded in the locally ingested source only.

wiki/topics/artificial-intelligence.title: artificial-intelligence
wiki/topics/artificial-intelligence.content:
Artificial Intelligence
Topic hub for AI/ML in biology and genomics — both the methods (foundation
models and deep learning for sequences, structures, single cells, and
perturbations) and the safety, dual-use, and governance questions they raise.
The wiki’s second topic (alongside splicing and
functional-single-cell-genomics).
Why it matters
Since the transformer (2017), foundation-models for biological sequences (DNA,
RNA, protein) and single cells have become central tools in genomics. They now span
structure prediction, regulatory-grammar modelling, mutation-effect prediction, and
perturbation response — while the same generative capability that designs beneficial
molecules can lower the barrier to harmful ones, keeping innovation vs safety in
tension (hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).
Sources (7)





























































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation2026Safety-centric view of generative AI in genomics — dual-use, bias, governance (Trends in Genetics)✗✗wang-2026-predicting-rna-3d-structure2026trrosettarna2 — rna-structure-prediction via a pre-trained SS prior + structure-aware attention (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓han-2026-generalizable-mutation-effect-prediction-across2026uniair — multimodal mutation-effect-prediction across immune-recognition (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable2026cmonge — conditional optimal-transport for single-cell-perturbation-modeling (Nat Mach Intell)✗✓yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions2026crossdna — cross-strand [[dna-language-modelsDNA language model]] (Nat Mach Intell)✗wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge2026txpert — knowledge-graphs for transcriptomic perturbation prediction (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓gold-2026-scoring-gene-importance-by-interpreting2026signature-tool (SIGnature) — model-interpretability of single-cell-foundation-models (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓
Concept map

Sequence & structure models — foundation-models · generative-ai · ai-in-genomics · dna-language-models · protein-language-models · rna-structure-prediction
Single cells & perturbation — single-cell-foundation-models · single-cell-perturbation-modeling · optimal-transport · knowledge-graphs · model-interpretability
Tasks & techniques — mutation-effect-prediction · immune-recognition · multimodal-learning
Risks & response — dual-use-risk · biosecurity · data-quality-and-bias · ai-safety · ai-governance

Key entities

Models / tools — trrosettarna2 · uniair · cmonge · crossdna · txpert · signature-tool · spliceai

Current synthesis (across the 7 sources)

Foundation models are now general-purpose biological tools. The same paradigm
spans DNA language models (crossdna), protein language models (uniair),
RNA structure (trrosettarna2), and single-cell embeddings (the FMs
signature-tool interprets) — a clear arc from the splicing-code/spliceai
origins into every biomolecular modality.
Inject domain priors, win efficiency. The strongest methods bake biology into
the architecture rather than scaling blindly: secondary-structure priors
(trrosettarna2), explicit cross-strand symmetry (crossdna, 408K params
beating 7B Evo2), gene knowledge-graphs (txpert), and optimal-transport
structure (cmonge). Several beat models orders of magnitude larger.
Generalization to the unseen is the shared frontier. Unseen drugs/doses
(cmonge), unseen perturbations and cell lines (txpert), unseen mutations
(uniair), and unseen RNA conformers (trrosettarna2) — all foreground OOD
generalization, and all hit limits tied to data-quality-and-bias.
Interpretation and measurement check the models. signature-tool turns FM
embeddings into testable gene-importance hypotheses (model-interpretability);
and across the wiki, direct measurement (massively-parallel-reporter-assay,
ΔPSI) still beats prediction where models are weak.
Safety stays the backdrop. Capability gains (immunogen/antibody design in
uniair, sequence generation) keep dual-use-risk live; no single safeguard
suffices, motivating layered ai-safety + ai-governance
(hassan-2026-a-safety-centric-perspective-on-innovation).

Open questions

How to govern foundation-model development that largely does not yet meet “high-risk” standards? (ai-governance)
How to make safeguards robust to novel sequences, not just known threats? (ai-safety)
Can sequence models predict individual-level and activating effects, where they currently fail? (yang-2026-explicit-dynamic-cross-strand-interactions, groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at)
Will single-cell perturbation models generalize across cell types and beat simple baselines? (driessen-2026-conditional-monge-gap-enables-generalizable, wenkel-2026-txpert-using-multiple-knowledge)


Cross-topic notes: the splicing-code / spliceai connection links this topic
to splicing; and functional-single-cell-genomics supplies the atlases,
variant maps, and platforms these models learn from and are tested against.


Claims grounded in the locally ingested sources only.

wiki/topics/functional-single-cell-genom.title: functional-single-cell-genomics
wiki/topics/functional-single-cell-genom.content:
Functional & Single-cell Genomics
Topic hub for experimental and resource-generating approaches that measure
biological function at single-cell, single-variant, or whole-organ resolution —
the wet-lab/measurement counterpart to the computational methods under
artificial-intelligence. Where that topic is about models, this one is about
maps, atlases, and platforms that those models learn from and are validated
against.
Why it matters
Genome sequence alone does not reveal function. This topic collects high-throughput
ways to measure it directly: protein abundance in single cells
(single-cell-proteomics), the expression effect of thousands of regulatory
variants (massively-parallel-reporter-assay), and the multimodal behavior of an
intact human organ (digital-twins). A recurring lesson across them is that
direct measurement still beats prediction in regimes where models are weak
(e.g. activating cis-regulatory variants, mRNA→protein inference).
Sources (3)

































SourceYearAnglehuman?resource?wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of2026single-cell-proteomics atlas of the developing human brain; transcriptome–proteome discordance & autism window (Nat Biotechnol, Resource)✓✓groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at2026massively-parallel-reporter-assay of >30k cis-regulatory mutations in sorghum (plant); cis-regulatory-grammar for crop edits (Nat Biotechnol)✗✓zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex2026digital-twins of ex vivo human lungs (EVLP); personalized therapy evaluation (Nat Biotechnol)✓✓
Concept map

Single-cell omics — single-cell-proteomics
Functional genomics — massively-parallel-reporter-assay · cis-regulatory-grammar · cis-regulatory-elements
Translational / platforms — digital-twins · multimodal-learning

Key entities

Platforms / tools — dt-lung

Current synthesis (across the 3 sources)

Measurement beats prediction where models are weakest. Direct MPRA measurement
out-predicts a fine-tuned genomic language model for activating regulatory edits
(groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at); single-cell proteomics
exposes mRNA–protein discordance that transcriptomics cannot
(wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of).
Resources first. All three papers are reusable resources (an atlas, a
variant-effect map, a digital-twin platform) more than single hypothesis tests —
the resource: true tag holds across the topic.
Human vs non-human split. Two are centrally human (developing brain, ex vivo
lungs); one is a crop plant (sorghum) — captured by the human_research tag.
Bridges to AI. Each is also a substrate for the artificial-intelligence
topic: atlases train single-cell-foundation-models, variant maps test
sequence models, and digital twins are themselves multimodal-learning.

Open questions

Can same-cell multi-omics confirm the pseudobulk transcriptome–proteome discordance map? (wu-2026-single-cell-proteomic-landscape-of)
Do MPRA-nominated activating edits translate to whole plants in the field? (groover-2026-mapping-cis-regulatory-mutations-at)
Does the digital-twin framework generalize beyond one center and to other organs? (zhou-2026-digital-twins-of-ex)


Grounded strictly in the locally ingested sources. See each source page for citations.

wiki/topics/splicing.title: splicing
wiki/topics/splicing.content:
Splicing
Topic hub for pre-mRNA splicing and alternative splicing — the removal
of introns from precursor mRNA by the spliceosome and its regulation, which
together form a central layer of eukaryotic gene regulation. The wiki’s deepest
topic: this page curates the sources (grouped below), the concept map, and the
current synthesis.
Why it matters
Splicing converts intron-containing “mosaic” genes into mature mRNA via a
two-step transesterification reaction. alternative-splicing — the regulated
inclusion/exclusion of exons — lets one gene produce many isoforms, expanding
the proteome and underpinning organismal complexity, development, disease, and
therapy (see editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer).
Sources (44)
Foundational biology & reviews

sharp-1994-split-genes-and-rna-splicing (1994) — Sharp’s Nobel Lecture: discovery of split genes; spliceosome biochemistry
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative (2015) — spliceosome as ribozyme; mechanisms of AS regulation (foundational)
nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome (2010) — AS as the engine of proteome diversity; kinetic splice-site choice
wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human (2008) — landmark mRNA-seq survey; ~92–94% of genes AS; defines PSI (human)
pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing (2008) — first deep mRNA-seq AS survey; ~95% of multiexon genes AS (human)
wang-2009-rna-seq-a-revolutionary-tool (2009) — framing review of RNA-seq for transcriptomics
kalsotra-2011-functional-consequences-of-developmentally-regulated (2011) — developmentally regulated AS networks
fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing (2014) — combinatorial, position-dependent RBP control of AS
witten-2011-understanding-splicing-regulation-through-rna (2011) — RNA splicing maps from CLIP + splicing data
wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts (2008) — parts list of regulatory elements → integrated splicing code (ExonScan)
baralle-2017-alternative-splicing-as-a-regulator (2017) — AS in development and tissue identity
scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease (2015) — RNA mis-splicing in disease; ASO/small-molecule therapies
shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in (2024) — co-transcriptional gene regulation (eukaryotes & prokaryotes)
marasco-2022-the-physiology-of-alternative (2022) — physiology of AS across tissues, cancer, ASD
wright-2022-alternative-splicing-as-a-source (2022) — AS as an evolutionarily labile source of diversity
rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing (2022) — regulation + disease + first splicing therapies
choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of (2025) — post-transcriptional-splicing as a regulatory layer
editorial-2025-rna-splicing-a-central-layer (2025) — framing editorial
peynet-2026-emerging-role-of-g-quadruplexes (2026) — rna-structure-and-splicing (G-quadruplexes)

Methods & tools — quantification & differential splicing

mortazavi-2008-mapping-and-quantifying-mammalian (2008) — RPKM + ERANGE: first genome-scale RNA-seq
katz-2010-analysis-and-design-of-rna (2010) — MISO: Bayesian isoform quantification + design
trapnell-2010-transcript-assembly-and-quantification (2010) — Cufflinks: assembly + FPKM
wu-2011-splicetrap-a-method-to-quantify (2011) — SpliceTrap: Bayesian exon-inclusion
anders-2012-detecting-differential-usage-of-exons (2012) — DEXSeq: differential exon usage (NB GLM)
shen-2014-rmats-robust-and-flexible-detection (2014) — rmats: replicate-aware ΔPSI event caller
alamancos-2015-leveraging-transcript-quantification-for-fast (2015) — SUPPA: PSI from transcript TPMs, ultrafast
bray-2016-near-optimal-probabilistic-rna-seq-quantification (2016) — kallisto: pseudoalignment quantification
li-2018-annotation-free-quantification-of-rna (2018) — LeafCutter: annotation-free intron excision; sQTL-scale
lin-2019-psi-sigma-a-comprehensive-splicing-detection-method (2019) — PSI-Sigma: all-isoform PSI; long reads
zhang-2019-deep-learning-augmented-rna-seq-analysis (2019) — DARTS: deep-learning-augmented differential AS
vaquero-garcia-2023-rna-splicing-analysis-using-heterogeneous (2023) — MAJIQ v2: LSVs across large/heterogeneous data
wang-2024-rmats-turbo-an-efficient-and-flexible (2024) — rmats-turbo: rMATS scaled to ×10⁴ samples
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the (2024) — the splicing-code: PWMs → deep learning
sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying (2026) — the modern splicing-methods toolkit
conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices (2016) — RNA-seq analysis best-practices survey
stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years (2019) — RNA-seq methods at maturity (long-read, single-cell, spatial)
hertel-2014-spliceosomal-pre-mrna-splicing-methods (2014) — Methods-in-Mol-Biol protocols volume

Population genetics & sQTL

park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative (2018) — AS variation across human populations; splicing-qtl (human)
zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls (2020) — regional sQTL structure in human brain (human)
demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing (2020) — PAIRADISE: allele-specific AS (human)
mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem (2021) — TASS catalogue: tandem splice sites (human, GTEx)

RBP mechanism & maps

van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional (2020) — ENCODE RBP map: 356 human RBPs (human)
begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled (2020) — Rbfox intermediate-affinity motifs; concentration-gated AS

Proteome

lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of (2019) — JCAST: splice-junction proteogenomics (human)


Tags: source pages carry human_research and resource flags; population/RBP-map/proteome sources are typically human, and tool papers are resource:true.

Concept map

Machinery & chemistry — spliceosome · splicing · splice-sites
Regulation — splicing-regulatory-elements · splicing-factors · rna-binding-proteins · splicing-code · rna-structure-and-splicing
Timing & coupling — cotranscriptional-splicing · post-transcriptional-splicing · splicing-and-chromatin
Outcomes & event types — alternative-splicing · intron-retention · splicing-and-disease · splicing-therapeutics · splicing-and-evolution
Measurement & genetics — splicing-methods · percent-spliced-in · rna-seq · splicing-qtl

Key entities

Factors — sf3b1 · u2af1 · srsf1 · hnrnpa1 · ptbp1
Gene examples — dscam · smn2
Structures — g-quadruplex
Tools — splicing-code / prediction — spliceai · darts
Tools — event/differential AS — rmats · rmats-turbo · miso · dexseq · splicetrap · psi-sigma · leafcutter · majiq-v2 · suppa · pairadise · exonscan
Tools — transcript quantification — cufflinks · kallisto · erange
Resources / datasets — encode-rbp-map · tass-catalogue · jcast

Syntheses

cotranscriptional-vs-post-transcriptional-splicing
alternative-splicing-functionality-debate

Current synthesis (across the 44 sources)

The machine is settled; the regulation is the frontier. The spliceosome
is a ~4.8 MDa dynamic RNP of five snRNPs whose catalytic core is RNA — a
ribozyme related to group II introns (sharp-1994-split-genes-and-rna-splicing,
lee-2015-mechanisms-and-regulation-of-alternative); cryo-EM resolved most
states (rogalska-2022-regulation-of-pre-mrna-splicing). Open questions are
about control, not chemistry.
AS is nearly universal. Founding mRNA-seq surveys put AS at ~92–95% of
multiexon human genes (wang-2008-alternative-isoform-regulation-in-human,
pan-2008-deep-surveying-of-alternative-splicing); it expands the proteome
(nilsen-2010-expansion-of-the-eukaryotic-proteome) and is read out per-event
as PSI.
A position-dependent, combinatorial code. Weak metazoan splice-sites +
splicing-regulatory-elements read by splicing-factors/rna-binding-proteins
in a context-dependent way (fu-2014-context-dependent-control-of-alternative-splicing,
witten-2011-understanding-splicing-regulation-through-rna,
wang-2008-splicing-regulation-from-a-parts); RBP concentration itself gates
outcomes (begg-2020-concentration-dependent-splicing-is-enabled). Modeling
this splicing-code went from PWMs to deep learning (SpliceAI, darts,
capitanchik-2024-from-computational-models-of-the).
A mature measurement ecosystem. Splicing is quantified two ways —
isoform-based (cufflinks, kallisto, suppa) and event-based
(rmats/rmats-turbo, miso, dexseq, leafcutter, majiq-v2,
psi-sigma, splicetrap) — over a decade of rna-seq tooling
(sousa-luis-2026-tools-and-tactics-for-studying,
conesa-2016-survey-of-best-practices,
stark-2019-rna-sequencing-the-teenage-years). Tools disagree, so orthogonal
validation matters.
Genetics connects sequence to splicing to disease. Population RNA-seq maps
sQTLs and allele-specific splicing
(park-2018-the-expanding-landscape-of-alternative,
zhang-2020-regional-variation-of-splicing-qtls,
demirdjian-2020-detecting-allele-specific-alternative-splicing); RBP
binding/function atlases (van-nostrand-2020-a-large-scale-binding-and-functional)
and isoform/proteome catalogues
(mironov-2021-an-extended-catalogue-of-tandem,
lau-2019-splice-junction-based-mapping-of) ground the regulatory logic.
Timing is itself regulatory. Splicing is largely co-transcriptional, but a
large fraction is post-transcriptional
(choquet-2025-the-regulation-and-function-of,
shine-2024-co-transcriptional-gene-regulation-in), and intron-retention

Mechanism → medicine. Splicing-factor mutations drive cancer and genetic
disease; the first splicing therapies are approved (nusinersen, risdiplam,
eteplirsen) — see scotti-2015-rna-mis-splicing-in-disease,
splicing-therapeutics, splicing-and-disease.
